433	\section{Cohomological vanishing in genus zero}
434	
435	\begin{lemma}
436	
437	
438	\leanok
439	[Vanishing of \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
440	  \label{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
441	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero}
442	  
443	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the proof of Theorem~1.3 (the inductive
444	  % step ``Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point.''):
445	  % the short exact sequence $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to
446	  % k(P) \to 0\( and its tensor with \)\mathscr L(D + P)$ giving
447	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\)
448	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
449	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its
450	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\),
451	  % which we denote by \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\)
452	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
453	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
454	  % Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
455	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
456	  % (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
457	  % does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\))."
458	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
459	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
460	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
461	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
462	  \[
463	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 0,
464	  \]
465	  i.e.\ \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\).
466	\end{lemma}
467	
468	\begin{proof}
469	  
470	  Specialise Hartshorne's inductive step at \(D = 0\). The standard short
471	  exact sequence
472	  \[
473	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
474	  \]
475	  (Hartshorne~II.6.18: the ideal sheaf of the locally principal closed
476	  subscheme \(P\) is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P])\); the quotient is the structure
477	  sheaf of \(P\), which is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\)
478	  supported at \(P\) since \(P\) has residue field \(\bar k\) by closedness
479	  over the algebraically closed base \(\bar k\)). Tensoring with the
480	  locally free rank-\(1\) sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\)
481	  (REF) preserves exactness and leaves
482	  the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged, giving
483	  \[
484	    0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
485	  \]
486	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\). Apply the long exact sequence of sheaf
487	  cohomology (REF, the project's $\Module \bar
488	  k\(-flavoured cohomology pipeline). Using that \)\dim C = 1$ kills
489	  \(H^i\) for \(i \geq 2\) (Grothendieck vanishing on a \(1\)-dimensional
490	  scheme), the long exact sequence reads
491	  \begin{multline*}
492	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^0(C, k(P)) \\
493	    \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^1(C, k(P)) \to 0.
494	  \end{multline*}
495	  Two ingredients close the proof:
496	
497	  \emph{(a) \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\).} This is the genus-\(0\)
498	  hypothesis: by REF,
499	  \[
500	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C),
501	  \]
502	  so \(g(C) = 0\) is exactly \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\),
503	  i.e.\ \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
504	
505	  \emph{(b) \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).} The skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) is
506	  supported at a single closed point, hence is flasque: every section
507	  on an open \(U \subset C\) either restricts to \(0\) (if \(P \notin U\)) or
508	  takes values in the stalk \(k(P)_P \cong \bar k\) (if \(P \in U\)), and
509	  restriction maps between opens of either type are surjective. A
510	  flasque sheaf on any topological space has vanishing higher
511	  cohomology (Hartshorne~III.2.5); in particular
512	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\), and \emph{a fortiori}
513	  \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
514	
515	  Substituting (a) and (b) into the long exact sequence collapses the
516	  segment ending at \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) to
517	  \[
518	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to 0,
519	  \]
520	  whence \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\), as required.
521	
522	  \emph{Lean reference note on the long exact sequence.} Mathlib
523	  (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the long exact sequence of
524	  \(\Ext_{\mathcal O_C}^*(\mathbf 1, -)\) at the abelian-group level
525	  (\texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact}); the
526	  project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured cohomology pipeline
527	  (REF) inherits the long exact sequence by
528	  forget-functor naturality. The skyscraper-cohomology vanishing
529	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\) is closed by either the flasque
530	  argument (Hartshorne~III.2.5; Mathlib has the abelian-sheaf form
531	  via \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.Flasque}) or, more directly for the
532	  curve case, by the affine-cover Čech-cohomology argument on
533	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and a small affine open around \(P\) (the
534	  skyscraper has zero sections on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), so the Čech
535	  complex degenerates).
536	\end{proof}
537	
538	\section{The dimension formula in genus zero}
539	
540	\begin{theorem}
541	
542	
543	\leanok
544	[\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
545	  \label{thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
546	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
547	  
548	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the Riemann--Roch chain inside
549	  % Example~1.3.5 applied with \(D = [P]\): $\chi(\mathscr L([P]))
550	  % = \deg([P]) + 1 - g = 1 + 1 - 0 = 2$, combined with the vanishing
551	  % \(H^1(\mathscr L([P])) = 0\) (read from
552	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 312, 314).
553	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let \(D\) be a
554	  % divisor on a curve \(X\) of genus \(g\). Then}
555	  % \(l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g\)."
556	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
557	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
558	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
559	  % is the Euler characteristic
560	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
561	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295 (the
562	  \(\chi\)-identity inside the proof) and Example~1.3.5, p.~297 (the
563	  genus-\(0\) specialisation chain).}
564	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
565	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
566	  \[
567	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 2.
568	  \]
569	\end{theorem}
570	
571	\begin{proof}
572	  
573	  Specialise the Euler-characteristic identity
574	  REF of \texttt{RR.2} to
575	  the divisor \(D = [P]\) of REF. The
576	  degree of \([P]\) is \(\deg([P]) = 1\) (every closed point contributes
577	  \(1\) to the bare-sum degree of REF), and the
578	  genus hypothesis \(g(C) = 0\) (REF) gives
579	  \[
580	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; \deg([P]) + 1 - g(C)
581	    \;=\; 1 + 1 - 0 \;=\; 2.
582	  \]
583	  Unfolding \(\chi\) via REF as a two-term Euler
584	  characteristic on a curve, this rewrites as
585	  \[
586	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
587	    \,-\, \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
588	    \;=\; 2.
589	  \]
590	  By
591	  REF, the second
592	  term vanishes:
593	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\). Substituting yields
594	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), as required.
595	
596	  \emph{Alternative direct route via the long exact sequence.} The
597	  same long exact sequence used in the proof of
598	  REF computes
599	  the dimension of \(H^0\) in one stroke without invoking
600	  \texttt{RR.2}: from
601	  \[
602	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
603	    \to H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to \cdots
604	  \]
605	  together with \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1\)
606	  (Hartshorne~I.3.4: \(H^0\) of the structure sheaf on a proper
607	  geometrically irreducible variety over \(\bar k\) is \(\bar k\)),
608	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) (skyscraper at a \(\bar k\)-rational
609	  point), and \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) (genus-\(0\)), the connecting
610	  map \(H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) has zero codomain and the
611	  segment yields the short exact sequence
612	  \[
613	    0 \to \bar k \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to \bar k \to 0
614	  \]
615	  of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces, whence $\dim_{\bar k}
616	  H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 1 + 1 = 2$. This alternative route does
617	  not consume the Euler-characteristic identity of \texttt{RR.2} and
618	  could be substituted at the formalisation stage; the chapter uses
619	  \texttt{RR.2} as written above to keep the dependency graph in
620	  line with the iter-173 RR sub-build sequencing.
621	\end{proof}
622	
623	\section{Existence of a non-constant rational function}
624	
625	\begin{corollary}
626	
627	
628	\leanok
629	[A genus-\(0\) curve admits a non-constant rational function with a
630	single simple pole]
631	  \label{cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
632	  % Alias label consumed under the shorter name by
633	  % \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex} (RR.4):
634	  \label{cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero}
635	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
636	  
637	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Exercise~1.1 (the existence of a
638	  % nonconstant rational function regular everywhere except at one
639	  % point on a genus-\(0\) curve, as a direct consequence of the
640	  % Riemann--Roch dimension formula) (read from
641	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
642	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "1.1. Let \(X\) be a curve, and let \(P \in X\) be a
643	  % point. Then there exists a nonconstant rational function $f \in
644	  % K(X)\(, which is regular everywhere except at \)P$."
645	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Exercise~1.1, p.~297.}
646	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
647	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
648	  there exists a non-constant rational function \(f \in K(C)\) such that:
649	  \begin{itemize}
650	    \item \(f\) is regular on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), i.e.\ \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
651	      for every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\);
652	    \item \(f\) has at most a simple pole at \(P\), i.e.\
653	      \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\);
654	    \item \(f \notin \bar k\), equivalently
655	      \(\div(f) \neq 0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\).
656	  \end{itemize}
657	  In particular, the pair \((1, f)\) spans
658	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
659	\end{corollary}
660	
661	\begin{proof}
662	  
663	  By
664	  REF,
665	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\). The constant subspace
666	  \(\bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\), obtained from the
667	  structural inclusion $\bar k \cong H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
668	  \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ (the rational function
669	  \(f = 1\), with \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), under the
670	  identification of
671	  REF), is a one-dimensional
672	  \(\bar k\)-vector subspace. Since the ambient space has dimension \(2\),
673	  the quotient \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) / \bar k \cdot 1\) is a
674	  non-zero one-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector space; any lift to
675	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of a non-zero element of the quotient is
676	  an element of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k \cdot 1\).
677	
678	  Choose such a lift $s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k
679	  \cdot 1$. Under the identification of
680	  REF the section
681	  \(s\) corresponds to a rational function \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\)
682	  satisfying \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), equivalently \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
683	  for every \(Q \neq P\) and \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\). The choice
684	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) means \(f\) is not the image of a constant,
685	  so \(f \notin \bar k\) (every non-zero element of
686	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \cong \bar k\) corresponds, under the
687	  identification, to a constant element of \(K(C)\)). The three bullets
688	  of the statement are now immediate.
689	
690	  Spanning. Linear independence of \(1\) and \(f\) over \(\bar k\) in
691	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) is the same condition as
692	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) above; combined with the dimension count
693	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), the pair \((1, f)\) is a
694	  \(\bar k\)-basis of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\).
695	
696	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean signature
697	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists\_nonconstant\_genusZero}
698	  returns an element \(f\) of the project's function-field carrier
699	  \(K(C)\) together with proofs of regularity on \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and
700	  the simple-pole bound at \(P\), packaged in the same shape as
701	  REF's output. The
702	  non-constancy is recorded as a separate field; the spanning
703	  statement is downstream and is consumed by \texttt{RR.4} via
704	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}.
705	\end{proof}

707	\section{Mathlib-status flag: the H\(^1\)-vanishing input}
708	
709	The vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\) on a genus-\(0\) curve
710	(REF) is the
711	critical cohomological input of this chapter. Its closure depends on
712	two Mathlib-status facts, both of which the project already consumes
713	elsewhere:
714	
715	\begin{itemize}
716	  \item \textbf{Long exact sequence of \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured sheaf
717	    cohomology.} Mathlib (\texttt{b80f227}) ships
718	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact} for
719	    \(\Ext^*\)-long-exact-sequences at the abelian-group level. The
720	    project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured pipeline
721	    (REF, \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex})
722	    inherits it by forget-functor naturality. This bridge is already
723	    consumed by REF of
724	    \texttt{RR.2}, so closing it here introduces no new Mathlib
725	    obligation.
726	  \item \textbf{Vanishing of higher cohomology of a skyscraper sheaf}
727	    on a Noetherian scheme. Hartshorne~III.2.5 gives the flasque
728	    formulation; the project's affine-cover Čech route
729	    (REF and surrounding
730	    \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex} machinery) computes
731	    the same vanishing for the skyscraper at a closed point by direct
732	    Čech-degeneration. \texttt{RR.2}'s inductive step already invokes
733	    \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), which is the combined statement
734	    \(\dim H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) and \(\dim H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\),
735	    so the vanishing \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\) is already a consumer
736	    obligation of \texttt{RR.2}.
737	\end{itemize}
738	
739	Consequently the H\(^1\)-vanishing
740	REF introduces no
741	Mathlib obligation that is not already on \texttt{RR.2}'s critical
742	path. The genus-\(0\) specialisation should therefore close at the
743	formalisation stage without splitting this chapter or pushing the
744	vanishing back into \texttt{RR.2}.

757	\section{Out of scope}
758	
759	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.3}
760	and are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters or queued for
761	follow-up iterations:
762	
763	\begin{itemize}
764	  \item The construction of the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)
765	    associated to an arbitrary Weil divisor $D = \sum n_i [P_i] \in
766	    \mathrm{Div}(C)$, and the linear-equivalence isomorphism
767	    \(\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')\) when \(D \sim D'\), are
768	    \emph{not} performed in this chapter. The chapter constructs only
769	    the specialisation \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) at \(D = [P]\) a prime divisor,
770	    which is the only case consumed by the genus-\(0\) classification
771	    chain. The general \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is queued for the
772	    post-RR-bridge follow-up (where it pairs with the general-\(g\)
773	    Riemann--Roch formula and the canonical-divisor identification).
774	  \item The general-\(g\) statement
775	    $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = \deg([P]) + 1 -
776	    g(C) + \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$, while a direct
777	    consequence of
778	    REF of \texttt{RR.2},
779	    is not separately formalised here; the chapter restricts to the
780	    genus-\(0\) specialisation \(g = 0\) on which
781	    REF is
782	    available.
783	  \item \textbf{Serre duality} $H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong
784	    H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*$ and the associated
785	    canonical divisor \(K_C = c_1(\omega_C)\) are \emph{not} used at any
786	    step of this chapter and are not deliverables; the chapter routes
787	    cohomological vanishing through the long exact sequence at
788	    \(D = 0\) (REF)
789	    instead. Serre duality is queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up.
790	  \item The morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) produced by applying
791	    \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} to the basis \((1, f)\) of
792	    REF,
793	    and its identification as an isomorphism, are the content of
794	    \texttt{RR.4} (sibling chapter
795	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added). The present
796	    chapter supplies the basis \((1, f)\) and stops.
797	  \item The general-\(g\) vanishing
798	    \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) = 0\) for \(\deg \mathcal L > 2g - 2\) is
799	    \emph{not} part of this chapter and is not required for the
800	    genus-\(0\) critical path. It is a Serre-duality-flavoured statement
801	    queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up.
802	  \item The interpretation of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) as the space
803	    of sections of a complete linear system \(|[P]|\) in
804	    projective-space form (Hartshorne~II.7.7) is gestured at by
805	    REF but the
806	    projective-space structure on the underlying set is not separately
807	    formalised; the chapter consumes only the \(\bar k\)-vector space
808	    structure on \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\).
809	\end{itemize}

12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	This chapter is the fourth and final sub-build chapter
14	\texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4} closing the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
15	\[
16	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
17	  \,\colon\,
18	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
19	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
20	\]
21	The chain assembled across the four sub-build chapters is:
22	\begin{itemize}
23	  \item \texttt{RR.1} (\texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}): the Weil-divisor
24	    group \(\Div(C)\), the degree map
25	    \(\deg \colon \Div(C) \to \mathbb Z\) (\texttt{def:divisor_degree}), and the
26	    degree-zero principal-divisor identity
27	    (\texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero}).
28	  \item \texttt{RR.2} (\texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}): the Riemann--Roch
29	    formula in genus zero \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\)
30	    (\texttt{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}).
31	  \item \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter, dispatched parallel iter-174):
32	    the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C([P])\), its global-sections identification
33	    \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) = 2\), and the corollary
34	    \texttt{cor:nonconstant\_function\_genus\_zero} producing a non-constant
35	    rational function $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \setminus
36	    \bar k \cdot 1\( on a genus-\)0\( curve with a marked \)\bar k$-point.
37	  \item \texttt{RR.4} (\emph{this chapter}): the global classification ``a
38	    smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\)
39	    and a \(\bar k\)-rational point is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).''
40	\end{itemize}
41	This chapter consumes \texttt{RR.3} (the non-constant function) and produces
42	the Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} pinned at
43	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean} line~290 and restated as
44	the interface block \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} of
45	\texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex}. The \texttt{RR.4}-closure of that target
46	also drops the \texttt{sorryAx} propagation tagged on
47	\texttt{rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme} via
48	\texttt{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}.
49	\paragraph{Scope of the \(\bar k\)-base classification.} The chapter restricts
50	to the case where the base field \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed. Over $\bar
51	k\( every closed point is \)\bar k\(-rational, so the ``\)C\( has a \)\bar
52	k$-rational point'' hypothesis of Hartshorne's Exercise~IV.1.3 is automatic
53	(the smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) is non-empty,
54	hence has closed points, all of which are \(\bar k\)-rational). The general
55	``\(k \to \bar k\) descent'' step ``genus-\(0\) curve over a non-closed \(k\) with
56	a \(k\)-rational point \(\cong \mathbb P^1_k\)'' is a separate sub-build hosted
57	in \texttt{Jacobian.tex} (the \texttt{genusZeroWitness.key} downstream
58	consumer) and is out of scope here.
59	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
60	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
61	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). The Lean signature
62	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean}) threads the
63	project's standing curve typeclass package:
64	\begin{quote}
65	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
66	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
67	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
68	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]}
69	\end{quote}
70	matching the typeclass set used by the headline target
71	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} in
72	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290--296}. The genus
73	\(g(C)\) is the one defined in \texttt{def:genus} of \texttt{Genus.tex},
74	namely \(g(C) = \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) via the project's
75	\(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology
76	(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}); the concrete projective line target
77	\(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) is the project's
78	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveLineBar~kbar} of

80	\section{The morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) from two global sections}
81	\begin{lemma}
82	\leanok
83	[Morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) from a globally generating pair of sections]
84	  \label{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections}
85	  
86	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}
87	  Let \(X\) be a scheme over a field \(\bar k\), let \(\mathcal L\) be an invertible
88	  sheaf on \(X\), and let \(s_0, s_1 \in H^0(X, \mathcal L)\) be two global
89	  sections whose common zero locus is empty:
90	  \[
91	    \{x \in X : s_0(x) = 0 \text{ and } s_1(x) = 0\} \;=\; \emptyset.
92	  \]
93	  (Equivalently, \(s_0\) and \(s_1\) generate \(\mathcal L\) at every point.) Then
94	  there exists a unique morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
95	  \[
96	    \varphi_{(s_0, s_1)} \colon X \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
97	  \]
98	  whose pullback of \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(1)\) is \(\mathcal L\) and whose
99	  pullbacks of the standard coordinate sections $x_0, x_1 \in
100	  H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal O(1))\( are \)s_0\( and \)s_1$ respectively. On the
101	  basic-open chart $D_+(x_0) = \mathbb A^1_{\bar k} \subset
102	  \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\( the morphism \)\varphi_{(s_0, s_1)}\( is the map ``\)x
103	  \mapsto s_1(x) / s_0(x)\(''; on the chart \)D_+(x_1)\( it is the map ``\)x
104	  \mapsto s_0(x) / s_1(x)$''; the two definitions agree on the overlap
105	  \(D_+(x_0) \cap D_+(x_1) = \mathbb G_{m, \bar k}\) by the cocycle identity
106	  \((s_1 / s_0) \cdot (s_0 / s_1) = 1\).
107	\end{lemma}
108	\begin{proof}
109	  This is the standard linear-system construction in the form of
110	  Hartshorne's Theorem~II.7.1 (``Morphisms to \(\mathbb P^n\)''), specialised to
111	  \(n = 1\). Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the corresponding API
112	  under the name
113	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} in
114	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic}:
115	  \begin{quote}
116	  \small
117	  \texttt{def Proj.fromOfGlobalSections \{\(\sigma\) A\} [CommRing A]
118	    [SetLike \(\sigma\) A] [AddSubgroupClass \(\sigma\) A] (\(\mathcal A\) :
119	    \(\mathbb N\) → \(\sigma\)) [GradedRing \(\mathcal A\)] \{X : Scheme\}\\
120	    \phantom{}\quad(f : A →+* X.presheaf.obj \(\top\))\\
121	    \phantom{}\quad(hf : Ideal.map f (HomogeneousIdeal.irrelevant \(\mathcal A\)).toIdeal = \(\top\)) :\\
122	    \phantom{}\quad(X \(\longrightarrow\) Proj \(\mathcal A\))}
123	  \end{quote}
124	  Specialising to \(\mathcal A = \bar k[x_0, x_1]\) with the standard $\mathbb
125	  N\(-grading by total degree, the inputs are a graded \)\bar k$-algebra
126	  homomorphism \(f \colon \bar k[x_0, x_1] \to H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)\) and a
127	  proof that the irrelevant ideal \((x_0, x_1) \cdot \bar k[x_0, x_1]\) maps to
128	  the unit ideal of \(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)\) (i.e.\ no common zeros). The
129	  resulting morphism lands in $\mathrm{Proj}\,\bar k[x_0, x_1] = \mathbb
130	  P^1_{\bar k}\( (Hartshorne~II, Example~7.1.3, the construction of \)\mathbb
131	  P^n\( as a \)\mathrm{Proj}\(). When \)\mathcal L$ is non-trivial the present
132	  lemma's signature is reduced to the Mathlib API by working on the open
133	  cover \(X = D(s_0) \cup D(s_1)\), where on each chart the sections become
134	  honest global functions (\(s_1 / s_0\) on \(D(s_0)\), \(s_0 / s_1\) on \(D(s_1)\),
135	  agreeing on the overlap). The chart-glue is performed by the
136	  project-bespoke wrapper
137	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}, whose body
138	  in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean} chains
139	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} with the chart-restriction-and-glue
140	  pattern of \texttt{Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms}.
141	  Uniqueness follows from the standard separation argument for morphisms to
142	  a separated scheme: two morphisms \(\varphi, \psi \colon X \to \mathbb P^1\)
143	  with \(\varphi^* x_i = \psi^* x_i\) for \(i = 0, 1\) agree on the basic-open
144	  charts \(D_+(x_0), D_+(x_1)\) (where they pull back the affine coordinates to
145	  the same rational function), and these charts cover \(\mathbb P^1\), so the
146	  two morphisms agree globally by gluing.
147	\end{proof}
148	\section{Degree of a morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) via its pole divisor}
149	% NOTE (iter-182 plan-phase Pin 2 signature strengthening). The iter-177
150	% file-skeleton placeholder existential
151	%   "$\exists (d : \mathbb N) (D : C.\mathrm{WeilDivisor}),\
152	%      0 < d \wedge D.\mathrm{degree} = d$"
153	% was vacuous in \(\varphi\) (no constraint tying \(D\) to \(\varphi\) or the
154	% point \(\infty\)). The iter-182 plan-phase refactor (refactor slug
155	% `pin2-sig-strengthen`) replaces it with the substantive existential
156	% pinning \(D\) to \(\varphi^*[\infty]\) and the degree to the function-field
157	% extension degree \([K(C):\bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\). The new typed-sorry def
158	% `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` (in
159	% `RationalCurveIso.lean`, analogous to the iter-181 typed-sorry def
160	% `lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField` of OCofP.lean) carries the
161	% Weil divisor \(\varphi^*[\infty]\); its body is queued for iter-183+
162	% via the affine-chart `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` route.
163	\begin{lemma}
164	\leanok
165	[Degree of a non-constant morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) via the preimage of \(\infty\)]
166	  \label{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor}
167	  
168	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor}
169	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV \S2, opening paragraph of "Hurwitz's Theorem"
170	  % (definition of the degree of a finite morphism of curves) (read from
171	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
172	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
173	  % \(f: X \to Y\) of curves is defined to be the degree \([K(X):K(Y)]\) of the
174	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
175	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.2, p.~299, opening of \S2.}
176	  Let \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) be a non-constant morphism
177	  from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\). Then
178	  \(\varphi\) is finite, its function-field extension
179	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\) (the canonical
180	  \(\varphi\)-induced map, supplied as an
181	  \texttt{[Algebra (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).functionField C.functionField]}
182	  instance argument on the Lean side) is finite, and there exists a Weil
183	  divisor \(D \in \Div(C)\) such that
184	  \[
185	    D \;=\; \varphi^*[\infty]
186	    \quad\text{and}\quad
187	    \deg(D) \;=\; [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,].
188	  \]
189	  Equivalently, the morphism \(\varphi\) satisfies
190	  \[
191	    [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,] \;=\;
192	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
193	                  \ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [P]\,\right),
194	  \]
195	  where \(t_\infty \in K(\mathbb P^1)\) is a local parameter at \(\infty\)
196	  (equivalently \(t_\infty = 1/u\) for the standard affine coordinate \(u\) on
197	  \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)); and for any closed point
198	  \(Q \in \mathbb P^1\) the same identification gives
199	  \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C):\bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\).
200	  \emph{Convention on the pole divisor.} The Weil divisor
201	  \(\varphi^*[\infty]\) is exposed in Lean as
202	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor \(\varphi\)}, a
203	  noncomputable definition introduced (as a typed \texttt{sorry}) at the
204	  iter-182 plan-phase signature strengthening of this lemma. The body of
205	  \texttt{poleDivisor} is queued for iter-183+ closure via the standard
206	  affine-chart construction (\texttt{Ideal.sum\_ramification\_inertia}
207	  in Mathlib, applied at each closed point \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\)
208	  to read off the local ramification multiplicity
209	  \(\ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty)\) from the maximal ideal at \(\infty\) in
210	  \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)). On the Lean side, the existential output of
211	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} packages
212	  \(D = \texttt{Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor}\;\varphi\) together with
213	  \(\deg(D) = \operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(\mathbb P^1)} K(C)\),
214	  mirroring the OCofP pattern in which a typed-sorry def
215	  (\texttt{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}) is introduced
216	  upstream of its consumer lemma.
217	\end{lemma}
218	% NOTE (iter-189 review): Lane I iter-189 prover identified a structural
219	% conflict between the iter-187 body of `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` (currently
220	% `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal (algebraMap _ _ t.val) halg`, i.e. a
221	% principal divisor of degree 0) and the RHS `Module.finrank K(P^1) K(C) > 0`.
222	% As stated, `Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank` (the Lean theorem pinned
223	% to this lemma) is mathematically false. Iter-190+ plan-phase must pick a
224	% corrective: (a) refactor `Hom.poleDivisor` to be the positive part
225	% `(div(\varphi^* t_\infty))_0 = \varphi^*[\infty]` (new project-bespoke
226	% `Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart` infrastructure), or (b) rename the
227	% theorem to operate on `positivePart (Hom.poleDivisor \varphi)`. See
228	% `.archon/task_results/AlgebraicJacobian_RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso.lean.md`
229	% (iter-189) for the full diagnosis.
230	\begin{proof}
231	  A non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves over \(\bar k\) is
232	  automatically finite. Indeed, the image of \(\varphi\) is a closed
233	  irreducible subset of \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) that is not a point (else
234	  \(\varphi\) would be constant) and not empty, so it is all of $\mathbb
235	  P^1_{\bar k}\( (the only proper irreducible closed subset of \)\mathbb
236	  P^1_{\bar k}\( is a point); hence \)\varphi$ is surjective. A surjective
237	  morphism between smooth proper irreducible curves of equal dimension is
238	  finite (proper + quasi-finite, where quasi-finiteness comes from the
239	  fact that \(\varphi\) has zero-dimensional fibres: a positive-dimensional
240	  fibre would force a one-dimensional irreducible component in the fibre,
241	  contradicting irreducibility of \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) and \(C\) at
242	  generic points). Hence the function-field extension
243	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) = \varphi^\# K(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\)
244	  is finite.
245	  The identification \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\) for
246	  any closed point \(Q \in \mathbb P^1\) is the multiplicativity of the
247	  degree under finite pullback (Hartshorne~II.6.9): for a finite morphism
248	  \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) of smooth proper curves and any divisor $D \in
249	  \Div(C')\(, \)\deg(\varphi^* D) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg(D)$. Specialising
250	  to \(D = [Q]\) (a divisor of degree \(1\) since \(\kappa(Q) = \bar k\)) gives
251	  \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = \deg(\varphi)\). Equivalently, the degree of
252	  \(\varphi\) equals the number of preimages of any closed point of $\mathbb
253	  P^1\(, counted with the ramification multiplicities \)\ord_P(\varphi^*
254	  t_\infty)\( at the preimage points \)P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)$.
255	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The lemma threads two auxiliary inputs:
256	  ``non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves is finite'' (the
257	  surjective-and-proper-and-quasi-finite argument above; Hartshorne~II.6.8)
258	  and ``multiplicativity of degree under finite pullback'' (Hartshorne~II.6.9
259	  and the multiplicativity of \(\deg\) under composition of \(\div\) and
260	  \(\deg\); the closely related ``\(\mathrm{div}(f)\) has degree zero'' specialisation
261	  is \texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero} of \texttt{RR.1}). Both are separable
262	  sub-lemmas inside \texttt{RR.4}'s Lean file
263	  (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean}) and are
264	  slotted for closure in a follow-up iteration after the
265	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} skeleton lands.
266	\end{proof}
267	\section{A degree-\(1\) morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism}
268	% NOTE (iter-181 Lane I signature refinement; see analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md
269	% Decision 2, verdict DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY). The iter-177 file-skeleton
270	% placeholder hypothesis "Nonempty (C'.functionField ≃+* C.functionField)"
271	% (an abstract function-field iso, unrelated to \(\varphi\)) was strictly
272	% weaker than the birational-extension body needs and was refined in
273	% iter-181 to the explicit function-field-extension-degree formulation
274	% [K(C):K(C')] = 1, i.e. Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField
275	% = 1, with C.functionField a C'.functionField-algebra via the canonical
276	% \varphi-induced map (Scheme.Hom.stalkMap at the generic point composed
277	% with IsFractionRing.lift). The proof-prose body below (the Hartshorne
278	% I.6.12 chain + scheme-theoretic alternative argument) is unchanged ---
279	% only the input shape changes.
280	\begin{lemma}
281	\leanok
282	[Degree-\(1\) morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism]
283	  \label{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso}
284	  
285	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso_of_degree_one}
286	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], I \S6 Corollary~6.12 (function-field-to-curve
287	  % equivalence of categories) (read from
288	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 62, doc p.~45)
289	  % + [Hartshorne], IV \S2 opening paragraph (degree of a finite morphism
290	  % of curves is the function-field extension degree) (read from
291	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
292	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.12. \emph{The following three categories are
293	  % equivalent:}
294	  % (i) \emph{nonsingular projective curves, and dominant morphisms;}
295	  % (ii) \emph{quasi-projective curves, and dominant rational maps;}
296	  % (iii) \emph{function fields of dimension 1 over \(k\), and \(k\)-homomorphisms.}"
297	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
298	  % \(f: X \to Y\) of curves is defined to be the degree \([K(X):K(Y)]\) of the
299	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
300	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, I.6 Corollary~6.12 (p.~45) + IV.2 opening
301	  (p.~299).}
302	  Let \(C, C'\) be smooth proper geometrically irreducible curves over an
303	  algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), and let \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) be
304	  a non-constant morphism whose induced function-field extension is of
305	  degree one,
306	  \[
307	    [\,K(C) : K(C')\,] \;=\; 1,
308	  \]
309	  formally
310	  \(\operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(C')} K(C) = 1\) in the Lean signature.
311	  Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes.
312	  \emph{Convention on the function-field map.} At each call-site of this
313	  lemma, the \(K(C')\)-algebra structure on \(K(C)\) used to interpret the
314	  degree \([K(C) : K(C')]\) is the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced
315	  function-field map, i.e.\ the composite
316	  \[
317	    K(C') \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{germ}\;}\;
318	    \mathcal O_{C', \varphi(\xi_C)}
319	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\varphi^\#\;}\;
320	    \mathcal O_{C, \xi_C}
321	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{frac.}\,\text{lift}\;}\; K(C)
322	  \]
323	  of the germ at the image of the generic point \(\xi_C \in C\) with the
324	  stalk-map of \(\varphi\) at \(\xi_C\) and the fraction-field lift at
325	  \(\xi_C\) (per \texttt{analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md}, Decision~2). On
326	  the Lean side this canonical instance is carried as a
327	  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
328	  to \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one}; consumers
329	  supply it explicitly at each call-site.
330	\end{lemma}
331	% NOTE (iter-189 review): Pin 3 Step 1 (function-field iso K(C') ≃+* K(C)
332	% from `Module.finrank = 1`) closed axiom-clean inline iter-189 (~10 LOC via
333	% `Subalgebra.bot_eq_top_of_finrank_eq_one` + `Algebra.surjective_algebraMap_iff`
334	% + `RingHom.injective` + `RingEquiv.ofBijective`). Step 2 (scheme-level lift
335	% via `Scheme.Hom.toNormalization`) remains a typed sorry in the body, with 4
336	% sub-obligations (a)-(d) documented inline at the sorry. Iter-190+ closure
337	% budget ~80-150 LOC per `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md`.
338	\begin{proof}
339	  The function-field hypothesis \([K(C) : K(C')] = 1\) states that the
340	  canonical \(\varphi\)-induced map \(\varphi^\# \colon K(C') \to K(C)\) is a
341	  finite extension of degree one; since \(K(C')\) is a field and the
342	  extension has dimension one as a \(K(C')\)-vector space, \(\varphi^\#\) is
343	  an isomorphism of function fields \(K(C') \cong K(C)\). (Equivalently, by
344	  the IV.2-opening identification \(\deg(\varphi) = [K(C):K(C')]\), this is
345	  the statement \(\deg(\varphi) = 1\); the morphism \(\varphi\) is finite in
346	  this case by the standard argument that a non-constant morphism of
347	  smooth proper curves is finite, identical to the
348	  \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} preamble.)
349	  By Hartshorne's Corollary~I.6.12, the assignment \(C \mapsto \bar k(C)\) is
350	  an equivalence of categories between smooth projective curves over $\bar
351	  k$ (with dominant morphisms) and function fields of transcendence degree
352	  one over \(\bar k\) (with \(\bar k\)-homomorphisms). Under this equivalence,
353	  the isomorphism \(\bar k(C') \cong \bar k(C)\) produced by the previous
354	  paragraph lifts to a unique isomorphism of smooth proper curves $C \to
355	  C'\(, and this isomorphism agrees with \)\varphi$ on the generic point (by
356	  construction: it is the inverse-image of \(\varphi^\#\) under the
357	  equivalence). The two morphisms \(C \to C'\) that agree on the generic
358	  point of the integral scheme \(C\) agree everywhere (a morphism out of an
359	  integral scheme to a separated scheme is determined by its restriction
360	  to the generic point, Hartshorne~II Ex.~4.2). Hence \(\varphi\) is itself
361	  the lift, and is therefore an isomorphism.
362	  \emph{Alternative direct argument.} Without invoking the
363	  equivalence-of-categories framing, the same conclusion is obtained
364	  scheme-theoretically: a finite degree-\(1\) morphism $\varphi \colon C \to
365	  C'$ between integral normal noetherian schemes is an isomorphism.
366	  Indeed, the direct image \(\varphi_* \mathcal O_C\) is a coherent
367	  \(\mathcal O_{C'}\)-module that is generically of rank $\deg(\varphi) =
368	  1\(, and an inclusion \)\mathcal O_{C'} \hookrightarrow \varphi_*
369	  \mathcal O_C\( of coherent rank-\)1$ sheaves whose generic ranks agree
370	  is an isomorphism over the locus where the quotient
371	  \(\varphi_* \mathcal O_C / \mathcal O_{C'}\) has trivial stalk. The
372	  quotient is a torsion coherent \(\mathcal O_{C'}\)-module of generic rank
373	  \(\deg(\varphi) - 1 = 0\), hence is itself torsion-free (a
374	  finitely-generated torsion module over a Dedekind domain of generic
375	  rank zero is zero), so vanishes. Therefore $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C =
376	  \mathcal O_{C'}\( and \)\varphi$ is the structure morphism of an
377	  isomorphism. Cf.\ Stacks tag~\textbf{0AVX} for the categorical version.
378	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The Lean signature
379	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one} consumes a
380	  non-constant morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) together with a
381	  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
382	  (the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced function-field map) and the
383	  function-field-extension-degree-one hypothesis
384	  \texttt{Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField = 1}, and
385	  produces an isomorphism \(C \cong C'\) in the slice category
386	  \(\mathtt{Over (Spec (.of\ kbar))}\). The proof body threads the Mathlib
387	  API \texttt{IsIntegralHom.iso\_of\_isUnit} (or the equivalent
388	  \texttt{IsAffineHom + Finite + RankOne} chain) and, where needed, the
389	  project-bespoke ``finite-morphism-of-degree-one is an isomorphism''
390	  helper. The Hartshorne~I.6.12 equivalence-of-categories framing is the
391	  intended high-level proof structure; the scheme-theoretic argument
392	  above is the spelled-out form for the Lean body.
393	\end{proof}
394	\section{Headline: genus-\(0\) curve over \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)}
395	\begin{theorem}
396	
397	\leanok
398	[Genus-\(0\) curve over \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)]
399	  \label{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}
400	  
401	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
402	  % already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely
403	  % suppose given a curve \(X\) of genus 0. Let \(P, Q\) be two distinct
404	  % points of \(X\) and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor \(D = P - Q\).
405	  % Since \(\deg(K - D) = -2\), using (1.3.3) above, we have \(l(K - D) = 0\),
406	  % and so we find \(l(D) = 1\). But \(D\) is a divisor of degree 0, so by
407	  % (1.2) we have \(D \sim 0\), in other words \(P \sim Q\). But this implies
408	  % \(X\) is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
409	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
410	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field, and let \(C\) be a smooth
411	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\).
412	  Then there exists an isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
413	  \[
414	    \varphi \colon C \stackrel{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}.
415	  \]
416	  Concretely, the Lean signature
417	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} (the existing pinned
418	  target at \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290}) is
419	  closed by the proof below; the existing interface block
420	  \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} of
421	  \texttt{chap:avr_for_rr} is its consumer-side restatement,
422	  pinning the same Lean declaration.
423	\end{theorem}
424	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the
425	% statement block above (Hartshorne states the classification and its
426	% proof inside the single Example; the proof is the second half beginning
427	% "We already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) \ldots"). The present
428	% proof body restates Hartshorne's argument in the project's notation
429	% (linear-system construction \(C \to \mathbb P^1\) via $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C
430	% ([P]))\(, degree-\)1$ identification, isomorphism).
431	\begin{proof}
432	  Over the algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) the smooth proper
433	  geometrically irreducible curve \(C\) has closed points, all of which are
434	  \(\bar k\)-rational (the residue field of a closed point of a curve over
435	  \(\bar k\) is a finite extension of \(\bar k\), hence is \(\bar k\) itself by
436	  algebraic closedness). Fix one such \(\bar k\)-rational point \(P \in C\);
437	  this is the marked point used to build the linear system below.
438	  \emph{Step 1: non-constant function \(f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\).}
439	  By Hartshorne's Riemann--Roch in genus zero
440	  (\texttt{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} of \texttt{RR.2}) applied to the
441	  effective divisor \([P]\) of degree \(1\), we obtain
442	  \(\ell([P]) = \deg([P]) + 1 = 2\). The constant section
443	  \(1 \in \bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) accounts for a
444	  one-dimensional \(\bar k\)-subspace; the two-dimensional quotient supplies
445	  a non-constant section $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \setminus
446	  \bar k \cdot 1$. This is the corollary
447	  \texttt{cor:nonconstant\_function\_genus\_zero} of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
448	  chapter, \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OCofP.tex}).
449	  \emph{Step 2: morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\) via the linear system \((1, f)\).}
450	  The pair \((1, f) \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))^2\) generates the
451	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) at every point of \(C\): at points
452	  \(Q \in C \setminus \{P\}\) the section \(1\) does not vanish in
453	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])_Q \cong \mathcal O_{C, Q}\), and at the marked point
454	  \(P\) the section \(f\) does not vanish in \(\mathcal O_C([P])_P\) (because \(f\)
455	  has a pole of order exactly \(1\) at \(P\) in the rational-function
456	  picture: \(f \in K(C) \setminus \bar k\) with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = [P]\)).
457	  Therefore by \texttt{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections} there
458	  exists a unique morphism
459	  \[
460	    \varphi \;:=\; \varphi_{(1, f)} \colon C \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
461	  \]
462	  with \(\varphi^* x_0 = 1\) and \(\varphi^* x_1 = f\) (so the pullback of
463	  the standard ratio coordinate \(u = x_1 / x_0\) on $\mathbb P^1 \setminus
464	  \{\infty\}\( is \)f$, and the pullback of the inverse coordinate
465	  \(t_\infty = x_0 / x_1 = 1 / f\) is the local parameter \(1/f\) at \(P\)).
466	  \emph{Step 3: \(\deg(\varphi) = 1\) via the pole divisor of \(f\).} The
467	  morphism \(\varphi\) is non-constant (since \(f\) is non-constant as a
468	  rational function on \(C\), the pullback \(\varphi^* u = f\) takes
469	  infinitely many values, ruling out a constant \(\varphi\)). By
470	  \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} applied to \(\varphi\),
471	  \[
472	    \deg(\varphi)
473	    \;=\;
474	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{Q \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
475	                  \ord_Q(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [Q]\,\right).
476	  \]
477	  We compute the pole divisor of \(f\) as a rational function on \(C\):
478	  since \(f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) with \(f\) non-constant and
479	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) the line bundle of the effective divisor \([P]\), the
480	  rational-function \(f\) has pole divisor \((f)_\infty = [P]\) (the
481	  unique pole of order \(1\) at \(P\), no other poles; this is the standard
482	  identification of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))\) with the rational functions
483	  \(g \in K(C)\) satisfying \(\mathrm{div}(g) + D \geq 0\), specialised to
484	  \(D = [P]\)). Hence \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty) = \{P\}\) with multiplicity~\(1\)
485	  and
486	  \[
487	    \deg(\varphi) \;=\; \deg([P]) \;=\; 1.
488	  \]
489	  \emph{Step 4: \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.} By Hartshorne's IV.2
490	  identification \(\deg(\varphi) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\), the
491	  degree-one conclusion of Step~3 rewrites as
492	  \(\operatorname{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C) = 1\) (with
493	  the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced
494	  \texttt{[Algebra \,(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).functionField\,
495	   C.functionField]} instance). By
496	  \texttt{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso} applied to the non-constant
497	  morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) (smooth proper
498	  geometrically irreducible curves over \(\bar k\) on both sides), under
499	  this function-field-extension-degree-one hypothesis, \(\varphi\) is an
500	  isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes.
501	  This produces the required isomorphism \(C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)
502	  closing the headline target
503	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}.
504	  \emph{Comparison with Hartshorne's original argument.} Hartshorne's
505	  Example~IV.1.3.5 phrases the argument in terms of two distinct points
506	  \(P, Q \in C\) and the degree-zero divisor \(D = P - Q\), deriving \(P \sim Q\)
507	  and concluding via II.6.10.1 that \(C\) is rational; combined with I.6.12
508	  this yields \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\). The present proof restates the same
509	  combinatorial content via the more directly formalisable linear-system
510	  construction: instead of going through the \(P \sim Q\) statement and
511	  invoking II.6.10.1 as a black box, we produce the explicit morphism
512	  \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1\) from the two sections \((1, f)\) of
513	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) given by Riemann--Roch and identify its degree
514	  directly. The two arguments are mathematically equivalent and have the
515	  same dependency footprint (Riemann--Roch in genus zero \(\Rightarrow\)
516	  \(\ell([P]) = 2\) \(\Rightarrow\) non-constant \(f\) \(\Rightarrow\) degree-\(1\)
517	  map to \(\mathbb P^1\) \(\Rightarrow\) iso); the linear-system phrasing maps
518	  more cleanly onto the Mathlib \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} API and
519	  the project's existing Weil-divisor/degree pipeline of \texttt{RR.1}.
520	\end{proof}
521	\begin{remark}
522	[Drop of \texttt{sorryAx} on the genus-\(0\) Albanese keystone]
523	  \label{rmk:rr4_unblocks_sorryAx_on_rigidity_genus0}

535	\section{Out of scope}
536	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.4} and
537	are sequenced elsewhere:
538	\begin{itemize}
539	  \item The general ``smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C\) of
540	    genus \(0\) over a \emph{non-closed} field \(k\) with a \(k\)-rational point
541	    \(\cong \mathbb P^1_k\)'' statement is the \(\bar k \to k\) descent of
542	    \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso}. The descent is hosted in
543	    \texttt{Jacobian.tex} via \texttt{genusZeroWitness.key}; it consumes
544	    \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} together with Galois descent for
545	    smooth proper curves and is out of scope for \texttt{RR.4}.
546	  \item The Mathlib API \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}
547	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic}) is
548	    consumed as a black box by
549	    \texttt{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections}; its own proof inside
550	    Mathlib (the universal property of \(\mathrm{Proj}\), graded-ring
551	    presentation of \(\mathbb P^n\)) is not re-derived here.
552	  \item Multiplicativity of degree under finite pullback (Hartshorne~II.6.9)
553	    is consumed as a black box by \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor};
554	    its proof is a separable sub-lemma threaded inside
555	    \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean} and is
556	    \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.4}'s blueprint statement (it lives at
557	    the same logical level as the closely related
558	    \texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero} of \texttt{RR.1}).
559	  \item The full equivalence of categories ``smooth projective curves over
560	    \(\bar k\) with dominant morphisms'' \(\cong\) ``function fields of
561	    transcendence degree~\(1\) over \(\bar k\) with \(\bar k\)-homomorphisms''
562	    (Hartshorne~I.6.12) is consumed as a high-level framing in
563	    \texttt{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso} but is \emph{not} re-formalised at
564	    the equivalence-of-categories level here. The proof body in the Lean
565	    file threads the spelled-out scheme-theoretic ``finite-degree-one
566	    morphism between integral normal schemes is an isomorphism''
567	    argument (Stacks~\textbf{0AVX}), which is the minimal-Mathlib-dependence
568	    pin compatible with the chapter's downstream needs.
569	  \item ``A smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\)
570	    has at least two distinct \(\bar k\)-points'' (the non-empty / infinite
571	    cardinality of \(C(\bar k)\)) is consumed implicitly by the present
572	    chapter but its formal statement lives in the project's curve API
573	    (\texttt{Genus.tex} / \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.Genus}); the chapter
574	    does not restate it.
575	  \item Cohomology of the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) on a curve
576	    (Hartshorne~III.5 + IV.1) is fully consumed via \texttt{RR.3}; in
577	    particular the \(H^1\)-vanishing of \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) on a genus-\(0\)
578	    curve is part of \texttt{RR.3}'s payload feeding
579	    \texttt{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} of \texttt{RR.2}, and is not
580	    re-derived here.
581	\end{itemize}

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.2.b sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
8	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) -- a projective \(S\)-scheme representing the
9	% functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) with
10	% Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\) on every fiber. The Quot construction is the
11	% \emph{existence engine} behind the relative Picard functor: A.2.c
12	% (\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) wires this chapter together with
13	% the relative Picard functor (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}, A.1.c)
14	% through the Abel map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\), which factors
15	% through the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\) -- the
16	% case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor. This chapter is gated on
17	% A.2.a (\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}, written this iter) and
18	% A.1.a (\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}, on disk).
19	%
20	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The construction embeds Quot as a
21	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
22	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
23	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
24	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
25	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
26	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
27	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability; it is a
28	% candidate for promotion to its own chapter
29	% \texttt{Picard\_GrassmannianScheme.tex} (writer iter-175+) -- see "Notes
30	% for Plan Agent" in the writer report.
31	
32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:quot_setup}
34	
35	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
36	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
37	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
38	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
39	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
40	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
41	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
42	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
43	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness; see~\)
44	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
45	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
46	
47	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1 and
48	% \S 5; FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
49	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500 +
50	% L2154-L2542). The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually

57	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
58	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
59	
60	\begin{definition}\leanok
61	[Hilbert polynomial]
62	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
63	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
64	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
65	  % Polynomials" (read from
66	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
67	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
68	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
69	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
70	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
71	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
72	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
73	  %  the function
74	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
75	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
76	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
77	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
78	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
79	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
80	  %
81	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
82	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
83	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
84	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
85	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
86	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
87	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
88	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
89	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
90	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
91	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
92	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
93	  III.5.2.}
94	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
95	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
96	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), the
97	  \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\) relative to \(L\)} is the
98	  polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) characterised by
99	  \[
100	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
101	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}|_{X_s} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
102	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}|_{X_s} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
103	  \]
104	  (a polynomial in \(m\) by Snapper's Lemma; the sum is finite by coherence and
105	  properness). When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
106	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
107	  well-defined on connected components.
108	\end{definition}

110	\section{The Quot functor}
111	\label{sec:quot_functor}
112	
113	\begin{definition}\leanok
114	[Quot functor]
115	  \label{def:quot_functor}
116	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor}
117	  
118	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) and
119	  % \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\)" (read from
120	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L394-L433
121	  % + L481-L498).
122	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
123	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(X\to S\) be a finite type
124	  %  scheme over it. Let \(E\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\).
125	  %  Let \(Sch_S\) denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
126	  %  over \(S\). For any \(T\to S\) in \(Sch_S\), a \textbf{family of quotients of \(E\)
127	  %  parametrised by \(T\)} will mean
128	  %  a pair \((\F,q)\) consisting of
129	  %
130	  %  (i) a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on \(X_T = X\times_ST\)
131	  %  such that the schematic support of \(\F\) is proper over \(T\)
132	  %  and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\), together with
133	  %
134	  %  (ii) a surjective \({\mathcal O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism of sheaves
135	  %  \(q:E_T  \to \F\) where \(E_T\) is the pull-back of \(E\) under
136	  %  the projection \(X_T \to X\).
137	  %
138	  %  Two such families \((\F,q)\) and \((\F,q)\) parametrised by \(T\) will
139	  %  be regarded as equivalent if \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\)),
140	  %  and \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) will denote
141	  %  an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
142	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,
143	  %  the pull-back of \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) under an \(S\)-morphism \(T'\to T\) is
144	  %  well-defined, which gives
145	  %  a set-valued contravariant functor
146	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets\) under which
147	  %  \(\)T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}\(\)
148	  %  [...]
149	  %  This shows that the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\) naturally decomposes
150	  %  as a co-product
151	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\(\)
152	  %  where for any polynomial \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\), the functor
153	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) associates
154	  %  to any \(T\) the set of all equivalence classes of
155	  %  families \(\langle \F,q \rangle\) such that at each \(t\in T\) the
156	  %  Hilbert polynomial of the restriction \(\F_t\), calculated
157	  %  using the pull-back of \(L\), is \({\Phi}\)."
158	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
159	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
160	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
161	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
162	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
163	  \[
164	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
165	  \]
166	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
167	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
168	  \begin{enumerate}
169	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
170	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) and which is flat over \(T\);
171	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
172	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
173	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
174	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
175	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
176	      \(\Phi\).
177	  \end{enumerate}
178	  Two pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) and \((\mathcal{F}', q')\) are equivalent iff
179	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
180	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
181	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
182	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
183	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).
184	\end{definition}
