186	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
187	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}
188	
189	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
190	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
191	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
192	linear algebra), we package the construction here.
193	
194	\begin{definition}\leanok
195	[Grassmannian scheme]
196	  \label{def:grassmannian_scheme}
197	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian}
198	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises~(2)" and
199	  % "Construction of Grassmannian" (read from
200	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L599
201	  % + L773-L850).
202	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
203	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), an explicit construction
204	  %  the Grassmannian scheme
205	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
206	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
207	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
208	  %  has been given at the end of this section. [...]
209	  %  Show that \(\grass(r,d)\) together with the quotient
210	  %  \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) represents the
211	  %  contravariant functor
212	  %  \(\)\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}\(\)
213	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any \(T\) the
214	  %  set of all equivalence classes
215	  %  \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients \(q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F\)
216	  %  where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\).
217	  %  [...]
218	  %  Using the above show that, more generally, if \(S\) is a scheme and
219	  %  \(E\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\),
220	  %  the functor \(\Grass(E,d)=\Quot_{E/S/S}^{d,{\mathcal O}_S}\)
221	  %  on all \(S\)-schemes which
222	  %  by definition associates to any \(T\)
223	  %  the set of all equivalence classes \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients
224	  %  \(q: E_T \to \F\) where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\),
225	  %  is representable. The representing scheme
226	  %  is denoted by \(\grass(E,d)\) and is called the rank \(d\)
227	  %  relative Grassmannian of \(E\) over \(S\)."
228	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) and "Construction of
229	  Grassmannian" (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
230	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(V\) be a locally free
231	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the
232	  \emph{Grassmannian of rank-\(d\) quotients of \(V\)} is the functor
233	  \[
234	    \mathrm{Grass}(V, d) : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set},
235	    \qquad
236	    T \longmapsto \{ \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle :
237	    q : V_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F},\; \mathcal{F}
238	    \text{ locally free of rank } d \}
239	  \]
240	  with equivalence \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Concretely $\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) =
241	  \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\( (the Quot functor for \)X = S\(, \)E = V$,
242	  constant Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\)).
243	\end{definition}
244	
245	\begin{theorem}\leanok
246	[Representability of the Grassmannian]
247	  \label{thm:grassmannian_representable}
248	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian.representable}
249	  
250	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
251	  % affine patches" through "Projective embedding"
252	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
253	  % L798-L930).
254	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
255	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), the Grassmannian scheme
256	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
257	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
258	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
259	  %  can be explicitly constructed as follows. [...]
260	  %  the schemes
261	  %  \(U^I\), as \(I\) varies over all the
262	  %  \({r\choose d}\) different
263	  %  subsets of  \(\{ 1,\ldots, r\}\) of cardinality \(d\),
264	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle \((\theta_{I,J})\) to form a
265	  %  finite-type scheme \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\). As each \(U^I\) is
266	  %  isomorphic to \(\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}\), it follows that
267	  %  \(\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\) is smooth of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\).
268	  %  [...]
269	  %  We now show that \(\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\) is proper. It is enough to
270	  %  verify the valuative criterion of properness for discrete valuation rings.
271	  %  [...]
272	  %  As \(\U\) is given by the transition functions \(g_{I,J}\) described above,
273	  %  the determinant line bundle
274	  %  \(\det(\U)\) is given by the transition functions
275	  %  \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J \in GL_1(U^I_J)\).
276	  %  [...]
277	  %  We leave it to the reader to verify that the sections \(\sigma_I\) form
278	  %  a linear system which is base point free and separates points
279	  %  relative to \(\spec \Z\), and so gives an embedding of
280	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) into \(\PP^m_{\Z}\) where \(m = {r\choose d}-1\).
281	  %  This is a closed embedding by the properness of
282	  %  \(\pi:\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\). In particular, \(\det(\U)\)
283	  %  is a relatively very ample line bundle on \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\)."
284	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Construction of Grassmannian" (FGA
285	  Explained Ch.~5).}
286	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(V\) a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module
287	  of rank \(r\), and \(1 \le d \le r\). The functor \(\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)\) of
288	  \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} is representable by a smooth projective
289	  \(S\)-scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \to S\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\),
290	  equipped with a tautological quotient
291	  \(\pi^* V \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). The determinant
292	  line bundle \(\det(\mathcal{U})\) is relatively very ample, and the
293	  associated linear system gives a closed embedding
294	  $\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \hookrightarrow
295	  \mathbb{P}_S\!\left(\bigwedge^d V\right)$ -- the \emph{Pl\"ucker embedding}.
296	\end{theorem}
297	
298	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the "Construction of Grassmannian" sub-section of
299	% [Nitsure], \S 1 (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
300	% L773-L930), which gives the construction in five named steps
301	% ("Construction by gluing together affine patches", "Separatedness",
302	% "Properness", "Universal quotient", "Projective embedding"). The total
303	% verbatim text is ~150 lines; it is reproduced piecewise in the
304	% SOURCE QUOTE comment of the theorem statement above.
305	\begin{proof}
306	  
307	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
308	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
309	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
310	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
311	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
312	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
313	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
314	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
315	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
316	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
317	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
318	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
319	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
320	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
321	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
322	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
323	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
324	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
325	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
326	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
327	
328	  The universal quotient $u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
329	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\( is given on \)U^I\( by the matrix \)X^I$
330	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
331	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
332	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
333	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
334	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
335	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
336	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
337	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
338	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
339	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
340	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
341	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
342	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism $T \to U^I \subset
343	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\(. Base change from \)\mathbb{Z}$ to a general noetherian
344	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
345	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
346	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
347	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
348	\end{proof}
349	
350	\section{Representability of the Quot scheme}
351	\label{sec:quot_representable}
352	
353	\begin{theorem}\leanok
354	[Representability of the Quot functor]
355	  \label{thm:quot_representable}
356	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotScheme}
357	  
358	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, "Main Existence Theorems"
359	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
360	  % L2220-L2273); cf.\ Grothendieck, FGA TDTE-IV.
361	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
362	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi: X\to S\) a projective morphism,
363	  %  and \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\). Then for any
364	  %  coherent \({\mathcal O}_X\)-module \(E\) and any polynomial \(\Phi \in \Q[\lambda]\),
365	  %  the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) is representable
366	  %  by a projective \(S\)-scheme \(\quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\)."
367	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, Theorem (Grothendieck), Theorem
368	  (Altman--Kleiman) (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ Grothendieck, FGA TDTE-IV.}
369	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a projective morphism,
370	  \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent
371	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). Then the
372	  functor \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) of \cref{def:quot_functor} is
373	  representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\),
374	  equipped with a universal quotient
375	  \[
376	    q^{\mathrm{univ}} :
377	    \pi^*_{\Quot} E \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}^{\mathrm{univ}}
378	    \quad \text{on } X \times_S \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S},
379	  \]
380	  flat over \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) with constant Hilbert polynomial
381	  \(\Phi\) on every fiber. The Hilbert scheme is the special case
382	  \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\): \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} = \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\)
383	  represents the functor of \(T\)-flat closed subschemes
384	  \(Y \subset X_T\) with fiberwise Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\).
385	\end{theorem}
386	
387	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: this proof is long (~5 named sub-sections in [Nitsure]
388	% \S 5). Each sub-step is recorded with its own SOURCE QUOTE in the
389	% sub-lemmas below. The structural skeleton of the proof is restated in
390	% project notation in the \begin{proof} block.
391	\begin{proof}
392	  
393	  The proof has four steps; each step is a sub-lemma in the next
394	  sub-sections.
395	  \begin{enumerate}
396	    \item \textbf{(Boundedness, \cref{lem:quot_boundedness}.)} Pick the
397	      Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity bound $m = m(\mathrm{rank}\,V,
398	      \mathrm{rank}\,W, \Phi)$ uniformly across all fibers (by Mumford's
399	      \(m\)-regularity theorem applied to \(E_s\) and to all coherent
400	      quotients of \(E_s\) with Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\)). For \(r \ge m\)
401	      and any \(T \to S\) and any \(T\)-flat quotient
402	      \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) with Hilbert polynomial
403	      \(\Phi\), the higher direct images \(R^i (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{F}(r)\)
404	      and \(R^i (\pi_T)_* E_T(r)\) and \(R^i (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}(r)\)
405	      vanish for \(i \ge 1\) (where \(\mathcal{G} = \ker q\)), and the
406	      direct images \((\pi_T)_* \mathcal{F}(r)\), \((\pi_T)_* E_T(r)\),
407	      \((\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}(r)\) are locally free of ranks fixed by the
408	      data; the canonical maps \((\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_*(-)(r) \to (-)(r)\)
409	      are surjective. Cohomology-and-base-change (\cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology},
410	      Stacks tag 02KH) propagates these properties across \(T\)-base change.
411	    \item \textbf{(Grassmannian embedding, \cref{lem:quot_alpha_injective}.)}
412	      Reduce by \cref{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W} to the case
413	      \(X = \mathbb{P}_S(V)\), \(E = \pi^* W\) with \(V, W\) locally free
414	      on \(S\), \(L = \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}(V)}(1)\). Fix \(r \ge m\).
415	      The Grassmannian embedding
416	      \[
417	        \alpha : \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \longrightarrow
418	        \mathrm{Grass}\!\left(
419	          W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V,\; \Phi(r)
420	        \right)
421	      \]
422	      sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) on \(T\) to the locally free
423	      quotient \((\pi_T)_*(q(r)) : (\pi_T)_* E_T(r) \to (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{F}(r)\).
424	      This is injective on \(T\)-valued points: from the map $T \to
425	      \mathrm{Gr}_S(W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))$ one recovers
426	      the kernel-pull-back \((\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}(r) \to E_T(r)\),
427	      whose cokernel is \(q(r) : E_T(r) \to \mathcal{F}(r)\);
428	      twisting back by \(-r\) recovers \(q\).
429	    \item \textbf{(Locally closed in the Grassmannian via flattening
430	      stratification.)} Let $T_0 = \mathrm{Gr}_S(W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V,
431	      \Phi(r))$ with universal quotient
432	      \(f : (W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V)_{T_0} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{J}\)
433	      and kernel \(\mathcal{K}\). Form the composite
434	      $h : \pi_{T_0}^* \mathcal{K} \hookrightarrow
435	      \pi_{T_0}^* (W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V) = \pi_{T_0}^* (\pi_{T_0})_* E_{T_0}
436	      \twoheadrightarrow E_{T_0}$
437	      and let \(q := \mathrm{coker}(h) : E_{T_0} \to \mathcal{F}\). By the
438	      flattening stratification (\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}
439	      of \cref{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\)
440	      on \(X_{T_0}\), there exists a locally closed subscheme
441	      \(T_0^\Phi \subseteq T_0\) such that for any \(\phi : Y \to T_0\), the
442	      pulled-back cokernel \(\mathcal{F}_Y\) is \(Y\)-flat with fiberwise
443	      Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\) iff \(\phi\) factors through \(T_0^\Phi\).
444	      The pair \((T_0^\Phi, \text{universal cokernel})\) represents
445	      \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\).
446	    \item \textbf{(Closed, hence projective,
447	      \cref{lem:quot_valuative_criterion}.)} The functor
448	      \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) satisfies the valuative criterion of
449	      properness with respect to DVRs (Exercise (7) of \S 1, proved
450	      using that flatness over a DVR is torsion-freeness). Combined
451	      with the locally closed embedding of step 3 into the projective
452	      $\mathrm{Gr}_S \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^{\Phi(r)}
453	      (W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V))$, this upgrades to a \emph{closed}
454	      embedding. Hence \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) is a projective \(S\)-scheme.
455	  \end{enumerate}
456	  Finally, by \cref{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W}, the general case
457	  (arbitrary \(X\) projective over \(S\) and arbitrary coherent \(E\)) follows
458	  from the case \(X = \mathbb{P}(V)\), \(E = \pi^* W\): pick a closed
459	  embedding \(X \subset \mathbb{P}(V)\) and write \(E\) as a coherent quotient
460	  of \(\pi^*(W)(\nu)\) for some vector bundle \(W\) on \(S\) and integer \(\nu\);
461	  the Quot scheme of the original data is then a closed subscheme of the
462	  Quot scheme of the universal data, by the closed-embedding clause of
463	  \cref{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W}.
464	\end{proof}

466	\section{Sub-lemmas of the construction}
467	\label{sec:quot_sub_lemmas}
468	
469	\begin{lemma}[Reduction to the universal case]
470	  \label{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W}
471	  
472	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Reduction to the case of
473	  % \(\Quot_{\pi^*W/\PP(V)/S}^{\Phi,L}\)"
474	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
475	  % L2294-L2332).
476	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
477	  % "It is enough to prove Theorem REF in the special case
478	  %  that \(X = \PP(V)\) and
479	  %  \(E= \pi^*(W)\) where \(V\) and \(W\) are vector bundles on \(S\), as
480	  %  a consequence of the next lemma.
481	  %
482	  %  \begin{lemma}
483	  %  (i) Let \(\nu\) be any integer.
484	  %  Then tensoring by \(L^{\nu}\) gives an isomorphism of
485	  %  functors from \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) to
486	  %  \(\Quot_{E(\nu)/X/S}^{\Psi,L}\) where the polynomial
487	  %  \(\Psi \in \Q[\lambda]\) is defined by \(\Psi(\lambda) = \Phi(\lambda + \nu)\).
488	  %
489	  %  (ii) Let \(\phi : E \to G\) be a surjective homomorphism of
490	  %  coherent sheaves on \(X\). Then the corresponding natural transformation
491	  %  \(\Quot_{G/X/S}^{\Phi,L} \to \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\)
492	  %  is a closed embedding.
493	  %  \end{lemma}"
494	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
495	  Let \(S\), \(X\), \(L\), \(E\), \(\Phi\) be as in \cref{def:quot_functor}.
496	  \begin{enumerate}
497	    \item (Twist-shift.) For \(\nu \in \mathbb{Z}\), tensoring by
498	      \(L^{\otimes \nu}\) gives an isomorphism of functors
499	      $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \xrightarrow{\sim}
500	      \Quot^{\Psi,L}_{E(\nu)/X/S}\( with \)\Psi(\lambda) = \Phi(\lambda + \nu)$.
501	    \item (Surjection induces closed embedding.) Given a surjection
502	      \(\phi : E \twoheadrightarrow G\) of coherent sheaves on \(X\), the
503	      induced natural transformation
504	      \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{G/X/S} \to \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) is a closed
505	      embedding of functors.
506	  \end{enumerate}
507	  Consequently, if \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\pi^* W / \mathbb{P}(V)/S}\) is
508	  representable for all \(V\), \(W\) vector bundles on \(S\), then so is
509	  \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) for every \(X \subset \mathbb{P}(V)\) closed and
510	  every coherent \(E\) on \(X\) that is a quotient of \(\pi^*(W)(\nu)|_X\) for
511	  some \(W\) and \(\nu\).
512	\end{lemma}
513	
514	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The statement (i) is obvious. The statement (ii) just
515	% says that given any locally noetherian scheme \(T\) and a family
516	% \(\langle \F,q\rangle \in \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(T)\), there exists
517	% a closed subscheme \(T' \subset T\) with the following universal property:
518	% for any locally noetherian scheme \(U\) and a morphism \(f: U\to T\),
519	% the pulled back homomorphism of \({\mathcal O}_{X_U}\)-modules \(q_U : E_U \to \F_U\)
520	% factors via the pulled back homomorphism \(\phi_U : E_U \to G_U\)
521	% if and only if \(U \to T\) factors via \(T'\hra T\).
522	% This is satisfied by taking \(T'\) to be the vanishing scheme
523	% for the composite homomorphism \(\ker(\phi) \hra  E \stackrel{q}{\to} \F\)
524	% of coherent sheaves on \(X_T\) (see Remark REF),
525	% which makes sense here as both \(\ker(\phi)\) and \(\F\) are coherent
526	% on \(X_T\) and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\)."
527	\begin{proof}
528	  
529	  (i) is direct from the definitions: tensoring with \(L^{\otimes \nu}\) is
530	  an autoequivalence of coherent sheaves on \(X\) (and on every \(X_T\)),
531	  preserving surjectivity, \(T\)-flatness, and properness of support, and
532	  shifting the Hilbert polynomial by \(\nu\) in the variable.
533	
534	  (ii) Given a family \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) on \(T\) in
535	  \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), the locus where \(q\) factors through
536	  \(\phi : E \twoheadrightarrow G\) is cut out by the vanishing of the
537	  composite $\ker(\phi)|_{X_T} \hookrightarrow E_T \xrightarrow{q}
538	  \mathcal{F}\(. Because \)\mathcal{F}\( is \)T$-flat, the schematic image of
539	  this composite descends to a closed subscheme \(T' \subset T\) such that
540	  \(f : U \to T\) factors through \(T'\) iff \(q_U\) factors through \(\phi_U\).
541	  Hence \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{G/X/S} \hookrightarrow \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\)
542	  is a closed embedding of subfunctors.
543	\end{proof}
544	
545	\begin{lemma}[Uniform Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity bound]
546	  \label{lem:quot_boundedness}
547	  
548	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Use of \(m\)-Regularity"
549	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
550	  % L2346-L2404). Builds on [Nitsure] \S 2 (Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity)
551	  % and \S 3 (semi-continuity and base-change), cited internally as
552	  % Theorem~"Mumford" and Theorem~"Base-change for flat sheaves".
553	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
554	  % "We consider the sheaf \(E = \pi^*(W)\) on \(X=\PP(V)\) where
555	  %  \(V\) is a vector bundle on \(S\),
556	  %  and take \(L = {\mathcal O}_{\PP(V)}(1)\).
557	  %  For any field \(k\) and a \(k\)-valued point
558	  %  \(s\) of \(S\), we have an isomorphism
559	  %  \(\PP(V)_s \simeq \PP^n_k\) where \(n=\rank(V)-1\),
560	  %  and the restricted sheaf \(E_s\) on \(\PP(V)_s\) is isomorphic to
561	  %  \(\oplus^p{\mathcal O}_{\PP(V)_s}\) where \(p=\rank(W)\).
562	  %  It follows from Theorem REF
563	  %  that given any \(\Phi \in \Q[\lambda]\), there exists an integer
564	  %  \(m\) which depends only on \(\rank(V)\), \(\rank(W)\) and \(\Phi\),
565	  %  such that for any field \(k\) and a \(k\)-valued point \(s\) of \(S\),
566	  %  the sheaf \(E_s\) on \(\PP(V)_s\) is \(m\)-regular, and for any
567	  %  coherent quotient \(q: E_s \to \F\) on \(\PP(V)_s\) with
568	  %  Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\), the sheaf \(\F\) and the kernel sheaf
569	  %  \(\G\subset E_s\) of \(q\) are both \(m\)-regular."
570	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, "Use of \(m\)-Regularity" (FGA Explained
571	  Ch.~5).}
572	  Let \(X = \mathbb{P}_S(V)\), \(E = \pi^* W\) with \(V\), \(W\) vector bundles on
573	  \(S\) of ranks \(n+1\), \(p\), \(L = \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}(V)}(1)\), and
574	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). There exists an integer
575	  \(m = m(n, p, \Phi)\), depending only on \(n\), \(p\), and \(\Phi\), such that
576	  for every geometric point \(s\) of \(S\) and every coherent quotient
577	  \(q : E_s \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) with Hilbert polynomial
578	  \(\Phi\), the sheaves \(E_s\), \(\mathcal{F}\), and \(\ker q\) on \(X_s\) are all
579	  \(m\)-regular in the sense of Castelnuovo--Mumford. Consequently, for
580	  every \(T \to S\) and every \(T\)-flat quotient
581	  \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) on \(X_T\) with Hilbert
582	  polynomial \(\Phi\) and every \(r \ge m\), the conclusions \textbf{(*)} and
583	  \textbf{(**)} of \cite[\S 5, "Use of \(m\)-Regularity"]{nitsure-hilbert-quot}
584	  hold: the direct images \((\pi_T)_* \mathcal{F}(r)\), \((\pi_T)_* E_T(r)\),
585	  \((\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}(r)\) are locally free of fixed ranks; the
586	  canonical surjections \((\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_*(-)(r) \twoheadrightarrow (-)(r)\)
587	  hold; and the higher direct images \(R^i (\pi_T)_* (-)(r)\) vanish for
588	  \(i \ge 1\).
589	\end{lemma}
590	
591	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see [Nitsure], \S 5, "Use of \(m\)-Regularity"
592	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L2346-L2404).
593	% The proof invokes the m-regularity theorem of [Nitsure] \S 2
594	% (Theorem "Mumford") to extract the uniform bound m, and the
595	% base-change-for-flat-sheaves theorem of [Nitsure] \S 3 (cited as
596	% "Base-change for flat sheaves") to propagate the conclusions across \(T\).
597	\begin{proof}
598	  
599	  Each geometric fiber \(E_s\) is a trivial bundle on \(\mathbb{P}^n_{\kappa(s)}\)
600	  of rank \(p\), so \(E_s\) is \(0\)-regular. The Castelnuovo--Mumford
601	  \(m\)-regularity theorem of [Nitsure] \S 2 produces, for any fixed Hilbert
602	  polynomial \(\Phi\), a uniform integer \(m = m(n, p, \Phi)\) above which
603	  every coherent quotient of \(E_s\) with Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\) -- and
604	  its kernel -- is \(m\)-regular. The Castelnuovo Lemma of [Nitsure] \S 2
605	  converts \(m\)-regularity into vanishing of \(H^i((-) (r))\) for \(i \ge 1\)
606	  and \(r \ge m\), and into global generation of \(H^0((-)(r))\). To
607	  propagate the fiber-wise statement to families over \(T\), apply the
608	  flat-base-change theorem (\cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}, Stacks
609	  tag 02KH) along \(T \to S\): cohomology of flat families commutes with
610	  base change, so the direct images \((\pi_T)_*(-)(r)\) are locally free of
611	  the rank dictated by the fiber Hilbert polynomials, and the canonical
612	  maps \((\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_*(-)(r) \to (-)(r)\) inherit fiberwise
613	  surjectivity.
614	\end{proof}
615	
616	\begin{lemma}[Grassmannian embedding \(\alpha\) is injective]
617	  \label{lem:quot_alpha_injective}
618	  
619	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Embedding Quot into Grassmannian"
620	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
621	  % L2409-L2451).
622	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
623	  % "We now fix a positive integer \(r\) such that \(r\ge m\).
624	  %  Note that the rank of \({\pi_T}_*\F(r)\) is \(\Phi(r)\)
625	  %  and \(\pi_*E(r) = W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V\).
626	  %  Therefore the surjective homomorphism
627	  %  \({\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)\)
628	  %  defines an element of the set
629	  %  \(\Grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V , \Phi(r))(T)\).
630	  %  We thus get a morphism of functors
631	  %  \(\)\alpha : \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \to
632	  %  \Grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))\(\)
633	  %  It associates to \(q: E_T \to \F\) the quotient
634	  %  \({\pi_T}_*(q(r)) : {\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)\).
635	  %
636	  %  The above morphism \(\alpha\) is injective because the quotient
637	  %  \(q: E_T \to \F\) can be recovered from
638	  %  \({\pi_T}_*(q(r)) : {\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)\)
639	  %  as follows."
640	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, "Embedding Quot into Grassmannian"
641	  (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
642	  In the universal setup \(X = \mathbb{P}_S(V)\), \(E = \pi^* W\), fix
643	  \(r \ge m\) from \cref{lem:quot_boundedness}. The natural transformation
644	  \[
645	    \alpha :
646	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \longrightarrow
647	    \mathrm{Grass}\!\left(
648	      W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V,\; \Phi(r)
649	    \right),
650	    \qquad
651	    \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle \longmapsto
652	    \langle (\pi_T)_*\mathcal{F}(r),\, (\pi_T)_*(q(r))\rangle
653	  \]
654	  is well-defined (the target is locally free of rank \(\Phi(r)\), and the
655	  canonical isomorphism $\pi_* E(r) \cong W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S}
656	  \mathrm{Sym}^r V\( gives the source) and \emph{injective} on \)T$-points:
657	  the data of the locally free quotient at level \(r\) determines
658	  \(q : E_T \to \mathcal{F}\).
659	\end{lemma}
660	
661	\begin{proof}
662	  
663	  Suppose \(\alpha(T)\) sends both \(\langle \mathcal{F}_1, q_1\rangle\) and
664	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}_2, q_2\rangle\) to the same \(T\)-point of the
665	  Grassmannian. Then the pulled-back kernel
666	  $(\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}_i(r) \to (\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* E_T(r)
667	  = \pi_{T_0}^* (W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V)_T$
668	  is the same. The diagram \textbf{(**)} of
669	  \cref{lem:quot_boundedness} gives a right-exact sequence
670	  \[
671	    (\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}_i(r) \xrightarrow{h}
672	    E_T(r) \xrightarrow{q_i(r)} \mathcal{F}_i(r) \to 0,
673	  \]
674	  so \(q_i(r)\) is recovered as the cokernel of \(h\); twisting by \(-r\)
675	  recovers \(q_i\), giving \(q_1 = q_2\) and hence
676	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}_1, q_1\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}_2, q_2\rangle\).
677	\end{proof}

679	\begin{lemma}[Valuative criterion of properness for \(\Quot\)]
680	  \label{lem:quot_valuative_criterion}
681	  
682	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Valuative Criterion for Properness"
683	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
684	  % L2510-L2542). Original reference: EGA IV(2) 2.8.1, cited there.
685	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
686	  % "The functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) satisfies the following valuative
687	  %  criterion for properness over \(S\): given any discrete valuation ring
688	  %  \(R\) over \(S\) with quotient field \(K\), the restriction map
689	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec R) \to \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec K)\(\)
690	  %  is bijective. This can be seen as follows.
691	  %  Given any coherent quotient
692	  %  \(q: E_K \to \F\) on \(X_R\) which defines an element \(\langle \F, q\rangle\)
693	  %  of \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec K)\).
694	  %  Let \(\ov{\F}\) be the image of the composite homomorphism
695	  %  \(E_R \to j_*(E_K) \to j_*\F\) where \(j : X_K \hra X_R\) is the open
696	  %  inclusion. Let \(\ov{q} : E_R\to \ov{\F}\) be the induced surjection.
697	  %  Then we leave it to the reader to verify that
698	  %  \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) is an element of \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec R)\)
699	  %  which maps to \(\langle \F, q\rangle\), and is the unique such element.
700	  %  (Use the basic fact that being flat over a dvr
701	  %  is the same as being torsion-free.)"
702	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, "Valuative Criterion for Properness"
703	  (FGA Explained Ch.~5); EGA IV(2) 2.8.1.}
704	  The functor \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) satisfies the valuative criterion
705	  for properness over \(S\): for every discrete valuation ring \(R\) with
706	  fraction field \(K\) and every morphism \(\Spec R \to S\), the restriction
707	  map
708	  \[
709	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}(\Spec R)
710	    \longrightarrow
711	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}(\Spec K)
712	  \]
713	  is bijective.
714	\end{lemma}
715	
716	\begin{proof}
717	  
718	  Given a \(\Spec K\)-point \(\langle \mathcal{F}_K, q_K\rangle\) on \(X_K\),
719	  let \(j : X_K \hookrightarrow X_R\) be the open inclusion. Define
720	  \(\overline{\mathcal{F}}\) as the image of the composite
721	  \(E_R \to j_* E_K \to j_* \mathcal{F}_K\), with induced surjection
722	  \(\overline{q} : E_R \to \overline{\mathcal{F}}\). The sheaf
723	  \(\overline{\mathcal{F}}\) is torsion-free over \(R\) (being a subsheaf of
724	  \(j_* \mathcal{F}_K\)), hence \(R\)-flat (flatness over a DVR
725	  \(=\) torsion-freeness), and restricts on the generic fiber to
726	  \(\mathcal{F}_K\). The pair \((\overline{\mathcal{F}}, \overline{q})\) is
727	  the unique extension of \(\langle \mathcal{F}_K, q_K\rangle\) to a
728	  \(\Spec R\)-point (uniqueness: any other extension agrees on the dense
729	  open \(X_K \subset X_R\) and is torsion-free, hence equals the image
730	  construction). The Hilbert polynomial is constant on connected
731	  \(\Spec R\) by flatness.
732	\end{proof}

734	\section{Cohomology and base change}
735	\label{sec:cohomology_base_change}
736	
737	The Quot construction uses cohomology-and-base-change in two places: the
738	boundedness step (\cref{lem:quot_boundedness}) and the Grassmannian
739	embedding (\cref{lem:quot_alpha_injective}). We record the relevant
740	statement as a named theorem so the Lean encoding can cite it directly.
741	
742	\begin{theorem}\leanok
743	[Flat base change of cohomology]
744	  \label{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}
745	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChangeCohomology}
746	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 02KH (lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology)
747	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947-L970).
748	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
749	  % "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
750	  %  \(\)
751	  %  \xymatrix{
752	  %  X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
753	  %  S' \ar[r]^g & S
754	  %  }
755	  %  \(\)
756	  %  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
757	  %  with pullback \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\).
758	  %  Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
759	  %  For any \(i \geq 0\)
760	  %  \begin{enumerate}
761	  %  \item the base change map of
762	  %  Cohomology, Lemma REF
763	  %  is an isomorphism
764	  %  \(\)
765	  %  g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
766	  %  \(\)
767	  %  \item if \(S = \Spec(A)\) and \(S' = \Spec(B)\), then
768	  %  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\).
769	  %  \end{enumerate}"
770	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 02KH (lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology).}
771	  Let
772	  \[
773	    \begin{array}{ccc}
774	      X' & \xrightarrow{g'} & X \\
775	      \downarrow f' &  & \downarrow f \\
776	      S' & \xrightarrow{g} & S
777	    \end{array}
778	  \]
779	  be a cartesian diagram of schemes with \(g\) flat and \(f\) quasi-compact and
780	  quasi-separated. Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent
781	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module with pull-back
782	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^* \mathcal{F}\). For every \(i \ge 0\):
783	  \begin{enumerate}
784	    \item the base-change map
785	      \(g^* R^i f_* \mathcal{F} \to R^i f'_* \mathcal{F}'\) is an isomorphism;
786	    \item if \(S = \Spec A\) and \(S' = \Spec B\), then $H^i(X, \mathcal{F})
787	      \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
788	  \end{enumerate}
789	\end{theorem}
790

803	\begin{definition}\leanok
804	[Canonical base-change map on sheaves of modules]
805	  \label{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map}
806	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.canonicalBaseChangeMap}
807	  \uses{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}
808	  Given a cartesian square of schemes
809	  \(\square = (g' : X' \to X,\ f' : X' \to S',\ f : X \to S,\ g : S' \to S)\)
810	  with \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) and \(g\) flat, the
811	  \emph{canonical base-change map} on sheaves of modules is the natural
812	  transformation \(g'^* f^* \mathcal{F} \longrightarrow f'^* g^* \mathcal{F}\)
813	  arising from the universal property of pullback. Encoded as a morphism
814	  of \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\)-valued sheaf functors on the pulled-back open
815	  cover; this is the morphism whose iso-status discharges
816	  \cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}(1) in the present
817	  finite-locally-free / quasi-coherent-of-finite-type setting.
818	\end{definition}

827	  \((\text{canonicalBaseChangeMap}\ \square).\text{app}\ \mathcal{F}.\text{app}\ U\)
828	  of \cref{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map} is an isomorphism. Proof
829	  reduces (Stacks 02KH(2)) to the affine-base case: \(H^0\) commutes with
830	  flat extension of scalars on a quasi-compact quasi-separated affine
831	  morphism.

971	\section{Lean encoding}
972	\label{sec:quot_lean_encoding}
973	
974	The Lean target file is the new
975	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean}. The construction
976	proceeds in three phases.
977	
978	\paragraph{Phase 1: Hilbert polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}).}
979	On a finite-type morphism \(\pi : X \to S\) with \(S\) noetherian and a
980	coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) with proper support over \(S\), the per-fiber
981	Hilbert polynomial is encoded as a function $s \mapsto
982	\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]$. Mathlib provides
983	\texttt{CategoryTheory.PolynomialModule} and Euler-characteristic
984	infrastructure on coherent sheaves on noetherian schemes; the polynomial
985	property of \(m \mapsto \chi(\mathcal{F} \otimes L^{\otimes m})\) requires
986	Snapper's Lemma, which is a project-side sub-build on top of the
987	graded-Euler-characteristic infrastructure.
988	
989	\paragraph{Phase 2: Grassmannian scheme (\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme},
990	\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).} The Grassmannian
991	\(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) is encoded as the relative gluing of \(\binom{r}{d}\)
992	affine charts \(U^I\) (each isomorphic to a relative affine space
993	\(\mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_S\)) via the Pl\"ucker cocycle \(\theta_{I,J}\). The
994	gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData}. The
995	universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\) is glued by the transition cocycle
996	\(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\), and the determinant line bundle
997	\(\det \mathcal{U}\) gives the Pl\"ucker closed embedding into
998	\(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\). The representability statement uses
999	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} (as in
1000	\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) on the universal pair
1001	\((\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d), \mathcal{U})\).
1002	
1003	\paragraph{Phase 3: Quot scheme (\cref{thm:quot_representable}).}
1004	The Quot functor is constructed as a locally closed subfunctor of the
1005	Grassmannian
1006	\(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\), via:
1007	\begin{enumerate}
1008	  \item the uniform \(m\)-regularity bound from
1009	    \cref{lem:quot_boundedness} (input: Castelnuovo--Mumford theorem,
1010	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.CastelnuovoMumford} -- project-side sub-build);
1011	  \item the Grassmannian embedding \(\alpha\) from
1012	    \cref{lem:quot_alpha_injective};
1013	  \item the flattening stratification (\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}
1014	    of \cref{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}, A.2.a) applied to the
1015	    universal quotient on the Grassmannian, producing the locally closed
1016	    stratum \(T_0^\Phi\);
1017	  \item the valuative-criterion upgrade
1018	    (\cref{lem:quot_valuative_criterion}) to closed embedding, hence
1019	    projective \(S\)-scheme.
1020	\end{enumerate}
1021	The reduction \cref{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W} from the universal
1022	case to the general case is encoded as a closed embedding of representable
1023	subfunctors via the kernel-vanishing scheme.
1024	
1025	\paragraph{Cohomology and base change.}
1026	\cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology} is the Mathlib-equivalent of
1027	Stacks tag 02KH. Mathlib carries a partial form
1028	(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.flat\_base\_change}) for affine bases; the
1029	full \(R^i f_*\) statement is a project-side bridge if not present at the
1030	pinned commit.
1031	
1032	\paragraph{Dependencies summary.}
1033	This file directly consumes
1034	\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} (A.1.a, on disk) for relative-spectrum
1035	gluing infrastructure and
1036	\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists} from
1037	\cref{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification} (A.2.a, written this iter)
1038	for the flattening stratum. Downstream, \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}
1039	(A.2.c) consumes \cref{thm:quot_representable} via the special case
1040	\(E = \mathcal{O}_C\) on a smooth proper curve \(C/k\), giving the Hilbert
1041	scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot^{\Phi,\mathcal{O}_C(1)}_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
1042	and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective divisors.
1043	
1044	\section{Out of scope}
1045	\label{sec:quot_out_of_scope}
1046	
1047	This chapter packages \emph{only} the Quot-scheme existence statement and
1048	its proof skeleton via the Grassmannian + flattening-stratification
1049	route. The following are explicitly out of scope here:
1050	\begin{itemize}
1051	  \item \textbf{Flattening stratification} (A.2.a) -- consumed-by, not
1052	    proved-here. Its blueprint home is the new chapter
1053	    \texttt{Picard\_FlatteningStratification.tex}.
1054	  \item \textbf{Hilbert scheme as a separate chapter}. The Hilbert scheme
1055	    \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) is the special case
1056	    \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\) of the Quot scheme and is
1057	    introduced inline (\cref{def:quot_functor}); no separate chapter.
1058	  \item \textbf{Castelnuovo--Mumford \(m\)-regularity theorem} -- input to
1059	    \cref{lem:quot_boundedness}, not proved here. The proof lives in

1061	  \item \textbf{Semi-continuity and base change} (Nitsure \S 3) -- the
1062	    flat-base-change theorem of cohomology (\cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology})
1063	    is stated here as a lemma, but the upstream Mathlib bridge / project-side
1064	    sub-build is not constructed in this chapter.
1065	  \item \textbf{Snapper's Lemma} -- the polynomial-eventually property of
1066	    Euler characteristics, used in \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Cited
1067	    via Nitsure \S 1 and (textbook reference) Hartshorne III.5.2;
1068	    project-side sub-build for the Lean encoding.
1069	  \item \textbf{Identification with \(\mathbb{P}^n\) and relative
1070	    Grassmannian variants} (Nitsure \S 1, Exercises (1)--(4)) --
1071	    optional applications, not consumed by Route A.
1072	  \item \textbf{Hironaka's counterexample to representability of

1076	  \item \textbf{Group-scheme structure on \(\Pic_{C/k}\)} (A.2.c) -- the
1077	    abelian-group-scheme refinement is downstream of the Quot existence
1078	    statement; its blueprint home is
1079	    \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
1080	  \item \textbf{Identity-component \(\Pic^0\) and abelian-variety
1081	    structure} (A.3) -- a separate Route A sub-row, fed by but not

2	\label{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.b sub-row of Route A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and the corollary ``regular local ring
8	% \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay'' that A.4.a (the codim-1 extension proof for
9	% Albanese morphisms out of a regular projective surface) consumes. Per
10	% STRATEGY.md L30 this row is gated downstream on A.4.a but is independently
11	% startable on the Mathlib side: the algebra here is decoupled from the
12	% Albanese geometry and lives entirely under \texttt{RingTheory.*}. The
13	% target Lean file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean}
14	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
15	% creating it. Note that Mathlib already houses parts of the depth /
16	% projective-dimension API
17	% (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Depth.*},
18	% \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.RegularSequence},
19	% \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.ProjectiveResolution}); a Plan-Agent audit

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
28	or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
30	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} \(2\), hence a rational function with no codimension-\(1\) poles on a neighbourhood of \(x\)
33	extends across \(x\). The depth \(\geq 2\) inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring \(R\) one has \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\),
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension \(d\) admits an \(R\)-regular sequence of length \(d\) generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\), i.e.\ \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(Corollary~REF below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its \(\text{Ext}\) reformulation
47	    (Definition~REF and Lemma~REF),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (Definition~REF),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (Lemma~REF),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (Theorem~REF), and
54	  \item the corollary regular \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (Corollary~REF).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (Section~REF) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes Corollary~REF to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.
60	
61	\section{Depth of a module over a local ring}
62	\label{sec:ab_depth}
63	
64	\begin{definition}
65	
66	\leanok
67	  [Depth of a module]
68	  \label{def:depth}
69	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth}
70	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth)
71	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17748-L17759).
72	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
73	  % "Let \(R\) be a ring, and \(I \subset R\) an ideal. Let \(M\) be a finite \(R\)-module.
74	  %  The {\it \(I\)-depth} of \(M\), denoted \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is defined as follows:
75	  %  \begin{enumerate}
76	  %  \item if \(IM \not = M\), then \(\text{depth}_I(M)\) is the supremum in
77	  %  \(\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences in \(I\),
78	  %  \item if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\).
79	  %  \end{enumerate}
80	  %  If \((R, \mathfrak m)\) is local we call \(\text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M)\) simply
81	  %  the {\it depth} of \(M\)."
82	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth).}
83	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(I \subseteq R\) be an ideal, and let \(M\) be a finitely
84	  generated \(R\)-module. The \emph{\(I\)-depth} of \(M\), written \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is the
85	  supremum in \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences contained
86	  in \(I\), provided \(IM \neq M\); if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\). When
87	  \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is a Noetherian local ring we abbreviate
88	  \(\text{depth}(M) := \text{depth}_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)\) and call this simply the
89	  \emph{depth} of \(M\). The depth of the ring \(R\) itself is \(\text{depth}(R)\), the depth
90	  of \(R\) as a module over itself.
91	\end{definition}
92	
93	Recall that a sequence \(f_1, \dots, f_r \in R\) is called \emph{\(M\)-regular} if
94	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_r)M \neq 0\) and for each \(i\), the element \(f_i\) is a non-zero-divisor on
95	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_{i-1})M\). The empty sequence is regular on \(M\) iff \(M \neq 0\) (it is
96	\emph{not} regular on the zero module, but the convention \(\text{depth}(0) = \infty\)
97	above is the usual one).
98	
99	The two practical computations of depth are the regular-sequence definition (used to
100	establish a \emph{lower} bound) and the \(\text{Ext}\) characterisation (used to establish an
101	\emph{upper} bound and to prove the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequality).
102	
103	\begin{lemma}
104	
105	\leanok
106	  [Depth via \(\text{Ext}\)]
107	  \label{lem:depth_via_ext}
108	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index}
109	  
110	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext)
111	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17845-L17885).
112	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
113	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\).
114	  %  Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module. Then \(\text{depth}(M)\)
115	  %  is equal to the smallest integer \(i\) such that
116	  %  \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\) is nonzero."
117	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext).}
118	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring with residue field
119	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak{m}\) and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely generated \(R\)-module. Then
120	  \[
121	    \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \inf\bigl\{\, i \in \mathbb{N} \;\bigm|\; \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \,\bigr\}.
122	  \]
123	\end{lemma}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
126	% "Let \(\delta(M)\) denote the depth of \(M\) and let \(i(M)\) denote
127	%  the smallest integer \(i\) such that \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\)
128	%  is nonzero. We will see in a moment that \(i(M) < \infty\).
129	%  By Lemma REF we have
130	%  \(\delta(M) = 0\) if and only if \(i(M) = 0\), because
131	%  \(\mathfrak m \in \text{Ass}(M)\) exactly means
132	%  that \(i(M) = 0\). Hence if \(\delta(M)\) or \(i(M)\) is \(> 0\), then we may
133	%  choose \(x \in \mathfrak m\) such that (a) \(x\) is a nonzerodivisor
134	%  on \(M\), and (b) \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta(M) - 1\).
135	%  Consider the long exact sequence
136	%  of Ext-groups associated to the short exact sequence
137	%  \(0 \to M \to M \to M/xM \to 0\) by Lemma REF:
138	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
139	%  Since \(x \in \mathfrak m\) all the maps $\Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)
140	%  \to \Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)$ are zero, see
141	%  Lemma REF.
142	%  Thus it is clear that \(i(M/xM) = i(M) - 1\). Induction on
143	%  \(\delta(M)\) finishes the proof."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	\leanok
148	  Write \(\delta := \text{depth}(M)\) and \(i := \inf\{ j : \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \}\).
149	  The base case \(\delta = 0\) is exactly \(\text{Hom}_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0\) (some element of
150	  \(M\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\), i.e.\ \(\mathfrak{m} \in \text{Ass}(M)\), i.e.\
151	  \(\mathfrak{m}\) contains a zero-divisor on \(M\)), which is \(i = 0\). For the inductive step
152	  pick \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) a non-zero-divisor on \(M\) with
153	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta - 1\). The short exact sequence
154	  \(0 \to M \xrightarrow{x} M \to M/xM \to 0\) induces a long exact sequence of $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa,
155	  -)\(. Multiplication by \)x\( annihilates each \)\text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ (because
156	  \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) and \(\kappa\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\)), so the long exact
157	  sequence breaks into short pieces giving
158	  \(\text{Ext}^{j-1}_R(\kappa, M/xM) \cong \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)\) at the relevant
159	  indices, and the smallest non-vanishing index drops by \(1\). By induction
160	  \(i(M/xM) = i - 1 = \delta - 1\), so \(i = \delta\).
161	\end{proof}

163	\section{Projective dimension}
164	\label{sec:ab_pd}
165	
166	\begin{definition}
167	
168	\leanok
169	  [Projective dimension]
170	  \label{def:projective_dimension}
171	  \lean{Module.projectiveDimension}
172	  % NOTE: Mathlib pinned at b80f227 may already expose this as
173	  % `Module.projectiveDimension` via
174	  % `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.ProjectiveDimension` /
175	  % `Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ProjectiveResolution`; the prover should re-export
176	  % rather than re-define if the API is present. This block fixes the
177	  % mathematical content the prover must match.
178	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(M\) be an \(R\)-module. The \emph{projective
179	  dimension} of \(M\), written \(\text{pd}_R(M)\), is the infimum in
180	  \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths \(n\) of projective resolutions
181	  \[
182	    0 \longrightarrow P_n \longrightarrow P_{n-1} \longrightarrow \cdots
183	    \longrightarrow P_1 \longrightarrow P_0 \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
184	  \]
185	  with each \(P_i\) a projective \(R\)-module. We say \(M\) has \emph{finite projective dimension}
186	  if \(\text{pd}_R(M) < \infty\), and \emph{infinite projective dimension} otherwise. By
187	  convention \(\text{pd}_R(0) = -\infty\) (some references); we use \(\text{pd}_R(0) = 0\) here
188	  (the empty resolution \(0 \to 0\) of length \(0\)), but the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula in
189	  Theorem~REF concerns only nonzero \(M\).
190	  In the local Noetherian setting a finitely generated \(R\)-module of finite projective
191	  dimension admits a \emph{minimal finite free resolution}: a resolution with each \(P_i\)
192	  free of finite rank and each transition map \(P_{i+1} \to P_i\) landing in \(\mathfrak{m} P_i\).
193	\end{definition}
194	
195	The two basic facts about \(\text{pd}_R(M)\) needed downstream are:
196	\begin{itemize}
197	  \item \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) iff \(M\) is projective (in the local Noetherian setting,
198	    iff \(M\) is free);
199	  \item if \(0 \to K \to P \to M \to 0\) is a short exact sequence with \(P\) projective, then
200	    \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_R(K) + 1\) provided \(K \neq 0\).
201	\end{itemize}
202	Both are standard homological algebra and are recorded under
203	\texttt{Module.projectiveDimension\_succ\_iff} or similar in Mathlib.
204	
205	\section{Depth on a short exact sequence}
206	\label{sec:ab_depth_ses}
207	
208	\begin{lemma}
209	
210	\leanok
211	  [Depth on a short exact sequence]
212	  \label{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
213	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_of_short_exact}
214	  
215	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses)
216	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17887-L17920).
217	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
218	  % "Let \(R\) be a local Noetherian ring. Let \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\)
219	  %  be a short exact sequence of nonzero finite \(R\)-modules.
220	  %  \begin{enumerate}
221	  %  \item
222	  %  \(\text{depth}(N) \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N'), \text{depth}(N'')\}\)
223	  %  \item
224	  %  \(\text{depth}(N'') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N') - 1\}\)
225	  %  \item
226	  %  \(\text{depth}(N') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N'') + 1\}\)
227	  %  \end{enumerate}"
228	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses).}
229	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let
230	  \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of nonzero finitely generated
231	  \(R\)-modules. Then
232	  \begin{enumerate}
233	    \item \(\text{depth}(N) \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N'),\; \text{depth}(N'')\bigr)\);
234	    \item \(\text{depth}(N'') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N') - 1\bigr)\);
235	    \item \(\text{depth}(N') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N'') + 1\bigr)\).
236	  \end{enumerate}
237	\end{lemma}
238	
239	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
240	% "Use the characterization of depth using the Ext groups
241	%  \(\Ext^i(\kappa, N)\), see Lemma REF,
242	%  and use the long exact cohomology sequence
243	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
244	%  from Lemma REF."
245	\begin{proof}
246	  
247	  
248	\leanok
249	  By Lemma~REF, \(\text{depth}(N')\) (resp.\ \(\text{depth}(N)\),
250	  \(\text{depth}(N'')\)) is the smallest index at which \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\) is
251	  nonzero on \(N'\) (resp.\ \(N\), \(N''\)). Apply the long exact \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\)
252	  sequence to \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\):
253	  \[
254	    \cdots \to \text{Ext}^{i-1}_R(\kappa, N'')
255	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N')
256	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N)
257	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N'')
258	    \to \text{Ext}^{i+1}_R(\kappa, N') \to \cdots
259	  \]
260	  Each of the three inequalities is then a direct read-off of the fact that if two
261	  of the three modules in a length-3 chunk vanish then the middle vanishes (for (1)) /
262	  the boundary terms vanish (for (2),(3)). Compare with the diagonal vanishing argument in
263	  the proof of Lemma~REF.
264	\end{proof}

266	\section{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula}
267	\label{sec:ab_main}
268	
269	\begin{theorem}
270	
271	\leanok
272	  [Auslander--Buchsbaum formula]
273	  \label{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}
274	  \lean{RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula}
275	  
276	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum)
277	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L27207-L27280).
278	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
279	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring. Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module
280	  %  which has finite projective dimension \(\text{pd}_R(M)\). Then we have
281	  %  \(\)
282	  %  \text{depth}(R) = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)
283	  %  \(\)"
284	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum); cf.\
285	  Matsumura, \emph{Commutative Ring Theory}, Theorem 19.1; Auslander--Buchsbaum,
286	  \emph{Homological dimension in local rings}, 1957.}
287	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely
288	  generated \(R\)-module of finite projective dimension. Then
289	  \[
290	    \text{pd}_R(M) \;+\; \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \text{depth}(R).
291	  \]
292	\end{theorem}
293	
294	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
295	% "We prove this by induction on \(\text{depth}(M)\). The most interesting
296	%  case is the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\). In this case, let
297	%  \(\)
298	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
299	%  \(\)
300	%  be a minimal finite free resolution, so \(e = \text{pd}_R(M)\).
301	%  By Lemma REF we may assume all matrix
302	%  coefficients of the maps in the complex are contained in the maximal
303	%  ideal of \(R\). Then on the one hand, by
304	%  Proposition REF we see that
305	%  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, breaking the long
306	%  exact sequence into short exact sequences
307	%  \begin{align*}
308	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e - 1}} \to K_{e - 2} \to 0,\\
309	%  0 \to K_{e - 2} \to R^{n_{e - 2}} \to K_{e - 3} \to 0,\\
310	%  \ldots,\\
311	%  0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
312	%  \end{align*}
313	%  we see, using Lemma REF, that
314	%  \begin{align*}
315	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1,\\
316	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2,\\
317	%  \ldots,\\
318	%  \text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e - 1),\\
319	%  \text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e
320	%  \end{align*}
321	%  and since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we conclude \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\).
322	%  This finishes the proof of the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).
323	%
324	%  Induction step. If \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), then we pick \(x \in \mathfrak m\)
325	%  which is a nonzerodivisor on both \(M\) and \(R\). This is possible, because
326	%  either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\) and \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the aforementioned
327	%  Proposition REF or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which
328	%  case \(M\) is finite free hence also \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\).
329	%  Thus \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by Lemma REF
330	%  (for example) and we can find an \(x \in \mathfrak m\) which is a nonzerodivisor
331	%  on both \(R\) and \(M\). Let
332	%  \(\)
333	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
334	%  \(\)
335	%  be a minimal resolution as above. An application of the snake lemma
336	%  shows that
337	%  \(\)
338	%  0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to
339	%  M/xM \to 0
340	%  \(\)
341	%  is a minimal resolution too. Thus \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\).
342	%  By Lemma REF we have
343	%  \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
344	%  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\).
345	%  Till now depths have all been depths as \(R\) modules, but we observe that
346	%  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) and similarly for \(R/xR\).
347	%  By induction hypothesis we see that the
348	%  Auslander-Buchsbaum formula holds for \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\). Since the
349	%  depths of both \(R/xR\) and \(M/xM\) have decreased by one and the projective
350	%  dimension has not changed we conclude."
351	\begin{proof}
352	  
353	  We induct on \(\text{depth}(M)\).
354	
355	  \emph{Base case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).} Let \(e := \text{pd}_R(M)\) and pick a minimal
356	  finite free resolution
357	  \[
358	    0 \longrightarrow R^{n_e} \longrightarrow R^{n_{e-1}} \longrightarrow
359	    \cdots \longrightarrow R^{n_0} \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
360	  \]
361	  with all transition maps having matrix coefficients in \(\mathfrak{m}\) (this is the
362	  ``minimal-resolution-after-trimming-trivial-summands'' normalisation, cf.\ Stacks
363	  \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}). On the one hand, the ``what is exact'' criterion
364	  (Stacks tag 00MF, \texttt{proposition-what-exact}, expressing exactness of a sequence of
365	  finite free modules in terms of depths of ideals of \(r\)-minors) gives
366	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, splitting the resolution into short exact
367	  sequences
368	  \[
369	    0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to K_{e-2} \to 0, \quad
370	    0 \to K_{e-2} \to R^{n_{e-2}} \to K_{e-3} \to 0, \quad \dots, \quad
371	    0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0,
372	  \]
373	  and applying Lemma~REF iteratively,
374	  yields the chain of inequalities
375	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1\),
376	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2\), \dots,
377	  \(\text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e-1)\), and finally
378	  \(\text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e\). Since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we deduce
379	  \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\). Combined with the previous inequality, $\text{depth}(R) = e =
380	  \text{pd}_R(M) + 0 = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)$.
381	
382	  \emph{Inductive step \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\).} We claim there exists \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\)
383	  which is a non-zero-divisor on both \(R\) and \(M\). Indeed: either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\), in
384	  which case \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the ``what is exact'' criterion applied to the start
385	  of a minimal resolution, or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which case \(M\) is finite free and
386	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\). Either way both \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) and
387	  \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), hence \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by
388	  Lemma~REF, so a common non-zero-divisor $x \in
389	  \mathfrak{m}\( exists. Take a minimal finite free resolution of \)M$ as above; the snake
390	  lemma applied to multiplication by \(x\) gives a minimal finite free resolution
391	  \[
392	    0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \cdots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to M/xM \to 0
393	  \]
394	  of \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\), of the same length \(e\). So
395	  \(\text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)\). By Stacks
396	  \texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}, both \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
397	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\), and one verifies
398	  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) (regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}\)
399	  correspond to regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}/(x)\)). Apply the inductive hypothesis to
400	  \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\):
401	  \[
402	    \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) + \text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(R/xR).
403	  \]
404	  Substituting yields
405	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + (\text{depth}(M) - 1) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\), i.e.\
406	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) = \text{depth}(R)\), as required.
407	\end{proof}

409	\section{Corollary: regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay}
410	\label{sec:ab_regular_cm}
411	
412	\begin{definition}
413	
414	\leanok
415	  [Cohen--Macaulay local ring]
416	  \label{def:cohen_macaulay_local}
417	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay}
418	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM)
419	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25288-L25299).
420	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
421	  % "A Noetherian local ring \(R\) is called {\it Cohen-Macaulay}
422	  %  if it is Cohen-Macaulay as a module over itself."
423	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM).}
424	  A Noetherian local ring \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is called \emph{Cohen--Macaulay} if
425	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\) (equivalently, if \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay as a module
426	  over itself, where Cohen--Macaulay for a finite \(R\)-module \(M\) means
427	  \(\dim(\text{Supp}(M)) = \text{depth}(M)\), cf.\ Stacks tag 00N3).
428	\end{definition}
429	
430	% NOTE (iter-185 review, supersedes iter-184): the Lean assembly
431	% `CohenMacaulay.of_regular` calls a non-private helper
432	% `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` (AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean
433	% L1096 — line drifted from iter-184's L944 reference) that now carries a
434	% structural induction scaffold via `regularLocal_inductive_step`, with the
435	% load-bearing sorry pushed into two named helpers: (i)
436	% `exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ` consolidating the
437	% Stacks 00NQ + 00NU substrate gaps in one signature, and (ii) an inline
438	% technical-bridge sorry inside `regularLocal_inductive_step` for the
439	% `R⧸(x)`-vs-`QuotSMulTop x R` linear-equiv upgrade (~10-20 LOC iter-186 close
440	% via `Submodule.ideal_span_singleton_smul` + `QuotSMulTop.mem_annihilator`).
441	% Neither has a `\lean{...}` pin in this chapter (the lean-vs-blueprint-checker
442	% iter185-auslander minor flagged adding a pin for the public-facing
443	% `exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` itself; iter-186+ chapter touch can add
444	% that hint). The Stacks 00NQ Mathlib gap remains the load-bearing substrate;
445	% iter-186+ work paths unchanged: (a) formalise Stacks 00NQ in project
446	% (~300 LOC); (b) Koszul-homology bypass via `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index`;
447	% (c) Mathlib upstream.
448	\begin{corollary}
449	
450	\leanok
451	  [Regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay]
452	  \label{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
453	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular}
454	  
455	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM)
456	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25714-L25730).
457	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
458	  % "Let \(R\) be a regular local ring and let
459	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) be a minimal set of generators
460	  %  for the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\). Then
461	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) is a regular sequence, and
462	  %  each \(R/(x_1, \ldots, x_c)\) is a regular local ring
463	  %  of dimension \(d - c\). In particular \(R\) is Cohen-Macaulay."
464	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM).}
465	  Every regular Noetherian local ring is Cohen--Macaulay: if \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is
466	  regular local of Krull dimension \(d\), then \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\).
467	\end{corollary}
468	
469	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
470	% "Note that \(R/x_1R\) is a Noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\)
471	%  by Lemma REF with \(x_2, \ldots, x_d\)
472	%  generating the maximal ideal. Hence it is a regular local ring by definition.
473	%  Since \(R\) is a domain by Lemma REF
474	%  \(x_1\) is a nonzerodivisor."
475	\begin{proof}
476	  
477	  
478	\leanok
479	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be regular of dimension \(d\) and pick a minimal generating set
480	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) of \(\mathfrak{m}\). By Stacks tag 00NQ (lemma-regular-domain), \(R\) is
481	  an integral domain, so \(x_1\) is a non-zero-divisor. The quotient \(R/x_1 R\) is a Noetherian
482	  local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\) (by Krull's principal ideal theorem,
483	  \texttt{lemma-one-equation}) with maximal ideal generated by \(x_2, \dots, x_d\), hence
484	  has dimension exactly \(d - 1\) and is again regular of dimension \(d - 1\). Inducting on \(d\),
485	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) is an \(R\)-regular sequence, so
486	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq d\). The reverse inequality \(\text{depth}(R) \leq \dim(R) = d\)
487	  always holds for any nonzero finite module over a Noetherian local ring
488	  (Stacks tag 00LK, \texttt{lemma-bound-depth}). Thus \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\), i.e.\
489	  \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay.
490	
491	  \emph{Remark on the Auslander--Buchsbaum route.} The same conclusion can be obtained from
492	  Theorem~REF as follows: take \(M := R\) itself. Then \(M\) is
493	  finitely generated and projective (free of rank \(1\)), so \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\). The
494	  formula gives \(\text{depth}(R) = 0 + \text{depth}(R)\), a tautology. The non-tautological
495	  use of Auslander--Buchsbaum is rather: for a regular local ring of dimension \(d\), every
496	  finitely generated module \(M\) has \(\text{pd}_R(M) \leq d\) (Stacks tag 00O2,
497	  \texttt{proposition-finite-gl-dim-regular}); applying the formula to such an \(M\) then
498	  relates \(\text{depth}(M)\) to \(d - \text{pd}_R(M)\), which is the form most often used in
499	  practice. The direct regular-sequence argument above is the cleanest proof of
500	  \(R\) Cohen--Macaulay itself.
501	\end{proof}
502	
503	\section{Application: codim-1 extension on a regular surface (gating A.4.a)}
504	\label{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}
505	
506	The downstream consumer is A.4.a: extending a rational map
507	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from a regular projective surface \(S\) to an abelian variety \(A\)
508	across a closed point \(x \in S\) that is missing from the domain of definition \(U \subseteq S\).
509	The classical mechanism (cf.\ Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, III.7) is:
510	
511	\begin{enumerate}
512	  \item The local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) is a regular Noetherian local ring of Krull
513	    dimension \(2\) (because \(S\) is regular and \(\dim S = 2\)).
514	  \item By Corollary~REF it is Cohen--Macaulay, so
515	    \(\text{depth}(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}) = 2\). Equivalently, the maximal ideal contains an
516	    \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\)-regular sequence of length \(2\) (any two transverse generators).
517	  \item Sections of the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_S\) on \(U = S \setminus \{x\}\) extend
518	    uniquely across \(x\) because \(H^0_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) and
519	    \(H^1_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) (these local-cohomology vanishings are equivalent to depth
520	    \(\geq 2\) at \(x\) by Stacks tag 0AVF; see Hartshorne, III.3.5, for the surface case).
521	  \item For a rational map \(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) with codimension-\(\geq 2\) indeterminacy
522	    locus on a regular surface, the same depth-\(\geq 2\) input produces a sheaf-theoretic
523	    extension of \(f\) to all of \(S\): pull back any sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_A\)-affine-locally,
524	    obtain sections over \(U\), and extend across \(x\) by the above.
525	\end{enumerate}
526	
527	So Corollary~REF is the gating input: the geometric A.4.a
528	proof can be written conditional on the algebraic Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary, and the
529	two halves develop independently --- as long as Theorem~REF and
530	Corollary~REF are formalized in
531	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} with the signatures pinned
532	in this chapter, A.4.a can consume them as Mathlib-style imports.
533

# Strategy

## Goal

Formalize Christian Merten's Jacobian challenge (`references/challenge.lean`):
nine protected declarations headlined by `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian` and
`Jacobian.nonempty_jacobianWitness` — existence of an Albanese / Jacobian
object uniform over the `k`-rational pointing of a smooth proper
geometrically irreducible curve `C / k`, with **no** `C(k) ≠ ∅` hypothesis.
End-state: zero inline `sorry`, kernel-only axioms (`propext`,
`Classical.choice`, `Quot.sound`). **Char-general:** the protected
signatures take `[Field k]` only — NO `CharZero`.

The witness OBJECT `J` is always real (constructed unconditionally); only
`isAlbaneseFor` is universally quantified over `P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C`. Spine:
**pointed vs. unpointed**. `genus C := \dim_k H^1(C, O_C)` (arithmetic
genus; protected — cannot be re-typed).

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
33	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
34	(\cref{def:genus}). By
35	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
36	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
37	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
38	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
39	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
40	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
41	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
42	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
43	degree of a line bundle.
44	
45	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
46	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
47	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
48	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
49	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
50	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.
51	
52	\section{The identity component of a group scheme}
53	\label{sec:identity_component_abstract}
54	
55	The identity component is an abstract feature of group schemes locally
56	of finite type over a field. We record the definition and its
57	open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure separately, so that the
58	Lean substrate
59	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} is reusable
60	outside the Picard context.
61	
62	\begin{definition}\leanok
63	[Identity component of a group scheme]
64	  \label{def:identity_component_group_scheme}
65	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
66	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps;
67	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
68	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2851-L2863).
69	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
70	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lem:agps}
71	  %  Let \(k\) be a field, and \(G\) a group scheme locally of finite
72	  % type.  Let \(G^0\) denote the connected component of the identity element \(e\).
73	  %
74	  % \tu{(1)} Then \(G\) is separated.
75	  %
76	  % \tu{(2)} Then \(G\) is smooth if it has a geometrically
77	  % reduced open subscheme.

81	  % extending \(k\).

83	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps.}
84	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type
85	  whose identity section \(e : \Spec k \to G\) factors through \(G\). The
86	  \emph{identity component} of \(G\), denoted \(G^0\), is the locally closed
87	  subscheme of \(G\) whose underlying topological space is the connected
88	  component of \(|G|\) containing the image of \(e\), equipped with the
89	  reduced-induced (a priori) subscheme structure inherited from \(G\).
90	  In characteristic zero, or more generally when \(G\) has a
91	  geometrically reduced open subscheme, the substructure agrees with
92	  the open subscheme structure of \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
93	\end{definition}

117	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
