1312	\begin{definition}
1313	\leanok
1314	  
1315	[The chart-\(i\) scheme morphism for \(\sigma_\times\)]
1316	  \label{def:gmscaling_chart}
1317	  
1318	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gmScalingP1_chart}
1319	  % Archon-original Lean scaffold for the iter-171 chart-glue body of \(\sigma_\times\)
1320	  % (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}). No external % SOURCE QUOTE.
1321	  For each chart index \(i \in \{0, 1\} = \mathrm{Fin}\,2\), the chart-\(i\) scheme morphism
1322	  \[ \mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart}\,\bar k\,i \colon
1323	     (\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover}\,\bar k).X\,i
1324	     \longrightarrow \mathtt{ProjectiveLineBarScheme}\,\bar k \]
1325	  realising \(\sigma_\times = \mathtt{gmScalingP1}\) (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}) on the \(i\)-th
1326	  chart of the pullback cover \(\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover}\) (\cref{def:gmscaling_cover}). On
1327	  chart \(1\) (target affine chart \(D_+(X_1)\), coordinate \(u = X_0/X_1\)) the morphism realises
1328	  \((x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x\) as the ring-level map \(u \mapsto u \otimes \lambda\) in
1329	  \(\mathrm{Localization.Away}\,t \otimes_{\bar k} \mathtt{GmRing}\,\bar k\) (here \(t\) is the
1330	  \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\,\{*\}\,\bar k\) generator); on chart \(0\) (target affine chart
1331	  \(D_+(X_0)\), coordinate \(t = X_1/X_0\)) it realises \(1/(\lambda x) = u/\lambda\) as the ring-level
1332	  map \(t \mapsto t \otimes \lambda^{-1}\). The two chart-side ring maps are packaged in Lean as
1333	  \(\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart0\_ringMap}\) and \(\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart1\_ringMap}\) (both
1334	  axiom-clean iter-171).
1335	  \medskip
1336	  \noindent\textbf{Construction recipe.} The scheme morphism is assembled from the chart-side
1337	  ring map by the standard ``ring map \(\Rightarrow\) scheme map between \(\Spec\)'s of relative tensor
1338	  products'' bridge, applied to the affine carrier of each cover chart. Concretely the chain is
1339	  \(\mathtt{pullbackSpecIso}\) (identifying \((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_i\) with \(\Spec\) of an
1340	  \(\bar k\)-tensor product of the chart-\(i\) chart ring with \(\mathtt{GmRing}\)), followed by
1341	  \(\Spec.\mathrm{map}\) of the ring map above, followed by the chart-ring iso of
1342	  \cref{def:proj_chart_ring_iso} (which identifies \(\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i\) with the
1343	  affine chart ring \(\bar k[u]\) at the \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\,\{*\}\,\bar k\)-level), and then
1344	  \(\mathrm{Proj.away}\,\iota\) to land in \(\mathbb P^1.\mathrm{left}\). The substantive step that is
1345	  \emph{not} algebraic (the chart-ring iso \cref{def:proj_chart_ring_iso} is already axiom-clean
1346	  via the iter-172 \cref{lem:mvPoly_to_homogeneousLocalization_away_surjective}) is the
1347	  source-side \(\mathtt{pullbackSpecIso}\) identification, which navigates the abstract
1348	  $\mathtt{pullback}\,(\mathtt{pullback.fst}\,\mathbb P^1.\mathrm{hom}\,\mathbb G_m.\mathrm{hom})\,
1349	  (\mathrm{Proj.away}\,\iota\,\mathcal A\,X_i)\( into the \)\Spec$-of-tensor-product form. The full
1350	  recipe is recorded in the iter-173 analogist note \(\mathtt{analogies/chart{\text-}bridge.md}\)
1351	  (in flight), and the prior 6-step decomposition in \(\mathtt{analogies/gmscaling{\text-}deep.md}\).
1352	\end{definition}

1356	[Cocycle agreement for \(\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart}\) on pairwise overlaps]
1357	  \label{lem:gmscaling_chart_agreement}
1358	  
1359	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gmScalingP1_chart_agreement}
1360	  % Archon-original Lean scaffold for the iter-171 chart-glue body of \(\sigma_\times\)
1361	  % (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}). No external % SOURCE QUOTE.
1362	  For every pair of chart indices \(x, y \in \mathrm{Fin}\,2\) the two restrictions of
1363	  \(\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart}\,\bar k\) (\cref{def:gmscaling_chart}) to the pairwise pullback
1364	  \(\mathrm{pullback}\,((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_x.f)\,((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_y.f)\) agree:
1365	  \[ \mathrm{pullback.fst}\,((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_x.f)\,((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_y.f)
1366	     \fatsemi \mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart}\,\bar k\,x
1367	     \ =\
1368	     \mathrm{pullback.snd}\,((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_x.f)\,((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_y.f)
1369	     \fatsemi \mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart}\,\bar k\,y. \]
1370	  This is the cocycle / overlap-coherence equation that \(\mathtt{Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms}\)
1371	  consumes in order to assemble the chart-wise pieces \(\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart}\,\bar k\,i\) into
1372	  a single morphism on the total \((\mathbb P^1 \otimes \mathbb G_m).\mathrm{left}\). The diagonal
1373	  cases \((x, y) = (0, 0)\) and \((x, y) = (1, 1)\) are immediate via
1374	  \(\mathtt{pullback.condition}\); the substantive content is the cross case \((0, 1)\) (and its
1375	  symmetric \((1, 0)\)), broken down below.

1388	  % (`refine ≪≫ ?_; refine; exact`), so its elaboration does not display its
1389	  % `Iso.trans`-spine syntactically; the canonical Mathlib simp chain

1393	  \noindent\textbf{(I) Setup and structural lift.} Restricting the indices to the cross case
1394	  \((x, y) = (0, 1)\), the two charts being compared are
1395	  \(\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart}\,\bar k\,0\) over the cover patch
1396	  \((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover}\,\bar k).X\,0 = D_+(X_0) \times \mathbb G_m\) (containing
1397	  \(0 \in \mathbb A^1\)) and \(\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart}\,\bar k\,1\) over
1398	  \((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover}\,\bar k).X\,1 = D_+(X_1) \times \mathbb G_m\) (containing
1399	  \(\infty\)). Their pairwise pullback intersection sits over the basic-open intersection
1400	  \(D_+(X_0 \cdot X_1) \times \mathbb G_m\), and the project records an explicit iso
1401	  identifying the intersection scheme with an affine \(\Spec\):
1402	  \[ \mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover\_intersection\_X\_iso}\,\bar k \colon
1403	     \mathrm{pullback}\,((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover}\,\bar k)_0.f)\,
1404	       ((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover}\,\bar k)_1.f)
1405	     \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1406	     \Spec\bigl(\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,(X_0 \cdot X_1) \otimes_{\bar k}
1407	       \mathtt{GmRing}\,\bar k\bigr). \]

1409	  inverse and cancel the resulting epimorphism --- the

1412	  \(\Spec\bigl(\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,(X_0 \cdot X_1) \otimes_{\bar k}
1413	     \mathtt{GmRing}\,\bar k\bigr) \rightrightarrows \overline{\mathbb P^1}\), where both

1416	  ring maps into \(\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,(X_0 \cdot X_1) \otimes_{\bar k}
1417	  \mathtt{GmRing}\,\bar k\).

1510	      The chart-agreement equation
1511	      \(\mathrm{pullback.fst} \fatsemi \mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart}\,\bar k\,0 =
1512	      \mathrm{pullback.snd} \fatsemi \mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart}\,\bar k\,1\)
1513	      on the cover intersection
1514	      \(\mathrm{pullback}\,((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_0.f)\,
1515	      ((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_1.f)\) is the statement that two morphisms
1516	      \(\mathrm{chart}_0, \mathrm{chart}_1 \colon \mathrm{(intersection)} \to
1517	      \overline{\mathbb P^1}\) agree on that intersection scheme. \emph{The key
1518	      observation is that \(\overline{\mathbb P^1}\) is separated over \(\bar k\)},
1519	      so the diagonal
1520	      \(\Delta_{\overline{\mathbb P^1}} \colon
1521	        \overline{\mathbb P^1} \longrightarrow
1522	        \overline{\mathbb P^1} \times_{\bar k} \overline{\mathbb P^1}\)
1523	      is a closed immersion. Because \(\overline{\mathbb P^1} = \Proj(\bar k[X_0, X_1])\)
1524	      is the \(\Proj\) of a graded \(\bar k\)-algebra, its separatedness over \(\Spec
1525	      \bar k\) is the standard Stacks fact ``\(\Proj(S)\) is separated'' (Stacks tag
1526	      \texttt{01KU} / lemma \texttt{lemma-proj-separated}); Mathlib supplies the
1527	      categorical packaging via \(\mathtt{IsSeparated}\) and
1528	      \(\mathtt{IsSeparated.diagonal\_isClosedImmersion}\).
1529	      \emph{Bypass.} Reformulate the cocycle as: the pair-morphism
1530	      \((\mathrm{chart}_0, \mathrm{chart}_1) \colon \mathrm{(intersection)}
1531	      \longrightarrow \overline{\mathbb P^1} \times_{\bar k} \overline{\mathbb P^1}\)
1532	      (built via \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Limits.prod.lift}\) on
1533	      \(\mathrm{chart}_0 := \mathrm{pullback.fst} \fatsemi \mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart}\,
1534	      \bar k\,0\) and
1535	      \(\mathrm{chart}_1 := \mathrm{pullback.snd} \fatsemi \mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart}\,
1536	      \bar k\,1\)) \emph{factors through the diagonal
1537	      \(\Delta_{\overline{\mathbb P^1}}\)}. By Mathlib's
1538	      \(\mathtt{IsClosedImmersion.lift\_iff\_range\_subset}\) (the universal property
1539	      of a closed immersion: a morphism factors through a closed immersion iff its
1540	      set-theoretic image is contained in the image of the immersion), the
1541	      factorization is equivalent to the set-level containment of the cocycle's image
1542	      in the image of \(\Delta\), which on points is exactly the chart equality on
1543	      the \emph{closed points} of the intersection. The closed-point check is
1544	      discrete (each closed point of the intersection lies over a single \(\bar k\)-
1545	      point of \(D_+(X_0 \cdot X_1) \times \mathbb G_m\) and the two chart maps both
1546	      evaluate to the same \(\bar k\)-point of \(\overline{\mathbb P^1}\)
1547	      corresponding to that \((x, \lambda)\)), and bypasses the simp coverage gap
1548	      that obstructs (III.a)/(III.b) entirely.
1549	      \emph{Concrete prover recipe.} The path closes in four steps:
1550	      \begin{enumerate}
1551	        \item Fix the diagonal
1552	          \(\Delta_{\overline{\mathbb P^1}} \colon \overline{\mathbb P^1}
1553	            \longrightarrow \overline{\mathbb P^1} \times_{\bar k}
1554	            \overline{\mathbb P^1}\)
1555	          via Mathlib's \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Limits.prod.lift}\,(\mathbf
1556	          1_{\overline{\mathbb P^1}})\,(\mathbf 1_{\overline{\mathbb P^1}})\), and
1557	          obtain the closed-immersion witness from
1558	          \(\mathtt{IsSeparated.diagonal\_isClosedImmersion}\) applied to the
1559	          separated structure morphism
1560	          \(\overline{\mathbb P^1}.\mathrm{hom} \colon \overline{\mathbb P^1} \to
1561	          \Spec \bar k\).
1562	        \item Build the pair-morphism
1563	          \((\mathrm{chart}_0, \mathrm{chart}_1) \colon
1564	            \mathrm{pullback}\,((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_0.f)\,
1565	              ((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_1.f)
1566	            \longrightarrow
1567	            \overline{\mathbb P^1} \times_{\bar k} \overline{\mathbb P^1}\)
1568	          via \(\mathtt{prod.lift}\) on the two named chart restrictions above.
1569	        \item Show this pair factors through \(\Delta_{\overline{\mathbb P^1}}\)
1570	          via the universal property of the intersection's pullback structure
1571	          combined with \(\mathtt{IsClosedImmersion.lift}\) (kernel-inequality
1572	          form; the \(\mathtt{\_iff\_range\_subset}\) variant is NOT in Mathlib
1573	          at \texttt{b80f227} per iter-188 verification, so this step requires
1574	          either upstream contribution or a project-side bridge plus
1575	          \(\mathtt{IsReduced}\) on the intersection): produce the section
1576	          \(\mathrm{(intersection)} \to \overline{\mathbb P^1}\) whose post-
1577	          composition with \(\Delta\) equals \((\mathrm{chart}_0,
1578	          \mathrm{chart}_1)\).
1579	        \item The factorization reduces, by composing with the two projections
1580	          \(\mathtt{prod.fst}\) and \(\mathtt{prod.snd}\) out of the codomain
1581	          \(\overline{\mathbb P^1} \times_{\bar k} \overline{\mathbb P^1}\), to
1582	          two scheme-equalities on the components --- each of which is a direct
1583	          chart-bridge equality on \((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_i\)'s structure
1584	          (i.e.\ the \emph{single-chart} content, not the full cocycle), discharged
1585	          using the same chart-ring iso \cref{def:proj_chart_ring_iso} and
1586	          \(\mathtt{pullbackSpecIso}\) bridge that \cref{def:gmscaling_chart} uses.
1587	      \end{enumerate}
1588	      \emph{Substrate hooks.} The categorical substrate is present at the

1	\chapter{The relative Picard functor and its \'etale sheafification}
2	\label{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.1.c sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It upgrades the set-valued
7	% relative Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) of
8	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (A.1.b) to its abelian-group-valued
9	% refinement and then to its \'etale sheafification
10	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\). The chapter is gated on
11	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (A.1.b, pullback functor + set-valued
12	% quotient on disk) and on \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} (A.1.a, the
13	% relative-spectrum machinery underlying any concrete Lean-side computation).
14	% It \emph{gates} A.2.c (\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) since
15	% Grothendieck's existence theorem represents precisely the \'etale-sheafified
16	% functor (cf.\ [Kleiman], \S 4, th:main: ``\dots represents
17	% \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\).''). It is \emph{not} responsible for representability
18	% (that is A.2.c) and not responsible for the identity-component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
19	% (that is A.3).
20	
21	% NOTE (planner directive iter-174). Per the iter-174 plan, the chapter has
22	% two responsibilities. First, package the abelian-group structure on the
23	% quotient \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) that the set-valued chapter
24	% A.1.b deliberately suppressed. The structure is canonical: \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\)
25	% is abelian under tensor product, the image \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup
26	% because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism, and the quotient is abelian. Second,
27	% \emph{sheafify} the resulting group-valued presheaf
28	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) in the \'etale topology; the
29	% sheafification is the functor Kleiman \S 4 represents. The \'etale-sheaf
30	% refinement is necessary because, by Kleiman \S 2, \(\Pic_X\) is never a Zariski
31	% (let alone \'etale) sheaf; the smallest \'etale sheaf below \(\Pic_X\) is the
32	% one that captures line bundles that are trivializable \emph{\'etale-locally},
33	% which is the right notion for Grothendieck--Mumford--Kleiman representability.
34	
35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
37	
38	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
39	\(k\). As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	\(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	\(k\)-scheme \(T\) and
42	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~REF
52	and \S~REF) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) on the category of \(k\)-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~REF) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\) in the \'etale topology on
58	\((\Sch/k)^{op}\), and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
61	
62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
73	
74	\begin{lemma}\leanok
75	[Group structure descends to the relative Picard quotient]
76	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
78	  
79	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
80	  % Definition df:aPf + Definition df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard
81	  % functors are \emph{abelian-group}-valued, by construction; the relative
82	  % Picard functor is defined as a quotient \emph{group})
83	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
84	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
85	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
86	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
87	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
88	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
89	  %  [\dots]
90	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
91	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
92	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
93	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
94	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the Picard functors are abelian-group-valued, and the
95	  relative one is a quotient of abelian groups; cf.\ Stacks tag 01CR for the
96	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic\)).}
97	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve and let \(T\) be a
98	  \(k\)-scheme. The Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is an abelian group under
99	  tensor product of line bundles, and the pullback map
100	  \[
101	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
102	  \]
103	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a group homomorphism. Its image
104	  \[
105	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T)
106	  \]
107	  is therefore a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\), and the quotient set of
108	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
109	  \[
110	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T
111	  \]
112	  inherits a canonical abelian-group structure under which the quotient map
113	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjective
114	  homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
115	\end{lemma}
116	
117	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not supply a separate "proof" block
118	% for df:Pfs; the abelian-group structure is part of the definition (the word
119	% \emph{group} appears in df:aPf already, and df:Pfs is a quotient of the same
120	% abelian groups). The verbatim quotes for the statements above are therefore
121	% the same as for the lemma. The project-side proof is a structural restatement.
122	\begin{proof}
123	  
124	  The abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) under tensor product is
125	  standard (Stacks tag 01CR or Hartshorne II~\S 6): for line bundles
126	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}'\), the tensor product
127	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\) is again
128	  invertible, the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) is the unit, and
129	  the dual \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the
130	  inverse. Pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules sends
131	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) (the structure sheaf is
132	  preserved by inverse image) and satisfies
133	  $\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} \mathcal{N}') \cong
134	   \pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \pi_T^*\mathcal{N}'$
135	  by the standard tensor compatibility of pullback. Therefore \(\pi_T^*\)
136	  preserves the unit, sends inverses to inverses, and is multiplicative on
137	  tensor products; it is a homomorphism of abelian groups.
138	
139	  The image \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is then a subgroup. The quotient set
140	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) of
141	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries the canonical quotient
142	  abelian-group structure (Lagrange's theorem on cosets specialised to an
143	  abelian quotient): for \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}' \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\), set
144	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{L}'] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}']\);
145	  well-definedness follows from the subgroup condition. The canonical map
146	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is by construction a
147	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
148	\end{proof}
149	
150	\section{The relative Picard presheaf as a group-valued presheaf}
151	\label{sec:relpic_groupvalued}
152	
153	We now refine the set-valued functor of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} into a
154	group-valued presheaf.
155	
156	\begin{definition}\leanok
157	[Relative Picard presheaf]
158	  \label{def:rel_pic_sharp}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp}
160	  
161	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
162	  % Definition df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor)
163	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
164	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
165	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
166	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
167	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
168	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
169	  % topologies  by
170	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
171	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
172	  %  \end{dfn}"
173	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
174	  Def.~df:Pfs.}
175	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. The
176	  \emph{relative Picard presheaf} of \(C\) over \(k\) is the functor
177	  \[
178	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \Ab,
179	    \qquad
180	    T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T),
181	  \]
182	  where the right-hand side is the quotient abelian group of
183	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, and the structure morphisms are induced by
184	  the pullback maps \(g^\sharp\) of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} (which restrict
185	  to group homomorphisms on the quotient because the source square commutes
186	  with all pullback functors, see the proof below). In Kleiman's notation with
187	  \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\), this is exactly \(\Pic_{X/S}\) of df:Pfs.
188	\end{definition}
189	
190	\begin{lemma}\leanok
191	[Naturality respects the group structure]
192	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}
193	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.functorial}
194	  
195	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
196	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is functorial
197	  % into abelian groups; the word \emph{functor} into the category of abelian
198	  % groups encodes both the identity / composition laws and the
199	  % group-homomorphism property of the restriction maps)
200	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
201	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
202	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
203	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
204	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
205	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
206	  %  [\dots]
207	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
208	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
209	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
210	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
211	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
212	  groups; the structure maps are group homomorphisms).}
213	  For a morphism \(g : T' \to T\) of \(k\)-schemes, the induced set map
214	  \(g^\sharp : \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T')\) of
215	  \cref{thm:pullback_natural} is a homomorphism of abelian groups with respect
216	  to the structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, and the identity and
217	  composition laws \(\mathrm{id}^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) and
218	  \((g \circ h)^\sharp = h^\sharp \circ g^\sharp\) hold as identities of group
219	  homomorphisms.
220	\end{lemma}
221	
222	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not write out a separate proof for
223	% functoriality; the structural assertion is part of the definition. No
224	% verbatim proof body is available in the source; the project-side argument
225	% is a direct unfolding of the pullback of modules.
226	\begin{proof}
227	  
228	  By \cref{thm:pullback_natural}, the set map \(g^\sharp\) is defined by
229	  \([\mathcal{L}] \mapsto [g_C^* \mathcal{L}]\), where
230	  \(g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\). Pullback
231	  of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(g_C\) preserves tensor products and the
232	  structure sheaf, hence the map
233	  \(g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')\) on Picard groups is
234	  itself an abelian-group homomorphism. By
235	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, the quotient map
236	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a group homomorphism, and
237	  \(g_C^*\) takes \(H_T\) into \(H_{T'}\) by \cref{lem:pullback_compose}; therefore
238	  \(g^\sharp\) is a group homomorphism on the quotient. The identity and
239	  composition laws are inherited from \(\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}\) and
240	  \((g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*\) on \(\mathcal{O}\)-module pullback.
241	\end{proof}
242	
243	\begin{theorem}\leanok
244	[\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) is a group-valued presheaf]
245	  \label{thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf}
246	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.presheaf}
247	  
248	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
249	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (relative Picard functor as a functor into
250	  % abelian groups)
251	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
252	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
253	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
254	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
255	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
256	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
257	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
258	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.}
259	  The assignment \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) of \cref{def:rel_pic_sharp},
260	  together with the structure maps \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) of
261	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}, defines a presheaf of abelian groups
262	  \[
263	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\;
264	                                     \AddCommGroup
265	  \]
266	  on the category of \(k\)-schemes.
267	\end{theorem}
268	
269	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with the prior theorems, Kleiman \S 2 does not supply
270	% a separate proof body --- the presheaf-of-abelian-groups property is part of
271	% the definition df:aPf + df:Pfs. The project-side proof assembles the lemmas
272	% above; no external proof is being translated.
273	\begin{proof}
274	  
275	  The object map is well defined by
276	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}: for each \(k\)-scheme \(T\), the value
277	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is an abelian group. The morphism map
278	  \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) is a group homomorphism by
279	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}, and satisfies the identity and
280	  composition laws by the same lemma. These data assemble into a functor
281	  \((\Sch/k)^{op} \to \AddCommGroup\), i.e.\ a presheaf of abelian groups on
282	  \((\Sch/k)\).
283	\end{proof}
284	
285	\section{\'Etale sheafification}
286	\label{sec:relpic_etale_sheaf}
287	
288	The presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) is generally not an \'etale (or even Zariski)
289	sheaf. The standard obstruction (Kleiman \S 2, L1292--L1302): for a \(k\)-scheme
290	\(T\) carrying a non-trivial line bundle whose pullback to \(C \times_k T\) becomes
291	non-trivial after an open cover, the Zariski separated-presheaf condition
292	fails. Therefore, to obtain a representable functor in the sense of
293	\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}, one must replace
294	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) by its \emph{associated \'etale sheaf}. Kleiman \S 2 names
295	this sheaf \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) and singles it out as the functor that Kleiman's
296	main existence theorem \S 4 (th:main) represents.
297	
298	\begin{definition}\leanok
299	[\'Etale sheafification of the relative Picard presheaf]
300	  \label{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification}
301	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf}
302	  % NOTE (iter-176 review): the iter-176 Lane G prover renamed this declaration
303	  % from ``AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme`` to
304	  % ``AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf`` to avoid a name collision with
305	  % ``Picard_FGAPicRepresentability`` (which legitimately uses ``PicScheme``
306	  % for the REPRESENTING SCHEME, a different mathematical object). The
307	  % ``\lean{...}`` pin is updated accordingly. The §"Lean encoding" bullet 5
308	  % below is also corrected.
309	  
310	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
311	  % Definition df:Pfs --- the second half of df:Pfs names the \'etale sheaf
312	  % associated to the relative Picard functor
313	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318
314	  %  and the surrounding paragraph L1320-L1328).
315	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
316	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
317	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
318	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
319	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
320	  % topologies  by
321	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
322	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
323	  %  \end{dfn}
324	  %  [\dots]
325	  %  if the latter functor is one of the three just displayed,
326	  %  then it is a sheaf in the indicated topology; in fact, it is the sheaf
327	  %  associated to any one of its predecessors, and the map between them is
328	  %  the natural map from a presheaf to its associated sheaf, as is easy to
329	  %  check.  In particular, the three displayed sheaves are the sheaves
330	  %  associated to the absolute Picard functor \(\Pic_X\), as well as to the
331	  %  relative Picard functor \(\Pic_{X/S}\)."
332	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
333	  Def.~df:Pfs (the \'etale-sheaf notation \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\)) and the
334	  associated-sheaf paragraph immediately following df:Pfs.}
335	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. The
336	  \emph{\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor} is the sheafification of
337	  the presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf}) in the
338	  global \'etale topology on \((\Sch/k)\):
339	  \[
340	    \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \;\;:=\;\;
341	       \bigl(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\bigr)^{\sim_{\et}}.
342	  \]
343	  Equivalently, \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is the unique (up to canonical
344	  isomorphism) sheaf of abelian groups on the \'etale site of \(\Sch/k\)
345	  equipped with a presheaf morphism $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to
346	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ which is universal among presheaf morphisms from
347	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) to \'etale sheaves of abelian groups. In Kleiman's
348	  notation this is \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) with \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\). When \(k\)
349	  is algebraically closed and \(T = \Spec k'\) for an algebraically closed field
350	  extension \(k'/k\), the natural map
351	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) is a bijection (Kleiman
352	  Exercise ex:Alr, L1357--L1361).
353	\end{definition}
354	
355	\begin{theorem}[\'Etale sheafification preserves the abelian-group structure]
356	  \label{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure}
357	  
358	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
359	  % Def.~df:Pfs --- the \'etale-sheafified functor \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) is named
360	  % alongside the abelian-group-valued \(\Pic_{X/S}\) in the same definition,
361	  % i.e.\ the sheafification lives in the same target category
362	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
363	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
364	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
365	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
366	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
367	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
368	  % topologies  by
369	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
370	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
371	  %  \end{dfn}"
372	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
373	  Def.~df:Pfs (the \'etale sheaf \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) is named together with the
374	  abelian-group-valued relative Picard functor, meaning the sheaf is taken in
375	  the category of abelian groups).}
376	  The \'etale sheafification
377	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) of \cref{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification} is a
378	  sheaf with values in the category of abelian groups: the canonical morphism
379	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is a homomorphism of group
380	  presheaves, and for each \(k\)-scheme \(T\) the value
381	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) is naturally an abelian group whose structure
382	  maps make \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) a presheaf
383	  \((\Sch/k)^{op} \to \AddCommGroup\).
384	\end{theorem}
385	
386	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not supply a self-contained proof of
387	% this auxiliary statement --- it is the standard fact that the sheafification
388	% of a presheaf of abelian groups in a Grothendieck topology is again a
389	% presheaf of abelian groups (equivalently, the forgetful functor
390	% \(\AddCommGroup \to \Set\) preserves filtered colimits, and the
391	% plus-construction is built from such colimits). The Lean side appeals to
392	% Mathlib's \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sheafification}\) machinery.
393	\begin{proof}
394	  
395	  The \'etale topology on \((\Sch/k)\) is a Grothendieck topology, and the
396	  forgetful functor \(\AddCommGroup \to \Set\) preserves and reflects the
397	  filtered colimits used by the plus-construction. Therefore the
398	  sheafification of a presheaf \(\mathcal{F} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \AddCommGroup\)
399	  in the underlying \'etale site agrees, after the forgetful functor to sets,
400	  with the sheafification of the underlying set-valued presheaf
401	  \(U \circ \mathcal{F}\); equivalently, the sheaf
402	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} = (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^{\sim_\et}\) is canonically a
403	  sheaf of abelian groups, the canonical morphism
404	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is a homomorphism of group
405	  presheaves, and the restriction maps are group homomorphisms. This is a
406	  standard consequence of the plus-construction with values in an algebraic
407	  category; on the Lean side it is the Mathlib pattern
408	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sheafification}\) applied with target category
409	  \(\AddCommGroup\).
410	\end{proof}
411

1	\chapter{Milne Theorem 3.2: Rational maps into abelian varieties}
2	\label{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.c sub-row of Route A
6	% (the positive-genus arm of \texttt{nonempty\_jacobianWitness}). It is the
7	% join of the two preceding sub-rows: A.4.a, the codim-1 indeterminacy /
8	% codim-\(\geq 2\) extension package (\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}),
9	% and A.4.b, the Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary
10	% ``regular local \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay''
11	% (\cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}). The single output of this
12	% chapter is Milne's Theorem~3.2 of Chapter~I~\S 3 of \emph{Abelian Varieties}:
13	% \emph{every rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
14	% extends, uniquely, to a regular morphism}. This is the input that A.4.d
15	% (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}) consumes to upgrade the symmetric-product
16	% rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) to a regular morphism \(J \to A\),
17	% which in turn yields the Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
18	% (Milne III~\S 6, Proposition~6.1).
19	%
20	% This chapter is also the canonical home of the Lean target
21	% \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_to\_av}. A placeholder
22	% lemma block \texttt{lem:rational\_map\_to\_av\_extends} was reserved
23	% earlier in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} (under the alternative Lean
24	% name \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalMap\_to\_av\_extends}); a Plan-Agent
25	% reconciliation pass should retarget that reservation at the theorem proved
26	% here (see "Notes for Plan Agent" in the writer report).
27	%
28	% The Lean target file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean}
29	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
30	% creating it.
31	
32	\section{Setup and statement}
33	\label{sec:thm32_setup}
34	
35	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), a smooth variety \(X\) over \(\bar k\) (in the
36	project's convention: a geometrically integral, geometrically reduced, separated scheme of
37	finite type over \(\bar k\) that is moreover smooth, hence nonsingular at every closed point),
38	and an abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) (in particular \(A\) is complete, smooth, and a
39	group object in \(\mathrm{Sch}/\bar k\)). A rational map
40	\(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) is, in the project's notation, the equivalence class of a
41	regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to A\) defined on a dense open subset
42	\(U \subseteq X\), two such pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\) and \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) being equivalent
43	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\).
44	
45	The result of this chapter is:
46	
47	\begin{theorem}
48	
49	\leanok
50	[Milne Theorem~3.2: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends]
51	  \label{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}
52	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_to_av}
53	  
54	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, doc p.~17
55	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
56	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
57	  % "THEOREM 3.2. A rational map \(\alpha\colon V--\to A\) from a nonsingular
58	  %  variety to an abelian variety is defined on the whole of \(V\)."
59	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, p.~17.}
60	
61	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), let \(A\) be
62	  an abelian variety over \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) be a rational
63	  map. Then \(f\) is defined on the whole of \(X\): there is a unique regular morphism
64	  \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\) whose restriction to some (equivalently, every) dense open
65	  set of definition \(U \subseteq X\) of \(f\) agrees with \(\varphi_U\).
66	\end{theorem}
67	
68	The uniqueness clause is immediate from \(X\) being reduced and separated: two regular
69	morphisms \(X \to A\) that agree on a dense open subset agree everywhere. The substance is
70	existence of the extension. Milne deduces it in one line by combining the codim-\(\geq 2\)
71	extension result for rational maps to a complete variety with the codim-1 indeterminacy
72	structure result for rational maps to a group variety:
73	
74	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
75	% "PROOF. Combine Theorem 3.1 with the next lemma."
76	\begin{proof}
77	  
78	  Milne's one-line proof is: \emph{combine Theorem~3.1 with Lemma~3.3}.
79	  Theorem~3.1 (the codim-\(\geq 2\) extension theorem for a rational map from a normal
80	  variety to a complete variety; valuative criterion of properness) is
81	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} of the present project; Lemma~3.3 (the
82	  pure-codimension-\(1\) structure of the indeterminacy locus of a rational map into a
83	  group variety) is \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} of the same chapter.
84	
85	  Concretely: let \(U \subseteq X\) be a maximal open set of definition of \(f\), and let
86	  \(Z := X \setminus U\) be its complement.
87	  \begin{enumerate}
88	    \item By \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} (Milne~3.1, the valuative-criterion extension
89	      to codim \(\geq 2\)), applied with the normal variety \(X\) and the complete variety
90	      \(A\), we have \(\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2\).
91	    \item By \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} (Milne~3.3, the pure-codim-\(1\)
92	      indeterminacy structure for maps into the group variety \(A\)), the closed subset
93	      \(Z\) is either empty or of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X\).
94	    \item Both conditions together force \(Z = \emptyset\): a non-empty pure-codimension-\(1\)
95	      closed subset has codimension exactly \(1\), contradicting \(\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2\).
96	  \end{enumerate}
97	  Therefore \(U = X\) and \(f\) admits a regular representative on all of \(X\), which is the
98	  required regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\). Uniqueness is the standard
99	  reduced-and-separated agreement principle (see the paragraph preceding the theorem).
100	\end{proof}
101	
102	\begin{remark}
103	  \label{rmk:thm32_role_of_ab}
104	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} The combination above relies on
105	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}, which is itself proved in
106	  \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} in two stages: a codim-\(1\) step
107	  (valuative criterion of properness, applied at the DVR
108	  \(\mathcal O_{X, Z}\) of each codimension-\(1\) point \(Z\) --- works because \(X\) is normal
109	  and \(A\) is complete) and a codim-\(\geq 2\) step (depth-\(\geq 2\) at codim-\(\geq 2\) points,
110	  giving the local-cohomology / Hartshorne~III.8 sheaf-extension property). The
111	  depth-\(\geq 2\) input on a smooth (regular) variety is exactly what
112	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies:
113	  for a regular local ring \((\mathcal O_{X, x}, \mathfrak m_x)\) of Krull dimension \(d\), the
114	  Auslander--Buchsbaum formula \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} (applied to \(M = R\)
115	  trivially, or to the depth-via-regular-sequence characterisation directly) gives
116	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = d\), so in particular
117	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\) whenever \(\mathrm{codim}_X(\{x\}) \geq 2\).
118	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
119	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), every closed point \(x\) is regular of
120	  dimension \(2\), so \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(2\); sections of
121	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(U = X \setminus \{x\}\) extend uniquely across \(x\), and the same is
122	  true for affine-locally pulled-back sections of \(\mathcal O_A\) along \(f\).
123	
124	  Auslander--Buchsbaum therefore does not appear by name in the proof above; it appears
125	  inside \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s codim-\(\geq 2\) half. The dependency
126	  \(\) is recorded on
127	  \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} to keep the leanblueprint dependency graph
128	  faithful: A.4.c truly does sit on top of A.4.b through A.4.a.
129	\end{remark}
130	
131	\section{Why the proof is char-free}
132	\label{sec:thm32_char_free}
133	
134	Each of the two inputs is char-free:
135	\begin{itemize}
136	  \item \emph{Theorem~3.1 / codim-\(\geq 2\) extension} (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}):
137	    the proof uses only the valuative criterion of properness and depth-\(\geq 2\) extension
138	    of sections on regular local rings; both are characteristic-agnostic.
139	  \item \emph{Lemma~3.3 / pure-codim-\(1\) indeterminacy for maps into a group variety}
140	    (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}): Milne's proof forms the difference map
141	    \(\Phi(x, y) := \varphi(x)\,\varphi(y)^{-1} \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow A\) and
142	    examines its zero/pole divisor along the diagonal --- a Weil-divisor argument that
143	    works for any algebraically closed base field. The argument uses only the group law
144	    on \(A\) (its smoothness as a group object), the regularity of \(X\) (so divisors and
145	    DVRs at codim-\(1\) points are available), and the existence of a divisor of zeros and
146	    poles for a nonzero rational function on a nonsingular variety. None of these inputs
147	    has a characteristic restriction.
148	\end{itemize}
149	Consequently \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} holds over every algebraically closed
150	field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states this proof verbatim in characteristic-free
151	generality at \S I.3, pp.~16--17.)
152	
153	\section{Application: the symmetric-product Albanese map (gating A.4.d)}
154	\label{sec:thm32_application_to_a4d}
155	
156	The downstream consumer is A.4.d (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}):
157	the Albanese universal property of the Jacobian \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\). Milne's proof of
158	Proposition~6.1 of \S III.6 (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~104) constructs the unique
159	morphism \(\psi \colon J \to A\) factoring a given pointed regular morphism
160	\(\varphi \colon C \to A\) through the canonical \(f_P \colon C \to J\) as follows. The
161	symmetric power \(C^{(g)}\) admits a regular morphism \(\varphi^{(g)} \colon C^{(g)} \to A\)
162	defined by
163	\[
164	  \varphi^{(g)}\bigl(P_1 + \dots + P_g\bigr) \;=\; \varphi(P_1) + \dots + \varphi(P_g),
165	\]
166	and a birational morphism \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow J\) (the Abel--Jacobi map). One then
167	seeks a morphism \(J \to A\) making the obvious diagram commute. The construction goes via
168	\(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) followed by descent to \(J\); the rational map
169	\(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) obtained at the intermediate step is exactly the kind that
170	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes to a regular morphism, because \(C^{(g)}\) is
171	a nonsingular variety (Milne~III.3) and \(A\) is an abelian variety. Without
172	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} this step has no replacement: Milne's autoduality
173	shortcut (\(J \cong J^\vee\)) routes through the theorem of the cube, which the project
174	excised at iter-163; see the analysis in \cref{sec:Jacobian_routeA4_albaneseUP} of
175	\cref{chap:Jacobian}.
176	
177	\section{Lean encoding}
178	\label{sec:thm32_lean_encoding}
179	
180	The Lean target file is the new
181	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean}. The single declaration
182	this chapter targets is the theorem
183	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_to\_av}, with signature (in informal
184	notation):
185	\[
186	  \texttt{X : Scheme} \;\to\; \texttt{[Smooth X over \(\bar k\)]} \;\to\;
187	  \texttt{A : AbelianVariety \(\bar k\)} \;\to\;
188	  (f : \texttt{X.RationalMap A}) \;\to\;
189	  \exists ! \, \widetilde f \colon X \to A, \;\; \widetilde f \;\text{extends}\; f.
190	\]
191	The proof body imports \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s exports and
192	\cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}'s exports from
193	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean} and
194	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} respectively, and combines them
195	in the three-step pattern above (codim \(\geq 2\) from A.4.a + pure-codim \(1\) from A.4.a
196	\(\Rightarrow\) empty indeterminacy locus).
197	
198	The Mathlib namespace target is
199	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap} (already housed in
200	\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap} at the project's pinned commit;
201	see \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, where the same namespace hosts the
202	order-at-a-prime-divisor map). The extension is therefore a method on the existing
203	\texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure rather than a free-standing predicate, allowing
204	downstream consumers in \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP} to call it as
205	\texttt{f.extend\_to\_av}.
206	
207	No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a two-line combination of A.4.a and
208	A.4.b. The chapter is therefore inexpensive on the prover side (the LOC budget is dominated
209	by A.4.a and A.4.b themselves); its purpose is to record the precise statement and the
210	exact dependency wiring so that A.4.d can consume a single named lemma.
211	
212	\section{Out of scope}
213	\label{sec:thm32_out_of_scope}
214	
215	This chapter packages \emph{only} the theorem
216	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} (Milne~3.2) and the immediate proof combining its
217	two inputs. The following adjacent content is explicitly out of scope here:
218	\begin{itemize}
219	  \item \textbf{The codim-\(\geq 2\) extension theorem (Milne Theorem~3.1) itself.}
220	    Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} as
221	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; this chapter only consumes it.
222	  \item \textbf{The pure-codim-\(1\) indeterminacy lemma (Milne Lemma~3.3) itself.}
223	    Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} as
224	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}; this chapter only consumes it.
225	  \item \textbf{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and its Cohen--Macaulay corollary
226	    (Milne~\(\equiv\) Stacks 090V / 00OD).} Proved in
227	    \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} as \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} and
228	    \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}; this chapter only consumes them (indirectly,
229	    inside \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; see \cref{rmk:thm32_role_of_ab}).
230	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.4 / Corollary~3.7 / Theorem~3.8 / Proposition~3.9 /
231	    Proposition~3.10.} The remainder of Milne~\S I.3 (the special cases ``rational map
232	    from a product of nonsingular varieties to an AV vanishing on the axes is constant'',
233	    ``rational map between AVs = homomorphism + translation'', ``birationally equivalent
234	    AVs are isomorphic'', ``every \(\mathbb A^1 \dashrightarrow A\) is constant'',
235	    ``every rational map from a unirational variety to an AV is constant'') is downstream
236	    of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} but is not needed by A.4 in the project's
237	    current routing: the genus-\(0\) base case
238	    \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route} closes
239	    via the proven Rigidity Lemma + \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut without invoking any of
240	    these. They are recorded as off-path in
241	    \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}.
242	  \item \textbf{The Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) (Milne Proposition~6.1).}
243	    Consumed by A.4.d, proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}; this chapter only
244	    provides the technical extension lemma that A.4.d invokes when promoting
245	    \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) to \(C^{(g)} \to A\).
246	  \item \textbf{Generalisations beyond an algebraically closed base field.} Milne's
247	    Theorem~3.2 is stated for varieties over an algebraically closed field; the
248	    project's positive-genus arm runs over \(\bar k\) throughout (\cref{chap:RigidityKbar}).
249	    Descent of the extension to a non-algebraically-closed base, if ever needed, would
250	    require additional Galois-descent machinery and is not in scope.
251	\end{itemize}

1889	  \cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}; no block on the genus-\(0\) critical path depends on it.
1890	  Let \(A\) be an abelian variety over the algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), and let
1891	  \(\varphi \colon \mathbb G_a \to A\) be a homomorphism of group objects (so \(\varphi(0) = 0_A\) and
1892	  \(\varphi(x + y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y)\)). Then \(\varphi\) is the trivial homomorphism: it

1900	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. According to (3.2), α extends to a regular map on the whole of A¹.
1901	% After composing α with a translation, we may suppose that α(0)=0. Then α is a homomorphism,
1902	% α(x+y)=α(x)+α(y); all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. But A¹−{0} is also a group variety, and similarly,
1903	% α(xy)=α(x)+α(y)+c; all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. This is absurd, unless α is constant." [Milne,
1904	% Prop 3.9, \S I.3, doc p.~19--20.]
1905	\begin{proof}
1906	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
1907	  Write \(A\) additively (an abelian variety is commutative). We give the conceptual
1908	  \emph{completeness} proof as the abstract argument, then record Milne's elementary $\mathbb
1909	  G_a/\mathbb G_m\( incompatibility, which applies whenever \)\varphi$ extends to a morphism on
1910	  \(\mathbb P^1\) (as it does in the genus-\(0\) application).
1911	  \textbf{Completeness proof.} The image \(\varphi(\mathbb G_a)\) is a connected subgroup of \(A\)
1912	  (image of the connected \(\mathbb G_a\) under a homomorphism). As the image of a homomorphism of
1913	  algebraic groups it is a \emph{closed} subgroup of \(A\), hence \emph{complete} (closed in the
1914	  complete \(A\)); as a homomorphic image of the \emph{affine} \(\mathbb G_a\) it is moreover
1915	  \emph{affine}. A connected variety that is simultaneously complete and affine over \(\bar k\) is a
1916	  single point: its ring of global regular functions is a field finite over \(\bar k\), hence equal
1917	  to \(\bar k\) (the \(\Gamma = \bar k\) collapse already packaged for proper integral sources in
1918	  \cref{lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}). Therefore \(\varphi(\mathbb G_a)\) is one point;
1919	  being a homomorphism, \(\varphi\) sends \(0\) there, so the point is \(0_A\) and
1920	  \(\varphi = \mathtt{toUnit}\,\mathbb G_a \fatsemi \eta_A\).
1921	  \medskip
1922	  \noindent\textbf{Milne's elementary \(\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m\) incompatibility (when \(\varphi\)
1923	  extends to \(\mathbb P^1\)).} Suppose \(\varphi = f|_{\mathbb G_a}\) for a morphism
1924	  \(f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A\) (the genus-\(0\) situation). Set \(g := f - f(1)\), so \(g(1) = 0_A\), and
1925	  form \(h_\times := \sigma_\times \fatsemi g \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to A\) with the
1926	  scaling companion \(\sigma_\times\) of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}. Applying the additive
1927	  decomposition \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} with \(V = \mathbb P^1\) (complete),
1928	  \(W = \mathbb G_m\), base points \(1 \in \mathbb P^1\) and \(1 \in \mathbb G_m\) (so
1929	  \(h_\times(1, 1) = g(1) = 0_A\)), the axis restrictions are \(x \mapsto g(x)\) and
1930	  \(\lambda \mapsto g(\lambda)\), whence \(g(\lambda x) = g(x) + g(\lambda)\) on \(\bar k^\times\);
1931	  re-expanding \(g\) gives the multiplicative relation
1932	  \(\varphi(xy) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y) + c\) with \(c = -\varphi(1)\). Now \(\varphi\) is at once
1933	  additive, \(\varphi(x + y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y)\), and ``multiplicative-additive'',
1934	  \(\varphi(xy) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y) + c\). Computing \(\varphi((x + x')\,y)\) in two ways for
1935	  \(x, x' \in \bar k\), \(y \in \bar k^\times\),
1936	  \[ \varphi((x+x')y) = \varphi(xy) + \varphi(x'y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(x') + 2\varphi(y) + 2c, \]
1937	  while also $\varphi((x+x')y) = \varphi(x+x') + \varphi(y) + c = \varphi(x) + \varphi(x') +
1938	  \varphi(y) + c\(; equating gives \)\varphi(y) = -c = \varphi(1)\( for every \)y \in \bar k^\times$.
1939	  Hence \(\varphi\) is constant on the dense \(\mathbb G_m \subset \mathbb G_a\), so constant on
1940	  \(\mathbb G_a\) (density, \(A\) separated), with value \(\varphi(0) = 0_A\). This is Milne's argument
1941	  verbatim and uses only the proven \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} plus the scaling action,
1942	  avoiding the ``image is a closed subgroup'' input of the completeness proof. \qedhere
1943	\end{proof}

1945	  \label{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}
1946	  \textbf{The primary route is the \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut; why no Theorem~3.2 and no
1947	  \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0\) are needed.} The genus-\(0\) base case ``$f \colon \mathbb P^1
1948	  \to A$ is constant'' is closed \emph{directly} by the proven additive decomposition
1949	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} composed with a total \(\mathbb G\)-action on \(\mathbb P^1\),
1950	  with \emph{no} appeal to Milne's Theorem~3.2 / Lemma~3.3 (rational-map extension), the theorem of
1951	  the cube, Auslander--Buchsbaum, or differentials. The reason the earlier draft thought
1952	  Theorem~3.2 was needed --- that the additive defect \(\psi(x,y) = f(x+y) - f(x) - f(y)\) lives only
1953	  on the affine \(\mathbb G_a \times \mathbb G_a\) and must be extended over $\mathbb P^1 \times
1954	  \mathbb P^1$ --- evaporates once one composes with a \emph{total} action morphism of
1955	  \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}: the composite is already a total morphism out of $\mathbb P^1 \times
1956	  \mathbb G\(, and the complete factor is \)\mathbb P^1$, exactly what
1957	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} (whose Lean signature needs only the first factor
1958	  complete) consumes.
1959	  \medskip
1960	  \noindent\textbf{Primary route --- the scaling shortcut (strategy decision of iter-164).} The
1961	  \emph{multiplicative} scaling action $\sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to
1962	  \mathbb P^1\(, \)(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}), closes the base case
1963	  in one shot, \emph{without} the additive homomorphism step or \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial} at
1964	  all. Normalise \(f(0) = 0_A\) (translate; here \(0\) is a scaling fixed point). Form $h_\times :=
1965	  \sigma_\times \fatsemi f\( and apply \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} with \)V = \mathbb P^1$,
1966	  \(W = \mathbb G_m\), base points \(0 \in \mathbb P^1\) and \(1 \in \mathbb G_m\) (so $h_\times(0, 1) =
1967	  f(0) = 0_A\(). Because \)0\( is a \emph{fixed point} of scaling, the \)W$-axis restriction collapses:
1968	  \(\lambda \mapsto h_\times(0, \lambda) = f(\lambda \cdot 0) = f(0) = 0_A\). The decomposition
1969	  therefore reads \(h_\times = p \fatsemi f\) with the second summand the group identity, i.e.
1970	  \[ \sigma_\times \fatsemi f = \mathrm{pr}_1 \fatsemi f \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to A,
1971	     \qquad\text{``}f(\lambda x) = f(x)\text{''}. \]
1972	  Precomposing with \(\lambda \mapsto (1, \lambda)\) gives \(f|_{\mathbb G_m} = \text{const}\,f(1)\);
1973	  since \(\mathbb G_m\) is dense in \(\mathbb P^1\) and \(A\) is separated, \(f\) is constant on $\mathbb
1974	  P^1$ (the dominant-source/separated-target handle \cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}, i.e.\ the
1975	  \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) family already used by \texttt{rigidity\_core}). This uses
1976	  \emph{only} the proven \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, the total morphism \(\sigma_\times\),
1977	  and density --- no \(\mathbb G_a\) additivity, no \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}, and in
1978	  particular \emph{no} ``image of a group homomorphism is a closed subgroup'' input (a deep Mathlib
1979	  gap). It is the least-Mathlib-blocked realisation of the base case and is the route the live
1980	  consumer \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} formalises.
1981	  \medskip
1982	  \noindent\textbf{Retained alternative --- the \(\mathbb G_a\)-additive route.} The Milne-faithful
1983	  argument routes through the translation action $\sigma \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a \to
1984	  \mathbb P^1\(: \)\sigma \fatsemi f\( is total, the additive decomposition makes \)f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ a
1985	  
1986	  homomorphism \(\mathbb G_a \to A\) (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}), and a homomorphism from the
1987	  
1988	  additive group into an abelian variety is trivial (\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}, Milne's
1989	  \(\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m\) incompatibility, Proposition~3.9). This reaches the same conclusion but
1990	  needs the triviality lemma; it is retained for fidelity to Milne and is \emph{off} the genus-\(0\)
1991	  critical path.
1992	\end{remark}

1	\chapter{Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form; Mumford \S4 in the abelian-variety case)}
2	\label{chap:Rigidity}
3	
4	This chapter records a self-contained helper: \emph{rigidity for morphisms of schemes that agree on a non-empty open}. The classical motivation is Mumford's rigidity for abelian-variety morphisms, but the project's formalised statement is more general: the source-side group-object hypothesis is unused by the proof and is dropped.
5	
6	The lemma is independent of the construction of \(\Jac(C)\). It is the load-bearing input for the M2.a sub-step of the genus-0 Albanese-witness argument (Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b), where the source is taken to be \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) (which is \emph{not} a group scheme).
7	
8	\section{Statement}
9	
10	\begin{theorem}\leanok
11	[Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form)]
12	  \label{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
13	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen}
14	  Let \(k\) be a field. Let \(X\) be a reduced geometrically irreducible \(k\)-scheme and let \(Y\) be a separated \(k\)-scheme. Let \(g_1, g_2 \colon X \to Y\) be two morphisms of \(k\)-schemes, and let \(U \subseteq X\) be a non-empty open subset. If
15	  \[
16	    U.\iota \circ g_1 \;=\; U.\iota \circ g_2
17	  \]
18	  as scheme morphisms (where \(U.\iota \colon U \hookrightarrow X\) is the canonical open immersion), then \(g_1 = g_2\) as morphisms of \(k\)-schemes.
19	\end{theorem}
20	
21	\begin{remark}
22	  The classical Mumford rigidity theorem (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S4) usually states the special case where \(Y\) is an abelian variety and the conclusion is phrased as ``a morphism vanishing on a non-empty open is zero''. The two forms are interderivable in the abelian-variety case via the difference morphism \(\delta = g_1 \cdot g_2^{-1}\) formed from the group law on \(Y\). The project formalises the more general statement above because the M2.a (genus-0 Albanese-witness) consumer specifically needs the source to be \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\), which is not a group scheme: the abelian-variety packaging is consumer-side and the scheme-level statement is what the proof actually establishes.
23	
24	  The refactor that landed the scheme-level form (iter-125; see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md}) dropped 8 unused hypotheses from the project's original group-scheme-flavoured declaration and weakened the target-side \([\mathtt{IsProper}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\) to \([\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\).
25	\end{remark}
26	
27	\section{Proof sketch}
28	
29	\begin{proof}
30	  \leanok
31	  We reduce to Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}), which gives the standard scheme-level fact that two morphisms from a reduced scheme to a separated scheme agreeing on a dominant subscheme agree everywhere.
32	
33	  The three load-bearing instances feeding that closure are:
34	  \begin{itemize}
35	    \item \emph{\(Y \to \Spec k\) is separated.} Hypothesis \([\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\) provides this directly (no projection from \(\mathtt{IsProper}\) needed). The \texttt{OverClass.fromOver} instance transports it to the form \(\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ (Y.\mathrm{left} \downarrow \Spec k)\) that the closure step expects.
36	    \item \emph{\(X\) is an irreducible space.} Hypothesis \([\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\ X.\mathrm{hom}]\) over the field-spectrum base \(\Spec k\) (which is a single point, hence subsingleton) yields \(\mathtt{IrreducibleSpace}\ X.\mathrm{left}\) via \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton}.
37	    \item \emph{The inclusion \(U.\iota\) is dominant.} A non-empty open in an irreducible space is dense, and a dense open immersion is dominant: \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι}.
38	  \end{itemize}
39	  With these three derived instances and the hypothesis \([\mathtt{IsReduced}\ X.\mathrm{left}]\), the closure \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} produces the underlying scheme-morphism equality \(g_1.\mathrm{left} = g_2.\mathrm{left}\) from the agreement-on-\(U\) hypothesis. The promotion to \(g_1 = g_2\) in \(\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)\) uses \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext}.
40	
41	  \medskip
42	  \noindent\textbf{Mathlib ingredients.}
43	  \begin{itemize}
44	    \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} for the scheme-level closure step.
45	    \item \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton} to derive irreducibility of \(X.\mathrm{left}\).
46	    \item \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι} to derive dominance of the open immersion \(U.\iota\) from the non-empty hypothesis.
47	    \item \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext} to promote underlying scheme equality to \(\mathrm{Over}\)-morphism equality.
48	    \item No abelian-variety / group-object infrastructure is needed: smoothness, properness, and group-object hypotheses on \(X\) and \(Y\) are all dropped from the formalised statement.
49	  \end{itemize}
50	\end{proof}
51	
52	\section{Use in the project}
53	
54	The rigidity theorem is used in two places downstream:
55	
56	\begin{itemize}
57	  \item \textbf{M2.a (genus-0 Albanese witness).} Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b reduces the genus-0 rigidity-over-\(\bar k\) argument (for \(f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}\) hitting the identity at a \(\bar k\)-point) to this theorem with \(X := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) and \(Y := A_{\bar k}\). The refactor of iter-125 (dropping the source-side group-object hypothesis) is what enables this application. The named project declaration packaging the C.2.a–C.2.e reduction is \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} in \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}; that chapter scaffolds the iter-126 Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar} with the C.2.d cotangent-vanishing keystone residual.
58	  \item \textbf{Uniqueness in the Albanese property.} Used in the uniqueness half of Theorem~REF (the Albanese property of \(\Jac(C)\)): given two morphisms \(g_1, g_2 \colon \Jac(C) \to A\) with \(g_1 \circ \alpha_P = g_2 \circ \alpha_P\), scheme-level agreement on the image of \(\alpha_P\) (a non-empty open after passing to \(C^{(g)} \twoheadrightarrow \Jac(C)\)) forces \(g_1 = g_2\).
59	\end{itemize}
60	
61	Note: the existence half of REF requires Phase~B/C infrastructure (Picard scheme, dual abelian variety). Rigidity alone yields \emph{uniqueness} only.
62	
63	\section{Mathlib status}
64	
65	The scheme-level form is a thin specialization of Mathlib's existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}); the project's contribution is the \(\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)\)-bundled packaging plus the convenience derivations of \texttt{IrreducibleSpace} and \texttt{IsDominant} from the project-side hypotheses.
66	
67	An upstream-PR candidate is a thin corollary in Mathlib's \texttt{Separated} file:
68	\[
69	  \texttt{ext\_of\_eqOnNonemptyOpen} \;\colon\; \texttt{(non-empty open) + (reduced source) + (separated target) }\Rightarrow\texttt{ ext}
70	\]
71	packaging the dense-and-dominant chain into a single API call. The incremental value over the existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} is small (just the API convenience of "non-empty open" over "dominant morphism"); see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md} for the proposed statement and home file. The project does not pursue this upstream PR as part of the iter-125 work.
72
