3	\chapter{The invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) of a Weil divisor (RR.2\(_\ast\))}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OcOfD}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II~\S6, pp.~144--145
7	% (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\); Proposition 6.13; Remark 6.17.1;
8	% Proposition 6.18) and IV.1, p.~296 (the inductive-step short exact
9	% sequence). Read directly from
10	% references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161--162 and
11	% 313, by rendering the scanned page images and transcribing the
12	% statements character-by-character (the PDF has no text layer). Stacks
13	% pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (Weil divisors), 0AUW
14	% (the sheaf \(\mathcal O_X(D)\) from a Weil divisor), 0BE3 (degree-zero of
15	% a principal divisor on a complete nonsingular curve).
16	
17	\section{Setup and motivation}
18	
19	This chapter is a satellite of \texttt{RR.2}
20	(REF); together with \texttt{RR.1}
21	(REF), \texttt{RR.3}
22	(REF), and \texttt{RR.4}
23	(\texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex}) it forms the four-stage
24	sub-build closing the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
25	\[
26	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
27	  \,\colon\,
28	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
29	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
30	\]
31	The Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain consumed by REF
32	of \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} relies on the invertible-sheaf
33	functor
34	\[
35	  \mathcal O_C(-) \;\colon\; \mathrm{Div}(C) \;\longrightarrow\;
36	  \mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})
37	\]
38	sending a Weil divisor \(D = \sum_i n_i [P_i]\) to its associated invertible
39	sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) on \(C\). The Lean entry-point
40	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf} of
41	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean} (line~168) currently
42	encodes this functor as a typed-\texttt{sorry} \texttt{noncomputable def}
43	with no chapter pinning. This chapter supplies the missing pinning by
44	blueprinting the Lean file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean}
45	(to be created in iter-183), where the body of \texttt{sheafOf} and its
46	three immediate corollaries will live.
47	
48	\paragraph{Why this chapter is gated to \texttt{RR.2}.} The
49	\(\chi\)-identity proof of REF
50	in \texttt{RR.2} is an induction on the Weil divisor \(D\) via the
51	``\(D \rightsquigarrow D + [Y]\)'' step of Hartshorne~IV.1.3. That step
52	intrinsically references the body of \texttt{sheafOf}: it asks for the
53	short exact sequence
54	\[
55	  0 \to \mathcal O_C(D) \to \mathcal O_C(D + [Y]) \to k(Y)^{[\text{at } Y]}
56	  \to 0
57	\]
58	to be exact in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\), which is not a statement on the
59	\emph{signature} of \texttt{sheafOf} but on its \emph{body}. The iter-181
60	Lane H factoring of \texttt{RR.2} consequently
61	landed two helper sorries
62	(\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_zero},
63	\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_succ}) that are
64	\emph{intrinsically gated} on the body of \texttt{sheafOf}. Until this
65	chapter pins the body, those helpers remain vacuous regardless of how
66	much \(\chi\)-machinery \texttt{RR.2} adds on top.
67	
68	\paragraph{Why this chapter is the entry-point for \texttt{RR.3}.} The
69	existing \texttt{RR.3} chapter (REF) constructs
70	the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) associated to a \emph{closed point}
71	\(P \in C\) directly, bypassing the Weil-divisor packaging. The
72	REF below makes this bypass mathematically
73	coherent by exhibiting \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) at \(D = [P]\) as canonically
74	isomorphic to REF. With this isomorphism in
75	hand, \texttt{RR.3}'s downstream consumption of
76	\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) via the
77	\(\chi\)-identity routes through \texttt{sheafOf} cleanly.
78	
79	\paragraph{Why this chapter is the entry-point for \texttt{RR.4}.} The
80	two pins consumed by the \texttt{RR.4} chapter
81	(\texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex})
82	that translate Riemann--Roch outputs into the function-field/degree
83	language of \(C\) both reference \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) for general \(D\) (not
84	just \(D = [P]\)): the function-field-degree bridge takes a divisor
85	\(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) and reads off \(\deg(D)\) from the
86	\texttt{sheafOf}-associated invertible sheaf, while the
87	Weil-divisor / line-bundle correspondence is the substance of
88	Hartshorne's Proposition~6.15 (which the present chapter pins via
89	REF).
90	
91	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
92	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
93	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
94	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean}) carry the same
95	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
96	\begin{quote}
97	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
98	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
99	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
100	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
101	\end{quote}
102	The Weil divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) is the one defined in
103	REF of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex}; the
104	prime-divisor data type is REF; the order
105	\(\ord_Q(f)\) at a prime divisor \(Q\) is REF; the
106	principal divisor \(\div(f)\) of a non-zero rational function is
107	REF; the closed-point divisor \([P]\) is
108	REF. The line bundle at a closed point
109	already constructed in \texttt{RR.3} is
110	REF, and the present chapter's
111	REF makes the comparison
112	\(\mathcal O_C([P]) = \mathcal O_C(D)|_{D = [P]}\) explicit.
113	
114	\paragraph{Route alignment with iter-182 \texttt{ocofp-sheaf-internalhom}
115	analogy.} The iter-182 api-alignment analogy
116	\texttt{analogies/ocofp-sheaf-internalhom.md} surveyed three candidate
117	Mathlib paths for the closed-point specialisation \(\mathcal O_C([P])\):
118	(i)~\texttt{Sheaf-internal-Hom} on \(\mathbf{SheafOfModules}\) (rejected:
119	no \texttt{IdealSheafData \(\to\) SheafOfModules} realiser in Mathlib,
120	no \(\mathbf{SheafOfModules}\) dual functor in Mathlib);
121	(ii)~the abstract dual $\mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
122	\mathcal O_C)$ in a new \texttt{IdealSheafDual.lean} (rejected: high
123	LOC cost in exchange for a single sheaf the project can build directly);
124	(iii)~Hartshorne's \emph{subsheaf-of-\(\mathcal K_C\)} description (accepted).
125	The present chapter adopts route~(iii) for the general
126	\texttt{sheafOf \(D\)} as well, and REF verifies
127	that the general construction at \(D = [P]\) recovers the closed-point
128	specialisation of \texttt{RR.3} on the nose. The subsheaf-of-\(\mathcal K_C\)
129	recipe is mathematically Hartshorne's original (II~\S6, the definition of
130	\(\mathscr L(D)\)) and was equally available for the closed-point case; the
131	project's choice to use it both at the general \(D\) and at the \(D = [P]\)
132	specialisation is what makes the comparison
133	\(\mathcal O_C([P]) \cong \mathcal O_C(D)|_{D = [P]}\) a direct unfolding
134	rather than a non-trivial isomorphism.
135	
136	\section{The invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)}
137	
138	\begin{definition}\leanok
139	[Invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) of a Weil divisor]
140	  \label{def:sheafOf}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf}
142	  
143	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
144	  % sheaf \(\mathscr L(D)\) associated to a Cartier divisor on a scheme),
145	  % and Remark~6.17.1, Proposition~6.18 + Proposition~6.15, p.~145
146	  % (Cartier \(\leftrightarrow\) Weil correspondence on an integral
147	  % locally factorial scheme; identification of \(\mathscr L(D)\) as a
148	  % subsheaf of the constant sheaf at the function field \(K\)). Read from
149	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161--162,
150	  % transcribed character-by-character from rendered scanned page
151	  % images (the PDF has no text layer).
152	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
153	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
154	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
155	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
156	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
157	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), so \(f_i^{-1}\) and
158	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
159	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
160	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.15. \emph{If \(X\) is an integral
161	  % scheme, the homomorphism \(\mathrm{CaCl}\, X \to \mathrm{Pic}\, X\) of
162	  % (6.14) is an isomorphism.} Proof. We have only to show that every
163	  % invertible sheaf is isomorphic to a subsheaf of \(\mathscr K\), which
164	  % in this case is the constant sheaf \(K\), where \(K\) is the function
165	  % field of \(X\)."
166	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Remark 6.17.1. Clearly this gives a 1-1
167	  % correspondence between effective Cartier divisors on \(X\) and
168	  % \emph{locally principal} closed subschemes \(Y\), i.e., subschemes
169	  % whose sheaf of ideals is locally generated by a single element.
170	  % Note also that if \(X\) is an integral separated noetherian locally
171	  % factorial scheme, so that the Cartier divisors correspond to Weil
172	  % divisors by (6.11), then the effective Cartier divisors correspond
173	  % exactly to the effective Weil divisors."
174	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (the definition of
175	  \(\mathscr L(D)\)), Proposition~6.15 (the constant-sheaf-at-\(K\)
176	  identification on an integral scheme), and Remark~6.17.1 (Weil
177	  divisors and Cartier divisors agree on an integral separated
178	  noetherian locally factorial scheme).}
179	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
180	  \(\bar k\) and let \(D = \sum_{Q} n_Q [Q] \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor
181	  on \(C\), with the formal sum indexed by prime divisors \(Q\) of \(C\) and
182	  supported on a finite set \(\{Q : n_Q \neq 0\}\)
183	  (REF). The \emph{invertible sheaf
184	  \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to \(D\)}, also written
185	  \(\mathcal O_C(D) := \mathscr L(D)\) in Hartshorne's notation, is the
186	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the constant sheaf
187	  \(\mathcal K_C \cong K(C)\) (Proposition~6.15: on an integral scheme the
188	  sheaf of total quotient rings is the constant sheaf at the function
189	  field) defined section-wise by
190	  \[
191	    \Gamma\bigl(U, \mathcal O_C(D)\bigr) \;:=\;
192	    \{\, 0 \,\} \;\cup\;
193	    \bigl\{\, f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\;
194	      \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; -\,n_Q \text{ for every prime divisor }
195	      Q \text{ of } C \text{ with } Q \in U \bigr\}
196	  \]
197	  for each open \(U \subseteq C\). Restriction along \(V \hookrightarrow U\)
198	  is the identity on the underlying carrier \(K(C)\), which preserves
199	  membership (a smaller open imposes \emph{fewer} order constraints, so
200	  the constraint set on \(U\) embeds into the constraint set on \(V\)). The
201	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on
202	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\) is restricted from the
203	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on \(K(C)\) (a \(\bar k\)-scalar multiple of a
204	  rational function has the same order at every prime divisor).
205	
206	  \paragraph{Equivalence with the Cartier-divisor description.} The
207	  formal sum \(D = \sum_Q n_Q [Q]\) packages, on a curve where prime
208	  divisors are exactly closed points and the local rings at prime
209	  divisors are DVRs (smoothness \(+\) dimension~\(1\)), the Cartier-divisor
210	  data \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) of Hartshorne's definition: locally on a small
211	  open \(U_i\) containing exactly one prime divisor \(Q\) in the support of
212	  \(D\), pick a uniformiser \(\pi_Q \in K(C)\) at \(Q\) and set
213	  \(f_i := \pi_Q^{n_Q}\); on opens disjoint from the support of \(D\), set
214	  \(f_i := 1\). The transitions on overlaps \(U_i \cap U_j\) are units in
215	  \(\mathcal O_{C, Q}^{\times}\) for each prime \(Q\) in the overlap (any
216	  two uniformisers differ by a unit, by the DVR structure), so the
217	  \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) define a Cartier divisor whose Weil-divisor image
218	  under Hartshorne's (6.11) is the original \(D\). The
219	  ``\(f_i^{-1}\)-generated'' subsheaf of \(\mathcal K_C\) from Hartshorne's
220	  definition then coincides with the section-wise description above:
221	  the local generator \(f_i^{-1} = \pi_Q^{-n_Q}\) has order \(-n_Q\) at \(Q\),
222	  so the local sections are exactly \(f \in K(C)\) with
223	  \(\ord_Q(f \cdot \pi_Q^{n_Q}) \geq 0\), i.e.\ \(\ord_Q(f) \geq -n_Q\), on
224	  \(U_i\).
225	
226	  Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) (quoted in
227	  REF of REF)
228	  then guarantees that the resulting subsheaf $\mathcal O_C(D) \subset
229	  \mathcal K_C\( is an invertible sheaf on \)C$, independently of the
230	  choice of uniformisers \(\pi_Q\) (any two choices differ by a unit, so
231	  they generate the same \(\mathcal O_C\)-submodule).
232	
233	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
234	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf} takes a Weil
235	  divisor \(D : C.\text{left.WeilDivisor}\) and returns a
236	  \(\texttt{Sheaf}~J~(\mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\) on
237	  \(C.\text{left.toTopCat}\), where
238	  \(J = \texttt{Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat}\) is the
239	  standard topology on the topological space underlying \(C\). The
240	  closed-point specialisation \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) is
241	  REF of
242	  REF; their agreement at \(D = [P]\) is
243	  REF below. The construction follows
244	  the iter-182 \texttt{ocofp-sheaf-internalhom} recipe in
245	  \texttt{analogies/}: presheaf-by-hand via per-open
246	  \(\bar k\)-submodules of the function field, sheaf property reduced to
247	  the locality of the order conditions at the stalks of the prime
248	  divisors. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
249	\end{definition}
250	
251	\section{Immediate corollaries}
252	
253	\begin{lemma}\leanok
254	[Sheaf of the zero divisor is the structure sheaf]
255	  \label{lem:sheafOf_zero}
256	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_zero}
257	  
258	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
259	  \(\bar k\). Then the invertible sheaf associated to the zero divisor
260	  \(0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) is canonically the structure sheaf:
261	  \[
262	    \mathcal O_C(0) \;=\; \mathcal O_C
263	  \]
264	  as sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\), equivalently as
265	  objects in \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\).
266	\end{lemma}
267	
268	\begin{proof}
269	  
270	  At \(D = 0\) the coefficient \(n_Q = 0\) at every prime divisor \(Q\), so the
271	  section condition of REF reduces, on every open
272	  \(U \subseteq C\), to
273	  \[
274	    \Gamma\bigl(U, \mathcal O_C(0)\bigr)
275	    \;=\; \{ 0 \} \cup \{ f \in K(C)^{\times} \;|\;
276	      \ord_Q(f) \geq 0 \text{ for every prime divisor } Q \in U \}.
277	  \]
278	  By the local description of the structure sheaf on the integral
279	  scheme \(C\) (the regular functions on \(U\) are exactly the rational
280	  functions whose order is non-negative at every prime divisor of
281	  \(C\) landing in \(U\); this is the standard ``non-negative order \(=\)
282	  regular'' identification, e.g.\ Hartshorne~II~\S6 immediately before
283	  Proposition~6.11), the right-hand side is precisely
284	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C)\). The restriction maps on both sides are
285	  the identity on \(K(C)\), so the equality of presheaves promotes to an
286	  equality of sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\).
287	\end{proof}

289	\begin{lemma}\leanok
290	[Sheaf at a single closed point is the line bundle of
291	REF]
292	  \label{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint}
293	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_singlePoint}
294	  
295	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
296	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point, viewed as a Weil divisor
297	  \([P] \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) via REF. Then the
298	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) of REF agrees on
299	  the nose with the line bundle of the closed point
300	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) of REF:
301	  \[
302	    \mathcal O_C(D)\bigl|_{D = [P]}
303	    \;=\; \texttt{lineBundleAtClosedPoint}_{P}
304	  \]
305	  as sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\), equivalently as
306	  objects in \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\).
307	\end{lemma}
308	
309	\begin{proof}
310	  
311	  Unfold both definitions. On the \texttt{sheafOf} side, the formal sum
312	  \([P]\) has coefficients \(n_Q = 1\) if \(Q = P\) and \(n_Q = 0\) otherwise
313	  (where \(P\) is identified with the prime divisor it determines by
314	  REF). Thus
315	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) contains \(0\) and those
316	  \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) satisfying \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\) for every prime
317	  divisor \(Q \in U\) with \(Q \neq P\), plus \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\) if
318	  \(P \in U\).
319	
320	  On the REF side, the same conditions
321	  are exactly the ones extracted by
322	  REF of
323	  \texttt{RR.3}, at every open \(U\) (not only at the global section
324	  \(U = C\)): a rational function \(f \in K(C)\) is a section of
325	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) on \(U\) iff it lies in the structure sheaf at every
326	  \(Q \in U \setminus \{P\}\) and has at most a simple pole at \(P\) when
327	  \(P \in U\).
328	
329	  Restriction maps on both sides are the identity on \(K(C)\). The two
330	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\) are therefore equal
331	  section-by-section as subsets of \(K(C)\), and the equality propagates
332	  to an equality of sheaves of \(\mathcal O_C\)-modules. The
333	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on both sides is the restriction of the
334	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on \(K(C)\), so the identification is
335	  \(\bar k\)-linear on each section.
336	\end{proof}

338	\begin{lemma}\leanok
339	[Short exact sequence for \(D \rightsquigarrow D + [Y]\)]
340	  \label{lem:sheafOf_ses_single_add}
341	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_ses_single_add}
342	  
343	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (the inductive step of the proof
344	  % of Theorem~1.3, ``Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any
345	  % point...''). Read from
346	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313,
347	  % transcribed character-by-character from the rendered scanned page
348	  % image (the PDF has no text layer).
349	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its
350	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\),
351	  % which we denote by \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\)
352	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
353	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
354	  % Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
355	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\).
356	  % (Since \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by
357	  % it does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\).)"
358	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
359	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
360	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
361	  \(\bar k\), let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor, and let \(P \in C\) be
362	  a closed point (equivalently, a prime divisor
363	  REF on a smooth curve). Then the inclusion
364	  \(\mathcal O_C(D) \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) of
365	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\) fits into a short exact
366	  sequence
367	  \[
368	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D)
369	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
370	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
371	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
372	  \]
373	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\) (equivalently, in
374	  \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\)), where \(k(P)\) denotes the
375	  skyscraper sheaf at \(P\) with stalk \(\bar k\) (the residue field of \(P\)
376	  is \(\bar k\) since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed and \(P\) is a
377	  \(\bar k\)-rational closed point).
378	\end{lemma}
379	
380	\begin{proof}
381	  
382	  Hartshorne's argument (IV.1.3, the inductive step quoted above)
383	  proceeds in two beats:
384	
385	  \emph{Beat 1 (the ideal-sheaf SES at \(P\)).} The closed-subscheme
386	  inclusion \(P \hookrightarrow C\) has structure sheaf
387	  \(\mathcal O_P \cong k(P)\) (a skyscraper at \(P\) with stalk \(\bar k\)),
388	  and Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.18 identifies its ideal sheaf as
389	  \(\mathcal I_P = \mathcal O_C(-[P])\) (the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of
390	  \(\mathcal K_C\) generated by a local uniformiser \(\pi_P\) at \(P\) and by
391	  \(1\) away from \(P\), i.e.\ the locally-principal ideal of the locally
392	  principal closed subscheme \(P\)). This produces the structural short
393	  exact sequence
394	  \[
395	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(-[P])
396	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C
397	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
398	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
399	  \]
400	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\).
401	
402	  \emph{Beat 2 (tensor with \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\)).} The line bundle
403	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) is locally free of rank~\(1\)
404	  (Hartshorne~II.6.13(a), REF), so $- \otimes_{\mathcal O_C}
405	  \mathcal O_C(D + [P])\( is exact on \)\mathbf{Coh}(C)$ and preserves
406	  the short exact sequence of Beat~1:
407	  \[
408	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes
409	      \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
410	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
411	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
412	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0.
413	  \]
414	  Three identifications collapse this to the claimed sequence:
415	  \begin{itemize}
416	    \item $\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
417	      \mathcal O_C(D)$ (tensor product of line bundles is the
418	      \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\)-additive operation $D' + D'' \mapsto \mathcal O_C(D' +
419	      D'')$ via Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(b), which is in turn the
420	      ring-theoretic identity
421	      $\pi_P^{-1} \cdot \pi_P^{1 + n_P^{D}} \cdot \prod_{Q \neq P}
422	      \pi_Q^{n_Q^{D}} = \prod_Q \pi_Q^{n_Q^{D}}$ on the generating
423	      rational functions, where \(n_Q^{D}\) is the coefficient of \(Q\)
424	      in \(D\));
425	    \item $\mathcal O_C \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
426	      \mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ (the structure sheaf is the tensor unit);
427	    \item \(k(P) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong k(P)\) (tensoring a
428	      skyscraper at \(P\) by a locally free rank-\(1\) sheaf preserves the
429	      skyscraper; concretely, at the stalk over \(P\) the line bundle
430	      \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])_P\) is a free \(\mathcal O_{C,P}\)-module of
431	      rank one, and $k(P) \otimes_{\mathcal O_{C,P}} \mathcal O_{C,P}
432	      \cong k(P)\( is just \)k(P)\(; away from \)P$ the skyscraper is the
433	      zero sheaf, on which the tensor is the zero sheaf).
434	  \end{itemize}
435	  Substituting yields
436	  \[
437	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D)
438	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
439	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
440	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0,
441	  \]
442	  exact in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\), as required.
443	
444	  \emph{Compatibility with the structural inclusion.} The first map
445	  \(\mathcal O_C(D) \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) is the
446	  inclusion of subsheaves of \(\mathcal K_C\): at the level of sections,
447	  the constraint \(\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D}\) of
448	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\) implies the weaker constraint
449	  \(\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D} - 1\) of \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D + [P]))\)
450	  when \(P \in U\) (constraints on \(Q \neq P\) are unchanged), and the map
451	  is identity on \(K(C)\) at the carrier level. The second map
452	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \twoheadrightarrow k(P)\) is the evaluation
453	  ``leading coefficient at the pole at \(P\)'' (i.e.\ the
454	  \(\pi_P^{-(n_P^{D} + 1)}\)-coefficient in the Laurent expansion of \(f\)
455	  at \(P\)), which is well-defined on \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D + [P]))\)
456	  modulo the subgroup of sections with \(\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D}\) ---
457	  i.e.\ modulo \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\).
458	\end{proof}

460	\section{Sheaf-property correctness}
461	
462	The construction of REF is presheaf-by-hand: a
463	per-open \(\bar k\)-submodule of \(K(C)\), with restriction along
464	\(V \hookrightarrow U\) given by the identity on \(K(C)\). This section
465	sketches the verifications that make the construction land in the
466	target category \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\).
467	
468	\paragraph{Functoriality of the carrier.} For \(V \subseteq U\), the
469	constraint set defining \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\) is a \emph{subset}
470	of the constraint set defining \(\Gamma(V, \mathcal O_C(D))\) (fewer
471	prime divisors to constrain at), so the identity on \(K(C)\) restricts
472	to a well-defined inclusion
473	\(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D)) \hookrightarrow \Gamma(V, \mathcal O_C(D))\).
474	Composition \(W \subseteq V \subseteq U\) is again identity-on-\(K(C)\), so
475	strict functoriality holds.
476	
477	\paragraph{\(\bar k\)-linearity.} A \(\bar k\)-scalar \(\lambda \in \bar k\)
478	acts on \(K(C)\) by multiplication, and
479	\(\ord_Q(\lambda \cdot f) = \ord_Q(f)\) for \(\lambda \neq 0\)
480	(units have order zero); for \(\lambda = 0\), \(\lambda \cdot f = 0\) is
481	included by convention. Thus the constraint set is a
482	\(\bar k\)-submodule and the restriction maps are \(\bar k\)-linear.
483	
484	\paragraph{Sheaf property.} The order condition
485	``\(\ord_Q(f) \geq -n_Q\) for every prime divisor \(Q \in U\)'' is
486	\emph{stalk-local} at each \(Q\): it depends only on the germ of \(f\) at
487	the point of \(C\) underlying \(Q\), equivalently on the image of \(f\) in
488	the DVR \(\mathcal O_{C, Q}\). Consequently the sheaf property reduces
489	to two ingredients:
490	\begin{enumerate}
491	  \item \emph{The presheaf-of-sets underlying \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is a
492	    subpresheaf of the constant presheaf at \(K(C)\).} The constant
493	    presheaf at \(K(C)\) is a sheaf (every locally-constant section of a
494	    constant presheaf on a topological space is globally constant when
495	    the space is irreducible, which the topological space underlying an
496	    integral scheme is). A subpresheaf of a sheaf is itself a sheaf iff
497	    its membership condition is local; the order conditions are local
498	    at each prime divisor's underlying point, so the membership
499	    condition is local across an open cover and a section glued from
500	    locally-defined sections inherits the order condition at every
501	    prime divisor.
502	  \item \emph{The forget-functor bridge from
503	    \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\) to
504	    \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Set})\).} The categorical forget
505	    \(\mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k} \to \mathbf{Set}\) creates limits, hence
506	    reflects the sheaf property: a presheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules is a
507	    sheaf iff its underlying presheaf of sets is. Combined with~(i),
508	    this delivers the sheaf property for the
509	    \(\bar k\)-module-valued \(\mathcal O_C(D)\).
510	\end{enumerate}
511	
512	\paragraph{Invertibility.} On any open \(U \subseteq C\) disjoint from
513	the support of \(D\), the constraint set is exactly the structure-sheaf
514	constraint \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\) for every prime divisor \(Q \in U\); hence
515	\(\mathcal O_C(D)|_U = \mathcal O_C|_U\), and the trivialisation
516	\(\mathcal O_C(D)|_U \cong \mathcal O_C|_U\) is the identity. On a small
517	open \(U \subseteq C\) containing exactly one prime divisor \(Q\) from the
518	support of \(D\), the local generator \(\pi_Q^{-n_Q} \in K(C)\) trivialises
519	\(\mathcal O_C(D)|_U\) as a free rank-\(1\) \(\mathcal O_C|_U\)-module: a
520	section \(f \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\) satisfies $\ord_Q(f) \geq
521	-n_Q\( iff \)\pi_Q^{n_Q} \cdot f \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C)$, and the
522	``multiplication by \(\pi_Q^{n_Q}\)'' map is an
523	\(\mathcal O_C|_U\)-linear isomorphism
524	\(\mathcal O_C(D)|_U \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal O_C|_U\). The two types
525	of trivialising opens cover \(C\) (any prime divisor lies in at most
526	finitely many supports across a covering, and the support of \(D\) is
527	finite); patching the trivialisations on overlaps amounts to changing
528	uniformiser, which is unit-times the original (by the DVR property), so
529	the cocycle is in \(\mathcal O_C^{\times}\) and the sheaf is invertible.
530	
531	The closure of (i)+(ii) in Lean follows the project's existing
532	\texttt{Scheme.toModuleKPresheaf} /
533	\texttt{toModuleKPresheaf\_isSheaf} template in
534	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK/}, which
535	landed the analogous bottom-up
536	\(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\)-valued construction for the
537	structure sheaf. The order-condition locality is closed via the project's
538	\texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} stalk bridge of
539	\texttt{RR.1} (REF and surrounding lemmas).
540

580	\section{Out of scope}
581	
582	\begin{itemize}
583	  \item \textbf{Linear-equivalence isomorphism.} The natural
584	    isomorphism \(\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')\) when
585	    \(D \sim D'\) (i.e.\ \(D - D' = \div(f)\) for some
586	    \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\)) is \emph{not} formalised in this chapter.
587	    It is not needed for the genus-\(0\) critical path: \texttt{RR.2}
588	    consumes only \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D))\) and the SES additivity at
589	    closed points; \texttt{RR.3} consumes only the closed-point
590	    specialisation; \texttt{RR.4} consumes the function-field /
591	    degree bridge, which is divisorial in \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) rather than
592	    in \(\mathrm{Pic}(C) = \mathrm{Div}(C) / \sim\). The
593	    Cartier-divisor \(\leftrightarrow\) \texttt{Pic} isomorphism is
594	    queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter.
595	  \item \textbf{Sheaf-multiplication
596	    $\mathcal O_C(D) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D') \cong \mathcal O_C(D +
597	    D')$.} The tensor-product identity of Hartshorne's
598	    Proposition~6.13(b) is invoked inside the proof of
599	    REF at the single instance
600	    $\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
601	    \mathcal O_C(D)$, but the \emph{statement} of
602	    Proposition~6.13(b) as a separate lemma is not formalised in this
603	    chapter. The instance used inside the SES proof is local to that
604	    proof; a generalised tensor-additivity lemma is queued for the
605	    post-RR-bridge follow-up.
606	  \item \textbf{Pullback of \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) along a morphism.} The
607	    pullback \(f^* \mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_{C'}(f^* D)\) for
608	    a morphism of curves \(f : C' \to C\), used in degree-computation
609	    arguments on a base change, is \emph{not} formalised in this
610	    chapter and is not needed for the genus-\(0\) critical path.
611	  \item \textbf{Global-sections identification as the Riemann--Roch
612	    space.} The identification
613	    \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) \cong L(D)\) for a general Weil divisor
614	    \(D\) (i.e.\ the analogue of
615	    REF for general
616	    \(D\)) is queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up. The closed-point
617	    case \(D = [P]\) is the only specialisation consumed by the
618	    genus-\(0\) critical path, and it is handled in
619	    \texttt{RR.3} via REF.
620	  \item \textbf{Serre duality and the canonical sheaf.} As recorded in
621	    the \texttt{RR.2} and \texttt{RR.3} chapters, Mathlib (snapshot
622	    \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme
623	    and no Serre duality theorem. The genus-\(0\) critical path
624	    does \emph{not} require either; the present chapter accordingly
625	    does not engage them.
626	\end{itemize}

2183	  \uses{def:genus, thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}

2185	  % still `sorry`) — that prepare the chartwise glue for the iter-169 attack on

2188	  % chapter; this is a blueprint-writer-iter169 dispatch.

2190	\section{The headline: rigidity for a pointed genus-\(0\) curve}
2191	\begin{theorem}
2192	\leanok
2193	[Rigidity: a pointed morphism from a genus-\(0\) curve to an abelian variety is constant]
2194	  \label{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
2195	  
2196	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme}
2197	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field (\emph{arbitrary characteristic}; no
2198	  \(\mathtt{CharZero}\) hypothesis). Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
2199	  over \(\bar k\) with \(\genus(C) = 0\), and let \(A\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
2200	  group scheme over \(\bar k\) (an abelian variety) with identity \(\eta_A\). Then for every
2201	  morphism \(f \colon C \to A\) such that \(p \fatsemi f = \eta_A\) for some \(\bar k\)-point
2202	  \(p \colon \mathbf 1 \to C\), the morphism \(f\) equals the constant morphism at the identity,
2203	  \(f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A\).
2204	  This is the project's committed characteristic-free statement that the genus-\(0\)
2205	  Albanese-witness \(\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}\) consumes. Its signature mirrors
2206	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar}\) of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}
2207	  \emph{verbatim except} that the \([\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]\) instance is dropped: same
2208	  curve typeclasses (\(\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 1\), \(\mathtt{IsProper}\),
2209	  \(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\) on \(C\)), same abelian-variety typeclasses
2210	  (\(\mathtt{GrpObj}\), \(\mathtt{IsProper}\), \(\mathtt{Smooth}\), \(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\)
2211	  on \(A\)), and the same pointed-morphism conclusion shape $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi
2212	  \eta[A]$.
2213	\end{theorem}
2214	\begin{proof}
2215	  By \cref{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1} the genus-\(0\) curve \(C\) over the algebraically closed
2216	  field \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) via some isomorphism
2217	  \(\varphi \colon C \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\). The composite
2218	  \(f \circ \varphi^{-1} \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) is then a morphism from
2219	  \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) to the abelian variety \(A\), hence constant by
2220	  \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}: its image is a single \(\bar k\)-point \(a_0 \in A\).
2221	  Therefore \(f\) itself is constant with value \(a_0\). The pointed hypothesis
2222	  \(p \fatsemi f = \eta_A\) evaluates this constant at the \(\bar k\)-point \(p\) and pins it to the
2223	  identity, \(a_0 = \eta_A\). Consequently \(f\) factors through the terminal object as the
2224	  constant morphism at the identity, \(f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A\).
2225	  No differential (\(df = 0\)) input, no \(H^0(C, \Omega_C) = 0\) / Serre-duality decomposition,
2226	  and no Picard representability is used; the argument is valid in arbitrary characteristic.
2227	  Once formalised it lets the \([\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]\) hypothesis carried by
2228	  \(\mathtt{rigidity\_over\_kbar}\) be dropped, and (after the \(\bar k \to k\) descent step hosted
2229	  in \cref{chap:Jacobian}, not here) feeds \(\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}.\mathtt{key}\).
2230	  % NOTE (iter-166 review): proof body landed iter-166 via clean iso-transport
2231	  % (`obtain ⟨φ⟩ := genusZero_curve_iso_P1 _hgenus; set g := φ.inv ≫ f; obtain ⟨a₀, hga₀⟩ :=
2232	  % morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const g; pin a₀ = η[A] via `toUnit_unique` basepoint transport;
2233	  % back-transport via `φ.hom_inv_id`'). `lean_verify` axioms $\{\mathtt{propext},
2234	  % \mathtt{sorryAx}, \mathtt{Classical.choice}, \mathtt{Quot.sound}\}\( — \)\mathtt{sorryAx}$
2235	  % propagates honestly through (a) `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (RR-bridge sub-build, deferred to
2236	  % iter-167+) and (b) `morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const`'s 5 helper sorries. Lifts to axiom-clean
2237	  % once both upstreams discharge; checker confirmed no laundering.
2238	\end{proof}

2	\chapter{Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)}
3	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}
4	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II \S6, pp.~130--137 +
5	% IV.1, pp.~294--296 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
6	% Stacks pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (divisors),
7	% 02ME (order at a point on a regular 1-dimensional scheme),
8	% 0BE0 (degree of a divisor on a curve), 0BE3 (principal divisors have
9	% degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve).
10	\section{Setup and motivation}
11	This chapter is the first of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
12	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
13	\[
14	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
15	  \,\colon\,
16	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
17	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
18	\]
19	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
20	divisor of a closed point on a curve (\texttt{RR.1}, this chapter); the
21	Riemann--Roch dimension formula \(\ell(D) = \deg D + 1\) for \(\deg D \geq 0\) on
22	a genus-\(0\) curve (\texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections argument identifying
23	\(\dim_k H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) via \(1\) and a non-constant function
24	(\texttt{RR.3}); the ``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)''
25	classification recovered as a degree-\(1\) morphism into \(\mathbb P^1\) via
26	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
27	Mathlib ships no \texttt{WeilDivisor} on a scheme. Adjacent pieces exist, but
28	none of them is the data structure we need: \texttt{MeromorphicOn.divisor}
29	(\texttt{Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor}) is the order function of a
30	meromorphic function valued in a \emph{normed} field, not a formal sum of
31	codimension-\(1\) cycles on a scheme; \texttt{CommRing.Pic}
32	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) is the ring-level Picard group of
33	invertible modules under tensor product, with no scheme-level analogue;
34	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap}
35	(\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}) packages rational
36	maps but not divisors. Consequently this chapter, and the three siblings, are
37	\emph{project-bespoke}: the only Mathlib tools they rely on are
38	discrete-valuation rings, the function field, and the ambient scheme API.
39	The audit grounding this scope decision is in
40	\texttt{analogies/rrbridge-survey.md}.
41	This chapter establishes the formal-sum data type
42	\(\mathrm{Div}(X) = \bigoplus_{\substack{Z \subset X \\ \mathrm{codim} 1}} \mathbb Z\) on a
43	Noetherian integral scheme \(X\) satisfying Hartshorne's condition \((*)\), the
44	principal-divisor homomorphism \(\mathrm{div} \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \mathrm{Div}(X)\) on a
45	curve, the degree map \(\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \to \mathbb Z\) on a smooth proper
46	curve over an algebraically closed field, and the linear-equivalence
47	relation. The \emph{degree-zero of a principal divisor} statement
48	(\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}) is included for completeness; its proof
49	decomposes into a separate sub-build inside \texttt{RR.1} (it uses the
50	auxiliary lemma that any non-constant rational function on \(C\) defines a
51	finite morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\)). The line-bundle construction $\mathcal
52	O_C(D)\( and the associated cohomology computation \)H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$
53	live in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3}; the Riemann--Roch dimension
54	formula lives in \texttt{RR.2}; the genus-\(0\) classification proper lives in
55	\texttt{RR.4}.
56	Throughout this chapter, the standing convention is Hartshorne's condition
57	\((*)\):
58	% SOURCE QUOTE: "\((\ast)\) \(X\) is a noetherian integral separated scheme which
59	% is regular in codimension one."
60	\textit{Source: Hartshorne, II \S6, p.~130, the conditional \((*)\).}
61	\[
62	  (\ast)\qquad
63	  X \text{ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which is regular in
64	  codimension one.}
65	\]
66	For the degree map and for principal-divisor statements on a curve, we
67	additionally assume \(X\) is a complete (i.e.\ proper) nonsingular curve over
68	an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), where Hartshorne's condition \((*)\) is
69	automatic.
70	\paragraph{Standing hypothesis \((*)\) in the Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).}
71	Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) does not bundle Hartshorne's four-clause
72	\((\ast)\) as a single typeclass. The chapter pins \((*)\) to a \emph{layered}
73	typeclass set that the prover-agent threads through the Lean signatures in
74	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean}:
75	\begin{itemize}
76	  \item \textbf{Basic formal-sum layer} (carrier of \ref{def:prime_divisor} and
77	    \ref{def:codim1_cycles}, the additive group \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\), the degree
78	    \ref{def:divisor_degree}, the degree-homomorphism
79	    \ref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}, and \ref{def:divisor_closed_point}): the
80	    only Mathlib typeclass invoked is \texttt{[IsIntegral X]}
81	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme} via
82	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Properties}). Integrality of \(X\) already
83	    delivers \texttt{IrreducibleSpace X.carrier} (the carrier-level instance
84	    chain), so \(X\) has a unique generic point and the function field
85	    \texttt{X.functionField} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField})
86	    is a field; separatedness and noetherianness are \emph{not} required for
87	    the bare formal-sum data type.
88	  \item \textbf{Order/principal layer} (\ref{def:order_at_point},
89	    \ref{def:principal_divisor}, \ref{thm:principal_hom}): the Lean
90	    signature additionally threads
91	    \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X]}
92	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Noetherian}) and Hartshorne's
93	    regular-in-codimension-one clause. The latter has \emph{no} Mathlib
94	    typeclass shipping it directly; the chapter's pin is the project-side
95	    predicate ``for every \(x \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} = 1\) the
96	    stalk \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\) is a discrete valuation ring'', encodable as
97	    the conjunction
98	    $\forall x,\; \texttt{Order.coheight}\,x = 1 \Rightarrow
99	    \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing}\,(\mathcal O_{X,x})$. Iter-173+ may
100	    introduce a \texttt{Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne} predicate to
101	    abbreviate this; until then the order/principal lemmas thread the DVR
102	    hypothesis explicitly at each codim-1 point.
103	  \item \textbf{Curve layer} (\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}): the standing
104	    hypothesis tightens to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
105	    over \(\bar k\) with explicit integrality witness, namely
106	    \begin{quote}
107	      \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
108	       (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
109	       \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
110	       \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
111	    \end{quote}
112	    matching the standing typeclass set used by the rest of the project's
113	    curve API (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.Genus},
114	    \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity}). Hartshorne's \((*)\) is
115	    automatic for this layer (\texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian]} from finite type
116	    over a field; regular-in-codim-1 from smoothness; separatedness from
117	    properness; integrality threaded).
118	\end{itemize}
119	\noindent
120	The \texttt{[IsIntegral X]} hypothesis is the only one threaded through the
121	\emph{statement} of every Lean signature in
122	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean} that mentions a
123	function field, principal divisor, or order. The noetherian-and-DVR
124	content lives inside the proof bodies (sub-build), where the prover threads
125	the appropriate stronger hypothesis at the use site.
126	\section{Codim-\(1\) cycle group}
127	\begin{definition}
128	\leanok
129	[Prime divisor of a scheme]
130	  \label{def:prime_divisor}
131	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor}
132	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "prime divisor" under
133	  % condition \((*)\)) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
134	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW (divisors).
135	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\). A \emph{prime
136	  % divisor} on \(X\) is a closed integral subscheme \(Y\) of codimension one."
137	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of prime divisor).}
138	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). A \emph{prime divisor} on \(X\) is a closed integral
139	  subscheme \(Y \subset X\) of codimension one. Equivalently, by the standard
140	  generic-point correspondence (Hartshorne II \S6, p.~130, ``let $\eta \in
141	  Y\( be its generic point\ldots''), \)Y$ is determined by its generic point
142	  \(\eta \in X\): closed integral subschemes of codimension one of \(X\) are in
143	  natural bijection with points \(x \in X\) such that the closure
144	  \(\overline{\{x\}} \subset X\) has codimension one. The closure
145	  \(\overline{\{x\}}\), equipped with the \emph{reduced induced subscheme
146	  structure}, is automatically integral (irreducible, because the closure
147	  of a single point is irreducible, and reduced by construction); the only
148	  non-automatic condition is the codimension.
149	  \paragraph{Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).} The Lean structure
150	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor} bundles a point
151	  \texttt{point : X} together with a codimension-one witness, encoded via
152	  the Mathlib predicate
153	  \[
154	    \texttt{Order.coheight\ point\ =\ 1}
155	  \]
156	  on the specialisation preorder of the topological space underlying \(X\).
157	  Concretely, Mathlib's preorder on \texttt{X.carrier} is defined by
158	  ``\(x \leq y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x\)'' (i.e.\ \(x \in \overline{\{y\}}\);
159	  see \texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Specialization} and
160	  \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}). Under this convention the generic
161	  point of an integral scheme is the unique \emph{maximal} element of the
162	  preorder, so for the generic point \(\eta_Y\) of a prime divisor \(Y\) the
163	  only point strictly above \(\eta_Y\) is the generic point \(\eta_X\) of \(X\),
164	  and the chain \(\eta_Y < \eta_X\) has length one, giving
165	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ \(\eta_Y\)\ =\ 1}. The DVR/integrality content of
166	  ``the closure of \texttt{point} with the reduced subscheme structure is
167	  integral'' is automatic from \(\overline{\{\,\texttt{point}\,\}}\) being
168	  irreducible, so the Lean structure does not need a separate integrality
169	  field. The iter-172 placeholder \texttt{isCodim1AndIntegral : True} in
170	  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean:84--90} is to be
171	  refined to \texttt{coheight : Order.coheight point = 1} (named field, type
172	  \texttt{Prop}) in the iter-173 prover step, retaining the same
173	  \texttt{point} field for downstream defeq.
174	  \paragraph{Reference predicates considered and rejected.}
175	  Two alternatives were considered and rejected at the iter-172
176	  lean-vs-blueprint-checker stage:
177	  \begin{itemize}
178	    \item \texttt{IsIntegral\ (X.closedSubschemeOfPoint\ x)}: rejected
179	      because Mathlib (\texttt{b80f227}) ships \emph{no}
180	      \texttt{closedSubschemeOfPoint} constructor. The closest available
181	      object is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData}
182	      (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf.Basic}), which packages
183	      a quasi-coherent ideal sheaf but does not produce a reduced induced
184	      subscheme on the closure of a point. Building that constructor is
185	      itself a Mathlib-upstream-scale project (Stacks tag 01J9 ``reduced
186	      induced scheme structure''), outside the scope of \texttt{RR.1}.
187	    \item A bundled \texttt{Scheme.IdealSheafData}-based prime-divisor
188	      carrier: rejected because the chapter only needs the generic-point
189	      witness for downstream order/principal-divisor consumption; carrying
190	      an \texttt{IdealSheafData} adds redundant data the chapter never
191	      uses.
192	  \end{itemize}
193	  The \texttt{Order.coheight}-based encoding is therefore the
194	  minimum-Mathlib-dependence pin compatible with the chapter's downstream
195	  needs.
196	\end{definition}
197	\begin{definition}
198	\leanok
199	[Codim-\(1\) cycle group / Weil divisor group of a scheme]
200	  \label{def:codim1_cycles}

2	\label{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.2.c assembly sub-row of
6	% Route~A (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It wires the

16	% material (\Picz) is A.3 and lives in a separate sibling chapter --- it is
17	% \emph{not} scoped into this chapter.
18	
19	% NOTE (planner directive iter-174). This chapter must produce the
20	% \emph{representable Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\) as a \(k\)-group scheme,
21	% locally of finite type, that represents the \'etale sheafification
22	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) defined in A.1.c. The three deliverables are
23	% (i) a line-bundle / Quot translation expressed via the Abel map

25	% Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\), hence through the
26	% Quot engine of A.2.b; (ii) the existence theorem itself, in the
27	% field-of-definition specialisation \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\) a smooth proper
28	% geometrically integral curve; (iii) the group-scheme refinement carried by
29	% the tensor product of invertible sheaves on \(C \times_k T\). The Lean
30	% encoding is delegated to a Lean file
31	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean} whose

33	% chapter.
34	
35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
37	
38	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
39	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
40	Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set\) of
41	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
42	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
43	\'etale-sheafified version \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) at
44	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
45	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
46	\(C/k\) projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
47	\(\Hilb_{C/k}\) and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective
48	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective \(k\)-schemes. Grothendieck's
49	existence theorem for \(\Pic\) assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
50	map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) to produce a representing scheme,
51	the \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The present chapter packages this
52	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
53	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
54	
55	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.4);
56	% arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26. Read from
57	% references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex.
58	
59	\section{Line bundles and the Quot / Hilbert scheme}
60	\label{sec:fga_pic_line_bundle_quot}
61	
62	The bridge from line bundles on \(C \times_k T\) to the Quot scheme is the
63	Abel map: a relative effective divisor \(D \subset C \times_k T\) over \(T\)
64	(an \(S\)-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
65	subscheme of \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), hence a \(T\)-point of the Hilbert
66	functor \(\Hilb_{C/k}(T)\), which is the special case \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
67	of the Quot functor of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}; and it determines an
68	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) on the relative curve,
69	representing a class in \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
70	
71	\begin{lemma}\leanok
72	[Line bundle / Quot correspondence via the Abel map]
73	  \label{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}
74	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
75	  
76	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3, Def.~dfn:Abel (the Abel
77	  % map), and Thm.~th:repDiv (representability of relative effective
78	  % divisors by an open subscheme of \(\IHilb_{X/S}\)); arXiv:math/0504020,
79	  % pp.~25, 23 (read from
80	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1837-L1841 + L2135-L2145).
81	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
82	  % "\begin{thm}\label{th:repDiv}
83	  %   Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat.  Then \(\mathrm{Div}_{X/S}\) is
84	  %  representable by an open subscheme \(\IDiv_{X/S}\) of the Hilbert scheme
85	  %  \(\IHilb_{X/S}\).
86	  %   \end{thm}"
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
88	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{dfn:Abel}
89	  %   The {\it Abel map\/} is the natural map of functors
90	  %      \(\)A_{X/S}(T)\:\mathrm{Div}_{X/S}(T)\to \Pic_{X/S}(T)\(\)
91	  %   defined by sending a relative effective divisor \(D\) on \(X_T/T\) to the
92	  %  sheaf \(\mc O_{\!X_T}(D)\).  The target \(\Pic_{X/S}\) may be replaced by any
93	  %  of its associated sheaves."
94	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3,
95	  Thm.~th:repDiv + Def.~dfn:Abel (cf.\ [Nitsure], ``Construction of Hilbert
96	  and Quot Schemes'', \S 1, where \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\)).}
97	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve, and let

1	\chapter{The relative cotangent presheaf}
2	\label{chap:differentials}
3	
4	This chapter develops the relative cotangent \emph{presheaf} \(\Omega_{X/S}\) of a morphism of schemes \(f \colon X \to S\), starting from the ring-theoretic K\"ahler differential module \(\Omega_{B/A}\) already available in Mathlib. The output is a presheaf of \(\struct{X}\)-modules whose sections on each open \(V \subseteq X\) are the K\"ahler differential module of the ring map \((f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V) \to \struct{X}(V)\) induced by \(f\), where \(f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\) denotes the \emph{presheaf-level} inverse-image functor (left Kan extension along \((\mathrm{Opens.map}\,f.\mathrm{base}).\mathrm{op}\)).
5	
6	The downstream role of this chapter is to provide the \emph{forward direction} of the standard smoothness criterion: smoothness of \(f\) implies that on every affine chart \(V \to U\) (with \(V \subseteq X\), \(U \subseteq S\) affine and \(f V \subseteq U\)) the appLE-algebra K\"ahler differential \(\Omega_{\Gamma(X, V)\,/\,\Gamma(S, U)}\) is a free \(\Gamma(X, V)\)-module of rank \(n\). This is the Jacobian-criterion direction and is what connects to the dimension hypothesis on the Jacobian: the smoothness of the curve \(C \to \Spec k\) in the Jacobian definition is the special case \(n = 1\), and the relative dimension of \(\Jac(C)\) is read off from the rank via the same forward implication. The bridge between this affine-chart-wise algebra-K\"ahler form and the section module of the relative cotangent presheaf \(\Omega_{X/S}\) was the former content of milestone~M1; the bridge declaration was excised iter-126 (zero in-tree consumers) and replaced in this chapter by the standalone K\"ahler-localization utilities of Section~REF, the off-loop Mathlib-PR work of which proceeds from \texttt{analogies/relative-differentials-presheaf-bridge.md}. The converse implication of the forward smoothness criterion is \emph{mathematically false} as stated and is documented separately in Section~REF.
7	
8	\section{The relative cotangent presheaf}
9	
10	\begin{definition}
11	
12	\leanok
13	[Relative cotangent presheaf of a morphism]
14	  \label{def:relative_kaehler_presheaf}
15	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.relativeDifferentialsPresheaf}
16	  Let \(f \colon X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes. The presheaf-level inverse-image presheaf of rings \(f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S}\) on \(X\) (the left Kan extension of \(\struct{S}\) along \((\mathrm{Opens.map}\,f.\mathrm{base}).\mathrm{op}\), given on each open \(V \subseteq X\) by the colimit \(\mathrm{colim}_{W : \,f V \subseteq W \subseteq S}\,\struct{S}(W)\)) is paired with \(\struct{X}\) by the canonical adjunction-transpose \(f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S} \to \struct{X}\) of the comorphism \(f^{\sharp}\colon\struct{S}\to f_{*}\struct{X}\). Applying the presheaf-of-modules K\"ahler differential construction \(\mathrm{relativeDifferentials}'\) to this map of presheaves of commutative rings produces a presheaf of \(\struct{X}\)-modules on \(X\), the \emph{relative cotangent presheaf} \(\Omega_{X/S}\).
17	\end{definition}
18	
19	\begin{lemma}
20	
21	\leanok
22	[Sections of \(\Omega_{X/S}\) on an open]
23	  \label{lem:relative_kaehler_presheaf_obj}
24	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_obj_kaehler}
25	  
26	  For every open \(V \subseteq X\), the sections of the relative cotangent presheaf are, by construction, the K\"ahler differential module of the ring map \((f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V) \to \struct{X}(V)\) induced by \(f\):
27	  \[
28	    \Omega_{X/S}(V) \;=\; \Omega_{\struct{X}(V) \,/\, (f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V)}.
29	  \]
30	  Here \((f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V) = \mathrm{colim}_{W : \,f V \subseteq W \subseteq S}\,\struct{S}(W)\) is the presheaf-level inverse-image section ring, \emph{not} \(\struct{S}(U)\) for an arbitrary affine open \(U \supseteq f V\). The relationship between this colimit-source K\"ahler module and the classical ring-theoretic \(\Omega_{B/A}\) for \(A = \struct{S}(U)\), \(B = \struct{X}(V)\) on an affine chart \(V = \Spec B \subseteq f^{-1}U\) with \(U = \Spec A \subseteq S\) affine is the content of the bridge documented in Section~REF.
31	\end{lemma}
32	
33	\begin{proof}
34	  
35	  \leanok
36	  By definition of \(\mathrm{relativeDifferentials}'\): the construction is built object-wise to be the Mathlib \texttt{KaehlerDifferential} module of the ring map at each open. The identification is therefore by \texttt{rfl} after unfolding the definition. The colimit description of \((f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V)\) is the definition of \texttt{TopCat.Presheaf.pullback} as the left Kan extension along \((\mathrm{Opens.map}\,f.\mathrm{base}).\mathrm{op}\) in Mathlib.
37	\end{proof}
38	
39	\begin{remark}[Comparison with Mathlib's ring-level \(\Omega_{B/A}\)]
40	  \label{rem:kahler_compatibility}
41	  Mathlib provides the ring-theoretic K\"ahler differential module \(\Omega_{B/A}\) together with the universal derivation \(\mathrm{d} \colon B \to \Omega_{B/A}\). The construction of \(\Omega_{X/S}\) on each open is, by definition, this Mathlib module applied at the ring level of the presheaf, so all ring-level Mathlib lemmas about \(\Omega_{B/A}\) transfer verbatim under Lemma~REF — but for the \emph{colimit-source} ring \((f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V)\), not for \(\struct{S}(U)\). Transferring to the classical \(A = \struct{S}(U)\) source on an affine chart is the bridge of Section~REF.
42	\end{remark}
43	
44	\section{Smoothness criterion for \(\Omega_{X/S}\) (forward direction)}
45	
46	The forward direction of the textbook smoothness criterion identifies a necessary condition on the relative K\"ahler differentials: every point of \(X\) admits an affine chart \(V \to U\) over which the appLE-algebra K\"ahler module \(\Omega_{B/A}\) is free of the prescribed rank, with \(A = \struct{S}(U)\) and \(B = \struct{X}(V)\). This is the global version of the algebraic-side fact that an \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}~\(n\)~algebra has a free K\"ahler differential module of rank \(n\). We state and prove the theorem directly on the appLE-algebra K\"ahler module, sidestepping the presheaf bridge of Section~REF. The converse implication (a local-freeness hypothesis on \(\Omega\) alone implying smoothness) is \emph{false}; see Section~REF.
47	
48	\begin{theorem}\leanok
49	[Smooth implies \(\Omega\) locally free (algebra-K\"ahler form)]
50	  \label{thm:smooth_locally_free_omega}
51	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.smooth_locally_free_omega}
52	  
53	  Let \(f \colon X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(n \in \mathbb{N}\). If \(f\) is smooth of relative dimension \(n\), then for every point \(x \in X\) there exist an affine open \(U \subseteq S\), an affine open \(V \subseteq X\) with \(x \in V\) and \(V \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\), such that the K\"ahler differential module
54	  \[
55	    \Omega_{\Gamma(X, V) \,/\, \Gamma(S, U)}
56	  \]
57	  (over the appLE algebra structure \(\Gamma(S, U) \to \Gamma(X, V)\) induced by \(f\)) is a free \(\Gamma(X, V)\)-module of rank \(n\).
58	\end{theorem}
59	
60	\begin{proof}
61	  
62	  \leanok
63	  Fix \(x \in X\). We use the abbreviation \(A := \Gamma(S, U)\) and \(B := \Gamma(X, V)\) for the section rings on the affine chart we will produce; the algebra map \(A \to B\) is the appLE map \((f.\mathrm{appLE}\,U\,V\,e).\mathrm{hom}\) induced by \(f\).
64	
65	  \emph{Step 1: extract a standard-smooth chart at \(x\).} The auto-generated \texttt{mk\_iff} bridge
66	  \[
67	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.smoothOfRelativeDimension\_iff} \quad [\text{verified}]
68	  \]
69	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Smooth}; produced by the \texttt{@[mk\_iff]} attribute on the class \texttt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}) unfolds the hypothesis \texttt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}~\(n\)~\(f\) into: for every \(x \in X\), there exist affine opens \(U \subseteq S\) and \(V \subseteq X\) with \(x \in V\) and \(V \leq f^{-1}U\), such that the induced algebra map \((f.\mathrm{appLE}\,U\,V\,e).\mathrm{hom}\colon A \to B\) satisfies \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}~\(n\)~\(A\)~\(B\).
70	
71	  \emph{Step 2: pass to plain standard-smooth.} The lemma
72	  \[
73	    \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.isStandardSmooth} \quad [\text{verified}]
74	  \]
75	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmooth}) yields \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth}~\(A\)~\(B\) from the relative-dimension-tagged form, unlocking the cotangent-module machinery in the next two steps.
76	
77	  \emph{Step 3: free K\"ahler module on the chart.} The Mathlib instance
78	  \[
79	    \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free\_kaehlerDifferential} \quad [\text{verified}]
80	  \]
81	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmoothCotangent}) synthesises \texttt{Module.Free}~\(B\)~\(\Omega_{B/A}\) from \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth}~\(A\)~\(B\).
82	
83	  \emph{Step 4: pin the rank.} The theorem
84	  \[
85	    \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank\_kaehlerDifferential} \quad [\text{verified}]
86	  \]
87	  (same file as Step 3) gives \(\rank_B\,\Omega_{B/A} = n\) under the hypotheses \texttt{Nontrivial}~\(B\) and \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}~\(n\)~\(A\)~\(B\).
88	
89	  \emph{Step 4.5: discharge the \texttt{Nontrivial}~\(B\) side condition.} The chart witness \(x \in V\) shows that the open \(V \subseteq X\) is non-empty as a set, so its structure-sheaf section ring \(B = \Gamma(X, V) = \struct{X}(V)\) is non-zero by \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.component\_nontrivial} (the structure sheaf of a scheme has \texttt{Nontrivial} sections on a non-empty open). This supplies the \texttt{Nontrivial}~\(B\) hypothesis required by Step 4.
90	
91	  Combining Steps 1--4.5, the chart \((U, V, e)\) produced by Step 1 satisfies \(x \in V\), \(\mathrm{IsAffineOpen}\,U\), \(\mathrm{IsAffineOpen}\,V\), and under the appLE algebra structure \(A = \Gamma(S, U) \to B = \Gamma(X, V)\), the K\"ahler module \(\Omega_{B/A}\) is a free \(B\)-module of rank \(n\). As \(x \in X\) was arbitrary, the statement follows.
92	
93	  This is the forward direction of the Jacobian criterion: Stacks Tag~02G1 (and Hartshorne~II.8, Theorem~II.5.2 in the Noetherian case).
94	
95	  \emph{Mathlib name summary.} The five \texttt{[verified]} closure pieces the proof relies on are
96	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.smoothOfRelativeDimension\_iff} (Step 1),
97	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.isStandardSmooth} (Step 2),
98	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free\_kaehlerDifferential} (Step 3),
99	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank\_kaehlerDifferential} (Step 4),
100	  and \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.component\_nontrivial} (Step 4.5).

1	\chapter{Genus of a smooth proper curve}
2	\label{chap:Genus}
3	
4	Throughout this chapter, \(k\) denotes a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(k\) of relative dimension \(1\). In other words, \(C \to \Spec k\) is a morphism of schemes carrying the geometric typeclasses that make it a curve in the modern sense.
5	
6	\section{Definition}
7	
8	% NOTE: classical definition \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\); see
9	% Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Ch.~IV \S1 (genus of a curve) — TOC
10	% summarised in `references/hartshorne-ag.md`. The finiteness backing
11	% (Serre on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme) is queued in
12	% `references/stacks-0BUG.md` and chapter
13	% \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK}.
14	\begin{definition}\leanok
15	[Genus of a smooth proper curve]
16	  \label{def:genus}
17	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}
18	  
19	  The \emph{genus} of a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(k\) is the natural number
20	  \[
21	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_k H^1(C,\, \mathcal O_C),
22	  \]
23	  the \(k\)-dimension of the first sheaf-cohomology group of the structure sheaf of \(C\).
24	\end{definition}
25	
26	\begin{proof}
27	  
28	  \leanok
29	  The definition is realised by passing the structure sheaf through the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules construction and applying the \(k\)-linear sheaf cohomology \(H^1\). The result is a \(k\)-vector space; its dimension is extracted via the standard finiteness rank.
30	
31	  \begin{remark}[Lean implementation]
32	    The formal definition uses the project's \(\ModuleCat k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf:
33	    \[
34	      \mathtt{genus}\,C \;:=\; \Module.\mathtt{finrank}\,k\,\bigl(\Scheme.\HModule\,k\,(\Scheme.\toModuleKSheaf\,C)\,1\bigr).
35	    \]
36	    The carrier \(\toModuleKSheaf C\) is the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules constructed from \(\struct C\) via the structure morphism; the wrapper \(\HModule k F 1\) is the \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology, given by \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\), which carries a canonical \(\Module k\)-structure inherited from the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category.
37	  \end{remark}
38	
39	  Mathlib's \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) returns the \(k\)-dimension when the underlying module is finite-dimensional and zero otherwise. For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve, Serre's theorem on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme guarantees \(\dim_k H^1(C, \struct C) < \infty\), so \(g(C)\) is the geometric genus on the relevant case. The closure is therefore the one-liner above, confirmed to typecheck against current Mathlib.
40	\end{proof}
41	
42	Equivalent reformulations available once Riemann--Roch and Serre duality are formalised:
43	\begin{itemize}
44	  \item \(g(C) = \dim_k H^0(C,\, \Omega^1_{C/k})\) (Serre duality).
45	  \item \(g(C) = 1 - \chi(\mathcal O_C)\), where \(\chi\) is the Euler characteristic.
46	  \item For a smooth projective curve over \(\mathbb C\), \(g(C)\) equals the topological genus of \(C(\mathbb C)\).
47	\end{itemize}
48	Each of these requires Mathlib infrastructure that is currently absent. The first reformulation in particular requires the sheaf of relative differentials of schemes (Phase~B), and the others require Riemann--Roch / Serre duality / a topological-vs-algebraic geometry comparison.
49	
50	\section{Mathlib gap}
51	\label{sec:Genus_status}
52	
53	The honest definition \(\dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) requires a \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology of \(\struct C\) on the topology of opens of the underlying space of \(C\). Through earlier iterations the project has built this:
54	
55	\begin{itemize}
56	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of \(\AddCommGrpCat\)-valued sheaves} (Mathlib): defined as \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\AddCommGrpCat.\mathrm{of}\,(\ULift\,\mathbb Z)\).
57	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of \(\ModuleCat k\)-valued sheaves} (this project): the parallel \(\Module k\)-valued construction with unit object \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\) instead of \(\ULift\,\mathbb Z\), gluing through the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category to recover a \(\Module k\) structure on the cohomology groups.
58	  \item \textbf{Structure sheaf as a \(\ModuleCat k\)-valued sheaf} (this project): for \(C\) over \(\Spec k\), the sheafification of the presheaf \(U \mapsto \ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,(\Gamma(C, U))\) where the \(k\)-action is the algebra structure induced by the structure morphism.
59	  \item \textbf{\(H^0\) algebraic bridge} (this project): \(H^0(C, \struct C)_{\Module k} \simeq_k \Hom_{\Sheaf}(\mathrm{const}, \toModuleKSheaf C)\), via Mathlib's \(\Ext\) linear equivalence. Used downstream for the geometric content \(H^0(C, \struct C) \simeq k\) on a connected proper \(k\)-curve.
60	\end{itemize}
61	
62	The remaining Mathlib gap for the \emph{theorem} (as opposed to the \emph{definition}) of genus:
63	
64	\begin{itemize}
65	  \item \textbf{Serre finiteness for \(H^i(C, \mathcal F)\) on a proper \(k\)-curve.} Mathlib's \(\ModuleCat.\mathtt{finite\_ext}\) provides \(\Module.\mathtt{Finite}\) for module-Ext only, not sheaf-Ext. The Čech-cohomology scaffold is the recommended direction.
66	\end{itemize}
67	
68	\section{User authorisation of \texttt{noncomputable}}
69	\label{sec:Genus_noncomputable}
70	
71	The original challenge file writes \(\mathtt{def\ genus}\), not \(\mathtt{noncomputable\ def\ genus}\). Because \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\), \(\Abelian.\Ext.\mathtt{instModule}\), and the structure-sheaf-as-\(k\)-module construction are all noncomputable in Mathlib, the honest closure of the body requires the \(\mathtt{noncomputable}\) modifier on the \(\mathtt{def}\). The user explicitly authorised agents to add this modifier as a non-signature change: names, argument types, and argument order remain verbatim.
72	
73	\section{What this iteration does and does not}
74	
75	This iteration \emph{defines} the genus. It does not \emph{compute} it on any specific curve, and it does not prove that the result equals the geometric genus in the classical sense (which requires Serre finiteness for non-vacuity and Riemann--Roch for verification on examples). The downstream theorem \(\mathtt{smoothOfRelativeDimension\_genus}\) in \(\mathtt{Jacobian.lean}\) states that the Jacobian of \(C\) is smooth of relative dimension \(g(C)\) --- that theorem will require Serre finiteness on the way and is queued for the iteration that closes the Jacobian construction itself.
76

1	\chapter{Mayer--Vietoris long exact sequence for sheaf cohomology with \(k\)-module coefficients}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_MayerVietoris}
3	
4	This chapter constructs the Mayer--Vietoris long exact sequence for sheaf cohomology valued in \(k\)-modules, and develops the \v{C}ech-acyclicity machinery for affine basic-open covers. Together these provide the categorical engine for the Serre finiteness argument (Phase~A step~6). The carriers \(H^n(X,\mathcal F)\) and \(H^{\prime n}(U,\mathcal F)\) live in Chapter~REF and are imported here.
5	
6	The chapter introduces no protected declarations: every definition, lemma, and instance here is auxiliary, supporting the construction of \(H^1(C,\mathcal O_C)\) in Definition~REF.
7	
8	\section{Functoriality of cohomology in the open variable}
9	\label{sec:hmodule_prime_cohomologyPresheaf}
10	
11	The cohomology-of-an-open carrier \(H^{\prime n}(X,\mathcal F)\) is contravariantly functorial in the open variable \(X\). Assembling this functoriality yields a presheaf
12	\[
13	  X \longmapsto H^{\prime n}(X, \mathcal F),
14	\]
15	and, varying \(\mathcal F\) as well, a bifunctor from sheaves of \(k\)-modules to presheaves of abelian groups. The codomain is the category of abelian groups because \(\Ext\) is always valued there, regardless of the linear enrichment of the source category; the per-fiber \(k\)-module structure is inherited from the reducible wrapper on \(H^{\prime n}\).
16	
17	\begin{definition}\leanok
18	[Cohomology presheaf functor, \(H'\) flavor]
19	  \label{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_cohomologyPresheafFunctor}
20	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule'_cohomologyPresheafFunctor}
21	  
22	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(C\) a category equipped with a Grothendieck topology \(J\), and assume sheafification and \(\Ext\) exist for sheaves of \(k\)-modules on \(J\). For \(n \in \mathbb N\), define the cohomology presheaf functor
23	  \[
24	    \mathcal H^{\prime n}(k, J) \;:\; \Sheaf\,J\,(\ModuleCat\,k) \longrightarrow (C^{\op} \longrightarrow \AddCommGrpCat)
25	  \]
26	  by the standard composition of the Yoneda embedding, the free-\(k\)-module functor, sheafification, and the \(n\)-th \(\Ext\) functor.
27	\end{definition}
28	
29	\begin{proof}
30	  
31	  \leanok
32	  The body is a direct categorical composition mirroring the general sheaf-cohomology presheaf functor. Universe pinning is required because the morphism universe of the sheaf category lives one level above the object universe.
33	\end{proof}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Cohomology presheaf, \(H'\) flavor]
37	  \label{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_cohomologyPresheaf}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule'_cohomologyPresheaf}
39	  
40	  In the setting of Definition~REF, let \(\mathcal F\) be a sheaf of \(k\)-modules. For \(n \in \mathbb N\), define the cohomology presheaf
41	  \[
42	    \mathcal H^{\prime n}(\mathcal F) \;:\; C^{\op} \longrightarrow \AddCommGrpCat
43	  \]
44	  to be the evaluation of the bifunctor at \(\mathcal F\).
45	\end{definition}
46	
47	\begin{proof}
48	  
49	  \leanok
50	  Direct evaluation of the bifunctor at \(\mathcal F\).
51	\end{proof}
52	
53	\begin{remark}
54	  For every object \(X\) of \(C\), the underlying type of \(\mathcal H^{\prime n}(\mathcal F)(X)\) is definitionally equal to \(H^{\prime n}(X, \mathcal F)\). The per-fiber \(k\)-module structure is therefore inherited through the \(H'\) wrapper.
55	\end{remark}
56	
57	\begin{remark}[Lean implementation]
58	  The cohomology presheaf functor is declared as a non-computable definition and the presheaf as a non-computable abbrev, matching the reducibility conventions of the general sheaf-cohomology API. The abbrev form is load-bearing: downstream definitional unfolding needs to see through to the fiber-level \(H'\) carrier.
59	\end{remark}
60	
61	\section{Mayer--Vietoris long exact sequence}
62	\label{sec:hmodule_prime_mayer_vietoris}
63	
64	A Mayer--Vietoris square for a Grothendieck topology \(J\) on a category \(C\) is a pullback square
65	% NOTE: rendered as a 2x2 array for KaTeX (leanblueprint web); the PDF
66	% build renders the same data via tikz-cd if the package is available.
67	\[
68	  \begin{array}{ccc}
69	    X_1 & \longrightarrow & X_2 \\
70	    \downarrow & & \downarrow \\
71	    X_3 & \longrightarrow & X_4
72	  \end{array}
73	\]
74	that is a \(J\)-cover: the pair of morphisms \(X_2 \to X_4\) and \(X_3 \to X_4\) is a covering family, and \(X_1\) is the pullback. For a sheaf \(\mathcal F\), the cohomology groups of the four corners are linked by a long exact sequence whose building blocks are restriction maps, biproduct maps, and a connecting homomorphism.
75	
76	\subsection{The restriction maps}
77	
78	\begin{definition}\leanok
79	[Sum of two restriction maps]
80	  \label{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_toBiprod}
81	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule'_toBiprod}
82	  
83	  Let \(S\) be a Mayer--Vietoris square, \(\mathcal F\) a sheaf of \(k\)-modules, and \(n \in \mathbb N\). Define the morphism
84	  \[
85	    \toBiprod \;:\; H^{\prime n}(X_4, \mathcal F) \longrightarrow H^{\prime n}(X_2, \mathcal F) \oplus H^{\prime n}(X_3, \mathcal F)
86	  \]
87	  to be the biproduct lift of the two restriction maps induced by \(X_2 \to X_4\) and \(X_3 \to X_4\).
88	\end{definition}
89	
90	\begin{proof}
91	  
92	  \leanok
93	  The biproduct exists because the category of abelian groups has finite biproducts. The morphism is built from the contravariant functoriality of the cohomology presheaf.
94	\end{proof}
95	
96	\begin{definition}\leanok
97	[Difference of two restriction maps]
98	  \label{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_fromBiprod}
99	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule'_fromBiprod}
100
